Definition: to leave somebody, especially somebody you are responsible for, with no intention of returning | to leave a thing or place, especially because it is impossible or dangerous to stay | to stop doing something, especially before it is finished; to stop having something | to stop supporting or helping somebody; to stop believing in something | to feel an emotion so strongly that you can feel nothing else
Example: The baby had been abandoned by its mother. | Snow forced many drivers to abandon their vehicles. | They abandoned the match because of rain. | The country abandoned its political leaders after the war. | He abandoned himself to despair.

Definition: to officially end a law, a system or an institution
Example: This tax should be abolished.

Definition: the deliberate ending of a pregnancy at an early stage | a medical operation to end a pregnancy at an early stage | the process of giving birth to a baby before it is fully developed and able to survive
Example: to support/oppose abortion | She decided to have an abortion . |

Definition: the fact of somebody being away from a place where they are usually expected to be; the occasion or period of time when somebody is away | the fact of somebody/something not existing or not being available; a lack of something
Example: The decision was made in my absence (= while I was not there) . | I was surprised by the absence of any women on the board of directors.
Idioms: absence makes the heart grow fonder : used to say that when you are away from somebody that you love, you love them even more | conspicuous by your absence : not present in a situation or place, when it is obvious that you should be there ; The meeting went well but Nigel was conspicuous by his absence.
Synonym: not being present | lack | no-show | not being present

Definition: not in a place because of illness, etc. | not present in something | showing that you are not really looking at or thinking about what is happening around you | to not go to or be in a place where you are expected to be | without
Example: She was absent from work for two weeks. | Love was totally absent from his childhood. | an absent expression | He had absented himself from the office for the day. | Absent further evidence, the police had no choice but to release him without charge.

Definition: total and complete | used, especially in spoken English, to give emphasis to what you are saying | definite and without any doubt | (of a legal decision) final | not limited in any way | existing or measured independently and not in relation to something else
Example: I've joined a class for absolute beginners. | We must keep costs to an absolute minimum . | There was no absolute proof. | The divorce became absolute last week. | absolute power/authority | Although prices are falling in absolute terms , energy is still expensive.

Definition: to take in a liquid, gas or other substance from the surface or space around | to take in and keep heat, light, energy, sound, etc. instead of reflecting it | to reduce the effect of a physical impact or movement | to take something into the mind and learn or understand it | to interest somebody very much so that they pay no attention to anything else | to make something smaller become part of something larger | to use up a large supply of something, especially money or time | to deal with changes, effects, costs, etc.
Example: Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air. | Black walls absorb a lot of heat during the day. | This tennis racket absorbs shock on impact. | It's a lot of information to absorb all at once. | This work had absorbed him for several years. | The town absorbs an influx of tourists every summer. | The new proposals would absorb $80 billion of the federal budget. | The company is unable to absorb such huge losses.
Synonym: take in completely | take in completely | soak up | soak up | soak up | take in | engross | take in completely

Definition: based on general ideas and not on any particular real person, thing or situation | existing in thought or as an idea but not having a physical reality | not representing people or things in a realistic way, but expressing the artist’s ideas about them | an abstract work of art | a short piece of writing containing the main ideas in a document | to remove something from somewhere | to make a written summary of a book, etc.
Example: abstract knowledge/principles | We may talk of beautiful things but beauty itself is abstract. | the work of American abstract expressionists like Mark Rothko | Kandinsky’s first pure abstracts are marked by their wild colour. | Abstracts of about 300 words should be submitted to the conference committee who will decide which papers to accept. | She abstracted the main points from the argument. |
Idioms: in the abstract : in a general way, without referring to a particular real person, thing or situation ; Legal questions rarely exist in the abstract; they are based on real cases. ; I’m just talking in the abstract now.

Definition: extremely silly; not logical and sensible | things that are or that seem to be absurd
Example: That uniform makes the guards look absurd. | He has a good sense of the absurd.

Definition: a large quantity that is more than enough
Example: The brochure promised beautiful walks with an abundance of wildlife.
Idioms: in abundance : in large quantities ; Fruit and vegetables grew in abundance on the island. ; Exotic plants grew there in great abundance.

Definition: the use of something in a way that is wrong or harmful | unfair, cruel or violent treatment of somebody | rude and offensive remarks, usually made when somebody is very angry | to make bad use of something, or to use so much of something that it harms your health | to use power or knowledge unfairly or wrongly | to treat a person or an animal in a cruel or violent way, especially sexually | to make rude or offensive remarks to or about somebody
Example: alcohol/drug abuse | child abuse | to scream/hurl/shout abuse | to abuse alcohol/drugs | She abused her position as principal by giving jobs to her friends. | The boy had been sexually abused . | The referee had been threatened and verbally abused .

Definition: a school or college for special training | a type of official organization that aims to encourage and develop art, literature, science, etc. | a secondary school in Scotland | a private school in the US | a school in England that is independent of local authority control
Example: She trained at the Royal Academy of Music. | the Royal Academy of Arts | He went to Ayr Academy. |  |

Definition: to happen faster or earlier; to make something happen faster or earlier | to start to go faster
Example: Inflation continues to accelerate. | The runners accelerated smoothly around the bend.

Definition: a way of pronouncing the words of a language that shows which country, area or social class a person comes from; how well somebody pronounces a particular language | the emphasis that you should give to part of a word when saying it | a mark on a letter to show that it should be pronounced in a particular way | a special importance that is given to something | to emphasize a part of something
Example: a northern/Dublin/Scottish accent | In ‘today’ the accent is on the second syllable. | Canapé has an accent on the ‘e’. | In all our products the accent is on quality. | The women accent their eyes with make-up.

Definition: agreed or approved of by most people in a society | that somebody agrees is of a good enough standard or allowed | not very good but good enough
Example: Children must learn socially acceptable behaviour. | For this course a pass in English at grade B is acceptable. | The food was acceptable, but no more.

Definition: the act of accepting a gift, an invitation, an offer, etc. | the act of agreeing with something and approving of it | the process of allowing somebody to join something or be a member of a group | the quality of being willing to accept an unpleasant or difficult situation
Example: Please confirm your acceptance of this offer in writing. | The new laws have gained widespread acceptance . | Your acceptance into the insurance plan is guaranteed. | acceptance of death/suffering

Definition: that can be reached, entered, used, seen, etc. | that can be reached, entered, used, etc. by somebody who has problems walking | easy to understand | easy to talk to and to get to know
Example: The remote desert area is accessible only by helicopter. | accessible toilets for wheelchair users | Her poetry is always very accessible. |

Definition: by chance; in a way that was not planned
Example: As I turned around, I accidentally hit him in the face.

Definition: to provide somebody with a room or place to sleep, live or sit | to provide enough space for somebody/something | to consider something such as somebody’s opinion or a fact and be influenced by it when you are deciding what to do or explaining something | to help somebody by doing what they want | to change your behaviour so that you can deal with a new situation better
Example: The hotel can accommodate up to 500 guests. | The garage can accommodate three cars. | Our proposal tries to accommodate the special needs of minority groups. | I have accommodated the press a great deal, giving numerous interviews. | I needed to accommodate to the new schedule.
Synonym: do a kindness for | oblige | give accommodation to | do a kindness for | give accommodation to

Definition: to travel or go somewhere with somebody/something | to happen or appear with something else | to play a musical instrument, especially a piano, while somebody else sings or plays the main tune
Example: His wife accompanied him on the trip. | Shouts of protest accompanied this announcement. | The singer was accompanied on the piano by her sister.

Definition: to succeed in doing or completing something
Example: The first part of the plan has been safely accomplished.

Definition: an impressive thing that is done or achieved after a lot of work | a skill or special ability | the successful completing of something
Example: It was one of the President's greatest accomplishments. | Drawing and singing were among her many accomplishments. | Money will be crucial to the accomplishment of our objectives.

Idioms: in accordance with something : according to a rule or the way that somebody says that something should be done ; in accordance with legal requirements ; We acted in accordance with my parents’ wishes.

Definition: in a way that is appropriate to what has been done or said in a particular situation | for that reason
Example: We have to discover his plans and act accordingly. | The cost of materials rose sharply last year. Accordingly, we were forced to increase our prices.
Synonym: in accordance with the fact | therefore

Definition: the fact of being responsible for your decisions or actions and expected to explain them when you are asked
Example: proposals for greater police accountability

Definition: responsible for your decisions or actions and expected to explain them when you are asked
Example: The state spends taxpayers’ money and should be held accountable .

Definition: a person whose job is to keep or check financial accounts
Example: We talked to the company’s chief accountant.

Definition: to gradually get more and more of something over a period of time | to gradually increase in number or quantity over a period of time
Example: I seem to have accumulated a lot of books. | Debts began to accumulate.

Definition: the process of gradually increasing or getting more and more of something over a period of time | an amount of something that has gradually increased over a period of time
Example: the accumulation of wealth | an accumulation of toxic chemicals

Definition: the state of being exact or correct; the ability to do something with skill and without making mistakes | the degree to which a measurement, calculation , etc. is exact or correct
Example: They questioned the accuracy of the information in the file. | the accuracy of radiocarbon dating

Definition: correct and true in every detail | able to give completely correct information or to do something in an exact way | an accurate throw, shot, weapon, etc. hits or reaches the thing that it was aimed at
Example: an accurate description/picture of something | a highly accurate electronic compass | There were only two accurate shots on goal.

Definition: in a way that is correct and true in every detail | in an exact way | hitting or reaching the thing that was aimed at
Example: The article accurately reflects public opinion. | Quantities must be measured accurately. | You need to hit the ball accurately.

Definition: a statement saying that you think a person is guilty of doing something wrong or illegal; the fact of accusing somebody
Example: I don't want to make an accusation until I have some proof.

Definition: to say that somebody has done something wrong or is guilty of something
Example: to accuse somebody of murder/a crime

Definition: the person who is on trial in a law court : | a person or people who may be guilty of a crime and who are being judged in a court of law
Example: The accused protested her innocence . |

Definition: a chemical, usually a liquid, that contains hydrogen and has a pH of less than seven. The hydrogen can be replaced by a metal to form a salt. Acids usually have a bitter sharp taste and can often burn holes in or damage things they touch. | a powerful illegal drug that affects people’s minds and makes them see and hear things that are not really there | that contains acid or has the essential characteristics of an acid ; that has a pH of less than seven | that has a bitter sharp taste | critical and unkind
Example: The acid burned a hole in her coat. | They smoke joints and drop acid. | Rye is tolerant of poor, acid soils. | acid fruit | an acid wit

Definition: to accept that something is true | to accept that somebody/something has a particular authority or status | to tell somebody that you have received something that they sent to you | to show that you have noticed somebody/something by smiling, waving, etc. | to publicly express thanks for help you have been given
Example: She refuses to acknowledge the need for reform. | The country acknowledged his claim to the throne. | All applications will be acknowledged. | I was standing right next to her, but she didn't even acknowledge me. | I gratefully acknowledge financial support from several local businesses.

Definition: to gain something by your own efforts, ability or behaviour | to obtain something by buying or being given it
Example: She has acquired a good knowledge of English. | Not all of the land acquired for the road has been paid for yet.
Idioms: an acquired taste : a thing that you do not like much at first but gradually learn to like ; Abstract art is an acquired taste.

Definition: the act of getting something, especially knowledge, a skill, etc. | something that somebody buys to add to what they already own, usually something valuable | a company, piece of land, etc. bought by somebody, especially another company; the act of buying it
Example: theories of child language acquisition | His latest acquisition is a racehorse. | They have made acquisitions in several EU countries.

Definition: a unit for measuring an area of land; 4 840 square yards or about 4 050 square metres
Example: 3 000 acres of parkland

Definition: to make something such as a device or chemical process start working
Example: The burglar alarm is activated by movement.

Definition: the act of making something such as a device or chemical process start working
Example: The activation of several target genes results in two major effects.

Definition: a person who works to achieve political or social change, especially as a member of an organization with particular aims
Example: human/civil/animal rights activists

Definition: used to emphasize something that is real or exists in fact | used to emphasize the most important part of something
Example: James looks younger than his wife but in actual fact (= really) he is five years older. | The rehearsal was fabulous, the actual performance even better.

Definition: very serious or severe | an acute illness is one that has quickly become severe and dangerous | describing or designed for patients suffering from an acute illness | very sensitive and well developed | intelligent and quick to notice and understand things | less than 90°
Example: There is an acute shortage of water. | acute appendicitis | acute patients | Dogs have an acute sense of smell. | He is an acute observer of the social scene. |
Synonym: severe | severe | severe | severe | severe | severe

Tags: Oxford_3000 OPAL_written AWL Success Literature_and_writing Film_and_theatre TV,_radio_and_news
Definition: to change your behaviour in order to deal more successfully with a new situation | to change something in order to make it suitable for a new use or situation | to change a book or play so that it can be made into a play, film, television drama, etc.
Example: It's amazing how soon you adapt. | These styles can be adapted to suit individual tastes. | Three of her novels have been adapted for television.

Definition: the action or process of changing something, or of being changed, to suit a new purpose or situation | a film, television drama or play that is based on a particular book or play but has been changed to suit the new medium | the process of change by which a species becomes better suited to its environment
Example: the adaptation of buildings for military purposes | a screen adaptation of Shakespeare’s ‘Macbeth’ | the adaptation of desert species to the hot conditions

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English informal North_American_English Social_issues Health_problems
Definition: the condition of being unable to stop using or doing something as a habit, especially something harmful
Example: cocaine addiction

Definition: more than was first mentioned or is usual
Example: additional resources/funds/security/funding/costs

Definition: in a way that is more than was first mentioned or is usual
Example: Additionally, the bus service will run on Sundays, every two hours.

Definition: enough in quantity, or good enough in quality, for a particular purpose or need
Example: They'll need an adequate supply of hot water.

Definition: in a way that is enough in quantity, or good enough in quality, for a particular purpose or need
Example: Are you adequately insured?

Definition: to stick to something
Example: Once in the bloodstream, the bacteria adhere to the surface of the red cells.

Definition: next to something
Example: The planes landed on adjacent runways.

Definition: to change something slightly to make it more suitable for a new set of conditions or to make it work better | to get used to a new situation by changing the way you behave and/or think | to move something slightly so that it looks neater or feels more comfortable
Example: Watch out for sharp bends and adjust your speed accordingly. | They'll be fine—they just need time to adjust. | He smoothed his hair and adjusted his tie.
Idioms: kindly adjust : used to acknowledge and apologize for something that causes problems or difficulties and ask people to accept and adapt to the situation ; The store will be closed this afternoon due to staffing shortages. Kindly adjust.

Definition: a small change made to something in order to correct or improve it | a change in the way a person behaves or thinks
Example: I've made a few adjustments to the design. | She went through a period of emotional adjustment after her marriage broke up.

Definition: to manage and organize the affairs of a company, an organization, a country, etc. | to make sure that something is done fairly and in the correct way | to give or to provide something, especially in a formal way | to give drugs, medicine, etc. to somebody | to kick or to hit somebody/something
Example: to administer a charity/fund/school | to administer justice/the law | The teacher has the authority to administer punishment. | Police believe his wife could not have administered the poison. | He administered a severe blow to his opponent's head.

Tags: Oxford_3000 OPAL_written AWL informal formal British_English Australian_English law Business Healthcare
Definition: the activities that are done in order to plan, organize and run a business, school or other institution | the government of a country, especially the US | the management of public affairs | the process or act of organizing the way that something is done | the people who plan, organize and run a business, an institution, etc. | the act of giving a drug to somebody | a situation in which the financial affairs of a business that cannot pay its debts are managed by an independent administrator
Example: Administration costs are passed on to the customer. | under the Trump administration | The inhabitants of the island voted to remain under French administration. | the administration of justice | university administrations | the administration of antibiotics | If it cannot find extra funds, the company will go into administration .

Definition: connected with organizing the work of a business or an institution
Example: an administrative job/assistant/error

Tags: Oxford_5000 AWL British_English Australian_English law Jobs Business
Definition: a person whose job is to manage and organize the public or business affairs of a company or an institution, or a person who works in an office dealing with records, accounts, etc. | a person who manages a computer system and controls who can access it | a person legally appointed (= chosen) to manage the financial affairs of a business that cannot pay its debts
Example: Such organizational decisions are made by the hospital administrators. | If you are unable to access the site, contact your system administrator . |

Definition: the act of accepting somebody into an institution, organization, etc.; the right to enter a place or to join an institution or organization | a statement in which somebody admits that something is true, especially something wrong or bad that they have done | the amount of money that you pay to go into a building or to an event
Example: Hospital admission is not necessary in most cases. | He is a thief by his own admission (= he has admitted it) . | admission charges/prices

Definition: a young person who is developing from a child into an adult | in or relating to the process of developing from a child into an adult
Example: adolescents between the ages of 13 and 18 and the problems they face | adolescent boys/girls/experiences

Tags: Oxford_3000 OPAL_written especially_US_English especially_North_American_English especially_British_English British_English North_American_English formal politics Life_stages Law_and_justice Family_and_relationships
Definition: to take somebody else’s child into your family and become its legal parent(s) | to start to use a particular method or to show a particular attitude towards somebody/something | to formally accept a suggestion or policy by voting | to choose a new name, a country, a custom, etc. and begin to use it as your own | to use a particular manner, way of speaking, expression, etc. | to choose somebody as a candidate in an election; to choose somebody as your representative
Example: a campaign to encourage childless couples to adopt | All three teams adopted different approaches to the problem. | The government adopted a resolution on disarmament. | to adopt a name/title/language | He adopted an air of indifference. | She was adopted as parliamentary candidate for Wood Green.

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_US_English especially_North_American_English especially_British_English British_English North_American_English politics Life_stages Law_and_justice
Definition: the act of adopting a child; the fact of being adopted | the decision to start using something such as an idea, a plan or a name | the act of choosing somebody as a candidate for an election
Example: She put the baby up for adoption. | The widespread adoption of new information technology could save $162 billion a year. | his adoption as the Labour candidate

Tags: Oxford_3000 business formal Success War_and_conflict Change,_cause_and_effect
Definition: progress or a development in a particular activity or area of understanding | the forward movement of a group of people, especially armed forces | money paid for work before it has been done or money paid earlier than expected | attempts to start a sexual relationship with somebody | an increase in the price or value of something
Example: We live in an age of rapid technological advance . | We feared that an advance on the capital would soon follow. | They offered an advance of £5 000 after the signing of the contract. | He had made advances to one of his students. | Share prices showed significant advances.
Idioms: in advance (of something) : before the time that is expected; before something happens ; a week/month/year in advance ; The rent is due one month in advance. ; Thanks in advance for your help. ; We try to plan well in advance. ; It would have helped to know in advance that you were coming. ; The fee is payable in advance . ; People were evacuated from the coastal regions in advance of the hurricane. | in advance (of something) : more developed than somebody/something else ; Galileo's ideas were well in advance of the age in which he lived.

Definition: negative and unpleasant; not likely to produce a good result
Example: adverse change/circumstances/weather conditions

Tags: Oxford_5000 AWL formal law North_American_English British_English Law_and_justice
Definition: a person who supports or speaks in favour of somebody or of a public plan or action | a person who defends somebody in court | (in Scotland and South Africa) a lawyer who has the right to argue cases in higher courts | to support something publicly
Example: an advocate for hospital workers | Solicitors often appear as advocates in the lower courts. |  | The group does not advocate the use of violence.

Definition: connected with beauty and art and the understanding of beautiful things | made in an artistic way and beautiful to look at | the qualities and ideas in a work of art or literature that relate to beauty and the nature of art | the branch of philosophy that studies the principles of beauty, especially in art
Example: the aesthetic appeal of the songs | Their furniture was more aesthetic than functional. | The students debated the aesthetic of the poems. |

Definition: events that are of public interest or political importance | an event that people are talking about or describing in a particular way | a sexual relationship between two people, usually when one or both of them are already in a relationship with somebody else | matters connected with a person’s private business and financial situation | a thing that somebody is responsible for (and that other people should not be interested in) | an object that is unusual or difficult to describe
Example: world/international affairs | The newspapers exaggerated the whole affair wildly. | She was having an affair with a co-worker. | She manages the family's financial affairs . | How I spend my money is my affair. | Her hat was an amazing affair with feathers and a huge brim.
Idioms: a state of affairs : a situation ; This state of affairs can no longer be ignored.

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_North_American_English informal formal_or_literary Feelings Personal_qualities
Definition: the feeling of liking or loving somebody/something very much and caring about them | a person’s feelings of love
Example: Children need lots of love and affection. | Anne had two men trying to win her affections.

Definition: cheap enough that people can afford to pay it or buy it
Example: We offer quality products at affordable prices .

Definition: the situation that exists as a result of an important (and usually unpleasant) event, especially a war, an accident, etc.
Example: A lot of rebuilding took place in the aftermath of the war.

Definition: at a later time; after an event that has already been mentioned
Example: Afterwards she was sorry for what she'd said.

Definition: a business or an organization that provides a particular service especially when representing other businesses or organizations | a government department that provides a particular service
Example: She works for an advertising agency . | law enforcement agencies
Idioms: through the agency of : as a result of the action of somebody/something ; He obtained his position through the agency of an old friend.

Definition: a list of items to be discussed at a meeting | a plan of things to be done, or problems to be addressed | the intention behind what somebody says or does, that is often secret
Example: We have a very full agenda of issues to discuss. | In our company, quality is high on the agenda . | The artist is letting his own agenda affect what was meant to be a community project.

Definition: a total number or amount made up of smaller amounts that are collected together | sand or broken stone that is used to make concrete or for building roads, etc. | made up of several amounts that are added together to form a total number | to put together different items, amounts, etc. into a single group or total
Example: a record aggregate of 285 points |  | aggregate demand/investment/turnover | The scores were aggregated with the first round totals to decide the winner.
Idioms: in (the) aggregate : added together as a total or single amount ; Businesses are, in the aggregate, deeper in debt than ever before. | on aggregate : when the scores of a number of games are added together ; They won 4–2 on aggregate.

Definition: feelings of anger and hate that may result in threatening or violent behaviour | a violent attack or threats by one person against another person or by one country against another country
Example: Video games have been blamed for causing aggression. | unprovoked military aggression

Tags: Oxford_3000 sometimes_disapproving Feelings Success Health_problems Healthcare
Definition: angry, and behaving in a threatening way; ready to attack | determined and acting with force in order to succeed | developing quickly and difficult to treat | using all possible treatment options in order to extend somebody’s life
Example: Seals have been known to exhibit aggressive behaviour towards swimmers. | a very aggressive advertising campaign | Doctors diagnosed her with a rare and aggressive cancer, which was feared to be incurable. | Nicholas was diagnosed with the rare disease in November and has undergone aggressive treatments since then.

Definition: connected with the science or practice of farming
Example: agricultural policy/land/production/development

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_North_American_English British_English North_American_English informal Farming
Definition: the science or practice of farming
Example: The number of people employed in agriculture has fallen in the last decade.

Tags: Oxford_3000 AWL US_English British_English North_American_English formal informal The_environment Money Social_issues
Definition: money, food, etc. that is sent to help countries in difficult situations | help that you need, especially to perform a particular task | an object, a machine, etc. that you use to help you do something | to help somebody/something to do something, especially by making it easier | to help or encourage something to happen
Example: humanitarian/food/medical aid | She walks with the aid of a stick. | Photos make useful teaching aids. | The test is designed to aid in the diagnosis of various diseases. | New drugs are now available to aid recovery.
Idioms: in aid of something/somebody : in order to help something/somebody ; collecting money in aid of charity | what’s… in aid of? : used to ask why something is happening ; What's all this crying in aid of? | aid and abet : to help somebody to do something illegal or wrong ; She stands accused of aiding and abetting the crime. ; He was charged with aiding and abetting the robbers.

Definition: a person who helps another person, especially a politician, in their job
Example: White House aides

Definition: a serious illness caused by a virus called HIV that attacks the body’s ability to resist infection (the abbreviation for ‘ Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome ’ )
Example: the AIDS epidemic

Definition: any vehicle that can fly and carry goods or passengers
Example: fighter/transport/military aircraft

Definition: although
Example: He finally agreed, albeit reluctantly, to help us.

Definition: to warn somebody about a dangerous situation or one that requires immediate action | to make somebody aware of something | a situation in which people are watching for danger and ready to deal with it | a warning of danger or of a problem | a signal on an electronic device to inform the user of something or remind them to do something | able to think quickly; quick to notice things | aware of something, especially a problem or danger
Example: Neighbours quickly alerted the emergency services. | They had been alerted to the possibility of further price rises. | Police are warning the public to be on the alert for suspicious packages. | a bomb/fire alert | You can set a vibrating alert to remind you. | Suddenly he found himself awake and fully alert. | We must be alert to the possibility of danger.

Tags: Oxford_5000 North_American_English_also law_or_specialist Social_issues Crime_and_punishment Space
Definition: a person who is not a citizen of the country in which they live or work | a creature from another world | strange and frightening; different from what you are used to | from another country or society; foreign | not usual or acceptable | connected with creatures from another world
Example: an illegal alien | aliens from outer space | an alien environment | an alien culture | The idea is alien to our religion. | alien beings from outer space

Definition: to arrange something in the correct position, or to be in the correct position, in relation to something else, especially in a straight line | to change something slightly so that it is in the correct relationship to something else
Example: Make sure the shelf is aligned with the top of the cupboard. | Domestic prices have been aligned with those in world markets.

Definition: arrangement in a straight line | a situation in which something is in the correct relationship with something else | political, business or other support given to one country or group by another
Example: the alignment of the sun, moon and earth at a particular time | Alignment between our commercial and creative goals is crucial. | our alignment with a leading data management company

Definition: in a very similar way | used after you have referred to two people or groups, to mean ‘both’ or ‘equally’ | very similar
Example: They tried to treat all their children alike. | Good management benefits employers and employees alike. | My sister and I do not look alike.
Idioms: great minds think alike : used to say that you and another person must both be very clever because you have had the same idea or agree about something | share and share alike : used to say that everyone should share things equally and in a fair way

Definition: a public statement that is made without giving proof, accusing somebody of doing something that is wrong or illegal
Example: to investigate/deny/withdraw an allegation

Definition: to state something as a fact but without giving proof
Example: The prosecution alleges (that) she was driving carelessly.

Definition: expressed as though something is a fact but without giving any proof
Example: crimes allegedly committed during the war

Definition: an agreement between countries, political parties, etc. to work together in order to achieve something that they all want | a group of people, political parties, etc. who work together in order to achieve something that they all want
Example: to form/make an alliance | The Green Alliance was formed to campaign against environmental damage.

Definition: to give something officially to somebody/something for a particular purpose
Example: A large sum has been allocated for buying new books for the library.

Definition: an amount of money, space, etc. that is given to somebody for a particular purpose | the act of giving something to somebody for a particular purpose
Example: We have spent our entire allocation for the year. | the allocation of food to those who need it most

Definition: an amount of money that is given to somebody regularly or for a particular purpose | the amount of something that is allowed in a particular situation | a small amount of money that parents give their children, usually every week or every month | an amount of money that can be earned or received before you start paying tax
Example: an allowance of $20 a day | a baggage allowance of 20 kilos |  | personal tax allowances

Definition: a large round container, usually made of wood or metal, with flat ends and, usually, curved sides | the contents of or the amount contained in a barrel ; a unit of measurement in the oil industry equal to between 120 and 159 litres | the part of a gun like a tube through which the bullets are fired | to move very fast in a particular direction, especially in a way that you cannot control

Definition: to be able to accept and deal with something unpleasant | to take responsibility for something | to support the weight of somebody/something | to show something; to carry something so that it can be seen | to not be suitable for something | to have a feeling, especially a negative feeling | to have a particular name | to carry somebody/something, especially while moving | to move, behave or act in a particular way | to give birth to a child | to produce flowers or fruit | to go or turn in the direction mentioned
Example: The pain was almost more than he could bear. | She bore the responsibility for most of the changes. | The ice is too thin to bear your weight. | The document bore her signature. | Her later work does not bear comparison with her earlier novels (= because it is not nearly as good) . | He bears no resentment towards them. | a family that bore an ancient and honoured name | three kings bearing gifts | He bears himself (= stands, walks, etc.) proudly, like a soldier. | She was not able to bear children. | These trees take a long time to bear fruit. | When you get to the fork in the road, bear right.
Idioms: bear arms : to be a soldier; to fight | bear fruit : to have a successful result | bear hard, heavily, severely, etc. on somebody : to be a cause of difficulty or worry to somebody ; Taxation bears heavily on us all. ; The burden of the tax bore most heavily on the poor. | bear/keep somebody/something in mind | bear/keep in mind that… : to remember somebody/something; to remember or consider that… ; You don't have to do anything about it now… just bear it in mind. | bear, take, etc. the brunt of something : to receive the main force of something unpleasant ; Schools will bear the brunt of cuts in government spending. | bear/give witness (to something) : to provide evidence of the truth of something ; The crowd of mourners at his funeral bore witness to the great affection in which he was held. ; The ancient temples bear silent witness to the passing dynasties. | be borne in on somebody : to be realized by somebody, especially after a period of time ; It was gradually borne in on us that defeat was inevitable. | bring something to bear (on somebody/something) : to use energy, pressure, influence, etc. to try to achieve something or make somebody do something ; We must bring all our energies to bear upon the task. ; Pressure was brought to bear on us to finish the work on time. | grin and bear it : to accept pain or bad luck without complaining ; There's nothing we can do about it. We'll just have to grin and bear it. | have a (heavy) cross to bear : to have a difficult problem that makes you worried or unhappy but that you have to deal with ; We all have our crosses to bear.

Definition: an animal, especially one that is large or dangerous, or one that is unusual | a person who is cruel and whose behaviour shows a lack of control | an unpleasant person or thing | a thing of a particular kind

Definition: to ask somebody for something especially in an anxious way because you want or need it very much | to ask somebody for money, food, etc., especially in the street | if a dog begs , it sits on its back legs with its front legs in the air, waiting to be given something
Example: Now you have to beg and plead . | A young woman was begging in the street. |
Idioms: beg leave to do something : to ask somebody for permission to do something ; I beg leave to add a few comments of my own. | be going begging : if something is going begging , it is available because nobody else wants it ; I’ll have that last cake if it’s going begging. | beg somebody’s pardon : to ask somebody to forgive you for something you have said or done ; He returned to beg her pardon for his sudden outburst. | beg the question : to make somebody want to ask a question that has not yet been answered ; All of which begs the question as to who will fund the project. | beg the question : to talk about something as if it were definitely true, even though it might not be ; These assumptions beg the question that children learn languages more easily than adults. | I beg your pardon : used to tell somebody that you are sorry for something you have said or done ; I beg your pardon, I thought that was my coat. | I beg your pardon : used to ask somebody to repeat what they have just said because you did not hear ; ‘It's on Duke Street.’ ‘I beg your pardon.’ ‘Duke Street.’ | I beg your pardon : used to tell somebody that you are offended by what they have just said or by the way that they have said it ; ‘Just go away.’ ‘I beg your pardon!’ | I beg to differ : used to say politely that you do not agree with something that has just been said ; ‘At least she is good at her job.’ ‘Oh, I beg to differ.’

in behalf of somebody | in somebody’s behalf : in order to help somebody ; He began a petition in behalf of the farmers. | on behalf of somebody | on somebody’s behalf : as the representative of somebody or instead of them ; On behalf of the department I would like to thank you all. ; Her son collected the Nobel Peace Prize on her behalf. | on behalf of somebody | on somebody’s behalf : in order to help somebody ; They campaigned on behalf of asylum seekers. | on behalf of somebody | on somebody’s behalf : because of somebody; for somebody ; Don't worry on my behalf.

Definition: improving a situation; having a helpful or useful effect

Definition: a person who gains as a result of something | a person who receives money or property when somebody dies

Definition: not straight | not able to stand up straight, usually as a result of being old or ill | dishonest

It was just a mistake!

Definition: next to or at the side of somebody/something | compared with somebody/something
Example: He sat beside her all night. | My painting looks childish beside yours.
Idioms: be beside the point : to not be important or closely related to the main thing you are talking about ; Yes, I know it was an accident, but that's beside the point. ; They took my words out of context, but that’s beside the point. | beside yourself (with something) : unable to control yourself because of the strength of emotion you are feeling ; He was beside himself with rage when I told him what I had done. ; They were beside themselves with excitement.

Definition: in addition to somebody/something; apart from somebody/something | used for making an extra comment that adds to what you have just said | in addition; also
Example: We have lots of things in common besides music. | I don't really want to go. Besides, it's too late now. | discounts on televisions, stereos and much more besides

Definition: to risk money on a race or an event by trying to predict the result | used to say that you are almost certain that something is true or that something will happen
Example: You have to be over 16 to bet. | I bet (that) we’re too late.
Idioms: bet the farm/ranch on something : to risk everything you have on an investment, a bet, etc. ; The company bet the farm on the new marketing model, only to find that it wasn’t successful. | I/I’ll bet! : used to show that you can understand what somebody is feeling, describing, etc. ; ‘I nearly died when he told me.’ ‘I bet!’ | I/I’ll bet! : used to tell somebody that you do not believe what they have just said ; ‘I'm going to tell her what I think of her.’ ‘Yeah, I bet!’ | I wouldn’t bet on it | don’t bet on it : used to say that you do not think that something is very likely ; ‘She'll soon get used to the idea.’ ‘I wouldn't bet on it.’ | you bet! : used instead of ‘yes’ to emphasize that somebody has guessed something correctly or made a good suggestion ; ‘Are you nervous?’ ‘You bet!’ | you can bet your life/your bottom dollar (on something/(that)…) : used to say that you are certain that something will happen ; You can bet your bottom dollar that he'll be late.

Definition: to give information about somebody/something to an enemy | to hurt somebody who trusts you, especially by lying to or about them or telling their secrets to other people | to ignore your principles or beliefs in order to achieve something or gain an advantage for yourself | to tell somebody or make them aware of a piece of information, a feeling, etc., usually without meaning to
Example: He was offered money to betray his colleagues. | She felt betrayed when she found out the truth about him. | He has been accused of betraying his former socialist ideals. | His voice betrayed the worry he was trying to hide.

Definition: on or to the further side of something | more than something | later than a particular time | used to say that something is not possible | too far or too advanced for somebody/something

Idioms: be beyond somebody : to be impossible for somebody to imagine, understand or do ; It's beyond me why she wants to marry Jeff.

Definition: a strong feeling in favour of or against one group of people, or one side in an argument, often not based on fair judgement | an interest in one thing more than others; a special ability | the fact that the results of research or an experiment are not accurate because a particular factor has not been considered when collecting the information | the bias of a piece of cloth is an edge cut diagonally across the threads | to unfairly influence somebody’s opinions or decisions | to have an effect on the results of research or an experiment so that they do not show the real situation
Example: accusations of political bias in news programmes (= that reports are unfair and show favour to one political party) | The course has a strong practical bias. | If a response rate is low, the risk of bias in the findings will be greater. | The skirt is cut on the bias. | The newspapers have biased people against her. | The experiment contained an error which could bias the results.

Definition: to offer a particular amount of money for something that is for sale and compete against other people to buy it, especially at a public sale of goods or property : | If two or more people bid for a job , they compete with each other to do the work by offering to do it for a particular amount of money : | If someone bids to do something, they compete with other people to do it: | (in some card games ) to say, before play starts , how many points you expect to win in a particular game : | to give a greeting to someone, or to ask someone to do something: | an offer of a particular amount of money for something that is for sale : | an offer to do something when you are competing with other people to do it: | (in some card games ) a statement , made before play starts , of how many points a player thinks he or she will get in a particular game : | an attempt to achieve or get something: | in order to try to get or do something: | to offer a particular amount of money for something when competing against other people to buy it: | People who bid for/on a job offer to do it for a particular amount of money . | Someone who bids to do something competes with others to do it: | to give a greeting to someone, or to ask someone to do something: | an offer of a particular amount of money for something that is for sale : | A bid is also an offer to do a job for a particular price : | A bid for something is an attempt to achieve or obtain it: | to compete to buy something by offering a particular amount of money for it: | to compete for work by offering to do it for a particular price : | to compete to be chosen to do something: | an offer to buy something for a particular price : | an offer to do work for a particular price : | an attempt to achieve or get something:
Example: She knew she couldn't afford it, so she didn't bid. | The department is trying to ensure fairer competition among firms bidding for city contracts . | Paris is bidding to host the next Olympics . | Before you decide how to bid, you need to evaluate how strong the hand of cards you are holding is. | They bade her good morning . | I made a bid of $150 for the painting . | Sydney made a successful bid to host the Olympic Games . | The declarer must make as many tricks as his bid has promised . | Her bid for re-election was unsuccessful . | They have reduced the cost of borrowing in a bid to get the economy moving again. | A collector bid $500,000 for the portrait . |  | Paris is bidding to host the next Olympics . | He bade us farewell . | The minimum bid for these dolls is $75. | His bid to build the garage was too high. | Her bid for reelection was unsuccessful . | We have a keen interest in the property , although we have not made any definite decision to bid. | Taylor-Stanley yesterday confirmed it had bid for the contract . | The city is bidding to host the 2020 Olympic Games . | a bid of £50m | The state government is inviting bids to carry out maintenance work on all the state highways . | He lost his bid for re-election to the board .

Definition: to tie somebody/something with rope, string, etc. so that they/it cannot move or are held together strongly | to tie a long thin piece of cloth around something | to make people, organizations, etc. feel united so that they live or work together more happily or effectively | to force somebody to do something by making them promise to do it or by making it their duty to do it | to stick together or to make things stick together in a solid mass | to fasten the pages of a book together and put them inside a cover | to sew a piece of material to the edge of something to decorate it or to make it stronger | an annoying situation that is often difficult to avoid
Example: The sails are bound to the mast with cord. | She bound up his wounds. | Organizations such as schools and clubs bind a community together. | He had been bound to secrecy (= made to promise not to tell people about something) . | Add an egg yolk to make the mixture bind. | two volumes bound in leather | The blankets were bound with satin. |
Idioms: bind/tie somebody hand and foot : to tie somebody’s hands and feet together so that they cannot move or escape | bind/tie somebody hand and foot : to prevent somebody from doing what they want by creating rules, limits, etc. | in a bind : in a difficult situation that you do not know how to get out of

Definition: the story of a person’s life written by somebody else; this type of writing
Example: Boswell’s biography of Johnson

Definition: connected with the science of biology | connected with the processes that take place within living things | related by blood | using enzymes (= chemical substances that are found in plants and animals) to get clothes, etc. clean
Example: the biological sciences | the biological effects of radiation | a child’s biological parents (= natural parents, not the people who adopted him/her) | biological and non-biological powders

Definition: a senior priest in charge of the work of the Church in a city or district | a piece used in the game of chess that is like a bishop’s hat in shape and can move any number of squares in a diagonal line
Example: the Bishop of Oxford |

Definition: having a strong, unpleasant taste; not sweet | very serious and unpleasant, with a lot of anger and hate involved | feeling angry and unhappy because you feel that you have been treated unfairly | making you feel very unhappy; caused by great unhappiness | extremely cold and unpleasant
Example: Black coffee leaves a bitter taste in the mouth. | a long and bitter dispute | She is very bitter about losing her job. | to weep/shed bitter tears | bitter cold
Idioms: a bitter pill (for somebody) (to swallow) : a fact or an event that is unpleasant and difficult to accept ; The election defeat was a bitter pill for the party to swallow. | to/until the bitter end : continuing until you have done everything you can, or until something is completely finished, despite difficulties and problems ; They were prepared to fight to the bitter end for their rights. ; We will fight this case to the bitter end.

Definition: very strange or unusual
Example: a bizarre situation/incident/story

Definition: the flat part of a knife, tool or machine, which has a sharp edge or edges for cutting | one of the flat parts that turn around in an engine or on a helicopter | the flat wide part of an oar (= one of the long straight pieces of wood that are used to row a boat) that goes in the water | a single flat leaf of grass | the flat metal part on the bottom of an ice skate
Example: The machine comes with a plastic guard over the blade to protect the operator. | the blades of a propeller |  | Not a blade of grass stirred. |

Definition: to think or say that somebody/something is responsible for something bad
Example: She doesn't blame anyone for her father's death.
Idioms: be to blame (for something) : to be responsible for something bad ; If anyone's to blame, it's James. ; Which driver was to blame for the accident? ; A spokesman said that bad weather was partly to blame for the delay. | don’t blame me : used to advise somebody not to do something, when you think they will do it despite your advice ; Call her if you like, but don't blame me if she's angry. | I don’t blame you/her, etc. (for doing something) : used to say that you think that what somebody did was reasonable and the right thing to do ; I don't blame you for getting angry. They were being unreasonable. | only have yourself to blame | have nobody/no one to blame but yourself : used to say that you think something is somebody’s own fault ; If you lose your job, you'll only have yourself to blame.

Definition: a large cover, often made of wool, used especially on beds to keep people warm | a thick layer or mass of something | including or affecting all possible cases, situations or people | to cover something completely with a thick layer
Example: It’s cold tonight—can I have another blanket? | a blanket of fog/snow/cloud | a blanket ban on tobacco advertising | The ground was soon blanketed with snow.

Definition: an explosion or a powerful movement of air caused by an explosion | a sudden strong movement of air | a sudden loud noise, especially one made by a musical instrument that you blow, or by a whistle or a car horn | strong criticism | a very happy experience that is a lot of fun | a piece of advertising or information that is sent to a large number of people at the same time by email | to violently destroy or break something into pieces, using explosives | to make a loud unpleasant noise, especially music | to criticize somebody/something severely | to hit, kick or shoot somebody/something with a lot of force | to direct air, water, etc. at somebody/something with a lot of force | to destroy something such as a plant with disease, cold, heat, etc. | people sometimes say Blast! when they are annoyed about something
Example: a bomb blast | A blast of hot air hit us as we stepped off the plane. | three short blasts on the ship’s siren | Blast for prison governors in judge’s report. | The party was a blast. | They sent out email blasts and posted information on all their websites. | They blasted a huge crater in the runway. | Music suddenly blasted out from the speakers. | The movie was blasted by all the critics. | He blasted the ball past the goalie. | Police blasted the demonstrators with water cannons. | Their whole crop had been blasted by a late frost. | Oh blast! The car won't start.
Idioms: a blast from the past : a person or thing from your past that you see, hear, meet, etc. again in the present | (at) full blast : with the greatest possible volume or power ; She had the car stereo on at full blast.

Definition: to lose blood, especially from a wound or an injury | (in the past) to take blood from somebody as a way of treating disease | to force somebody to pay a lot of money over a period of time | to remove air or liquid from something so that it works correctly | to spread from one area of something to another area
Example: My finger's bleeding. |  | The company seems intent on bleeding us for every penny we have. |  | Keep the paint fairly dry so that the colours don't bleed into each other.
Idioms: bleed somebody dry : to take away all somebody’s money ; The big corporations are bleeding some of these small countries dry. | my heart bleeds (for somebody) : used to say that you do not feel sympathy for somebody ; ‘I have to go to Brazil on business.’ ‘My heart bleeds for you!’
Synonym: run | run | run | run | run | run | run

Definition: to mix two or more substances together | to form a mixture with something | to combine with something in an attractive or effective way; to combine something in this way | to produce something by mixing different types together | a mixture of different types of the same thing | a pleasant or useful combination of different things
Example: Blend the flour with the milk to make a smooth paste. | Oil does not blend with water. | The old and new buildings blend together perfectly. | blended whisky/tea | a blend of tea | a blend of youth and experience
Idioms: blend/fade into the woodwork : to behave in a way that does not attract any attention; to disappear or hide

Definition: to ask God to protect somebody/something | to make something holy by saying a prayer over it | to call God holy; to praise God | used to express surprise | used to show warm feelings for somebody because of something they have done
Example: They brought the children to Jesus and he blessed them. | The priest blessed the bread and wine. | We bless your holy name, O Lord. | Bless my soul! Here comes Bill! | ‘He bought us all a present.’ ‘Oh, bless!’
Idioms: be blessed with something/somebody : to have something good such as ability, great happiness, etc. ; She's blessed with excellent health. ; We're blessed with five lovely grandchildren. | bless you : said to somebody after they have sneezed | bless you, her, him, etc. : used to show that you are pleased with somebody, especially because of something they have done ; Sarah, bless her, had made a cup of tea. | God bless : used when you are leaving somebody, to say that you hope they will be safe, etc. ; Goodnight, God bless.

Definition: God’s help and protection, or a prayer asking for this | approval of or permission for something | something that is good or helpful
Example: to pray for God’s blessing | The government gave its blessing to the new plans. | Lack of traffic is one of the blessings of country life.
Idioms: a blessing in disguise : something that seems to be a problem at first, but that has good results in the end ; Losing your job might turn out to be a blessing in disguise. | count your blessings : to be grateful for the good things in your life

Tags: Oxford_3000 old-fashioned humorous saying British_English informal Disability Transport_by_car_or_lorry
Definition: not able to see | people who are blind | not noticing or realizing something | seeming to be unreasonable, and accepted without question; seeming to be out of control | that cannot be controlled by reason | that a driver in a car cannot see, or cannot see around | that does not make a difference between people on the basis of the quality mentioned, or favour one group over another
Example: Doctors think he will go blind . | recorded books for the blind | She is blind to her best friend's faults. | blind faith/obedience | blind chance | a blind driveway | In a piece of gender-blind casting, Hamlet is played by British actress Maxine Peake.
Idioms: (as) blind as a bat : not able to see well ; She’s as blind as a bat without her glasses. | the blind leading the blind : a situation in which people with almost no experience or knowledge give advice to others who also have no experience or knowledge | love is blind : when you love somebody, you cannot see their faults | not a blind bit/the blindest bit of… : not any ; He didn't take a blind bit of notice of me (= he ignored me) . ; It won't make the blindest bit of difference (= it will make no difference at all) . | turn a blind eye (to something) : to pretend not to notice something bad that is happening, so you do not have to do anything about it ; The authorities were either unaware of the problem or they turned a blind eye to it.

Definition: to talk in a way that shows you are too proud of something that you have or can do | to have something that is impressive | something that a person talks about in a very proud way, often to seem more important or clever
Example: I don't want to boast, but I can actually speak six languages. | The hotel also boasts two swimming pools and a golf course. | Despite his boasts that his children were brilliant, neither of them went to college.
Synonym: be proud of | be proud of

Definition: brave and confident; not afraid to say what you feel or to take risks | in a thick, dark type | that can be easily seen; having a strong clear appearance | thick, dark type used for printing words or letters
Example: It was a bold move on their part to open a business in France. | Highlight the important words in bold type. | the bold outline of a mountain against the sky | Headwords are printed in bold.
Idioms: be/make so bold (as to do something) : used especially when politely asking a question or making a suggestion which you hope will not offend anyone (although it may criticize them slightly) ; If I may be so bold as to suggest that he made a mistake in his calculations… | (as) bold as brass : without showing any respect, shame or fear ; She marched in here, bold as brass, and demanded a pay rise.

Definition: an occasion when a bomb is dropped or left somewhere; the act of doing this
Example: recent bombings in major cities

Definition: something that forms a connection between people or groups, such as a feeling of friendship or shared ideas and experiences | an agreement by a government or a company to pay you interest on the money you have lent; a document containing this agreement | a sum of money that is paid as bail | a legal agreement by which a bank lends you money to buy a house, etc. which you pay back over many years; the sum of money that is lent | the ropes or chains keeping somebody prisoner; anything that stops you from being free to do what you want | a legal written agreement or promise | the way in which two things are joined together | the way in which atoms are held together in a chemical compound | to join two things strongly together; to join strongly to something else | to develop or create a relationship of trust with somebody
Example: the bond that links us | government bonds | He was released on $5 000 bond. | to pay off a bond | to release somebody from their bonds | We entered into a solemn bond. | a firm bond between the two surfaces |  | This new glue bonds a variety of surfaces in seconds. | New mothers need time to bond with their babies.
Idioms: somebody’s word is their bond : somebody’s promise can be relied on completely
Synonym: tie | tie | tie | tie | tie | tie | tie | tie | tie

Definition: an extra amount of money that is added to a payment, especially to somebody’s wages or salary as a reward | anything pleasant that is extra and more or better than you were expecting
Example: a £100 Christmas bonus | Being able to walk to work is an added bonus of the new job.

Definition: an arrangement that you make in advance to buy a ticket to travel somewhere, go to the theatre, etc. | an arrangement for somebody to perform at a theatre, in a concert, etc. | an act of the referee writing a player’s name in a book, as a punishment because an offence has been committed
Example: a booking form/hall/clerk |  |

Definition: a sudden increase in trade and economic activity; a period of wealth and success | a period when something such as a sport or a type of music suddenly becomes very popular and successful | a long pole that the bottom of a sail is attached to and that you move to change the position of the sail | a loud deep sound | a floating barrier that is placed across a river or the entrance to a harbour to prevent ships or other objects from coming in or going out | a long pole that carries a microphone or other equipment | to make a loud deep sound | to say something in a loud deep voice | to have a period of rapid growth; to become bigger, more successful, etc.
Example: Living standards improved rapidly during the post-war boom. | The only way to satisfy the golf boom was to build more courses. |  | the distant boom of the guns |  |  | Outside, thunder boomed and crashed. | ‘Get out of my sight!’ he boomed. | By the 1980s, the computer industry was booming.
Idioms: boom and bust : a situation in which a period of rapid economic growth is followed by one of sudden decline ; High house prices encourage boom and bust and leave the economy vulnerable. ; Amid the inevitable boom and bust cycle , families are facing pay cuts and job losses.

Tags: Oxford_5000 North_American_English informal becoming_old-fashioned Success Change,_cause_and_effect
Definition: to make something increase, or become better or more successful | to steal something | something that helps or encourages somebody/something | an increase in something | an increase in power in an engine or a piece of electrical equipment | an act of pushing somebody up from behind
Example: to boost exports/profits |  | a great/tremendous/welcome boost | a boost in car sales |  | He gave her a boost over the fence.

Definition: if something bounces or you bounce it, it moves quickly away from a surface it has just hit or you make it do this | to jump up and down on something | to move a child up and down while they are sitting on your knee in order to entertain them | to move up and down; to move something up and down | to move up and down in a particular direction | to move somewhere in a lively and cheerful way | if a cheque bounces , or a bank bounces it, the bank refuses to accept it because there is not enough money in the bank account to pay it | to tell somebody your ideas in order to find out what they think about them | if an email bounces or the system bounces it, it returns to the person who sent it because the system cannot deliver it | to force somebody to leave a job, team, place, etc. | to leave a place or group of people | the action of bouncing | the ability to bounce or to make something bounce | the energy that a person has | the quality in a person’s hair that shows that it is in good condition and means that it does not lie flat
Example: The ball bounced twice before he could reach it. | She bounced up and down excitedly on the bed. | I bounced the baby on my knee while Pete did the dishes. | Her hair bounced as she walked. | The bus bounced down the hill. | He bounced across the room to greet them. |  | He bounced ideas off colleagues everywhere he went. | I tried to send her an email but it bounced. | He was soon bounced from the post. | All right, cool, we'll bounce. | one bounce of the ball | There's not much bounce left in these balls. | All her old bounce was back. | thin fine hair, lacking in bounce
Idioms: be bouncing off the walls : to be so full of energy or so excited that you cannot keep still ; The kids have been bouncing off the walls all day. | on the bounce : one after the other, without anything else coming between ; We've won six matches on the bounce.
Synonym: jump | jump | jump | jump | jump | jump | jump | jump | jump | jump | jump | jump

Tags: Oxford_5000 formal North_American_English old-fashioned British_English informal
Definition: certain or likely to happen, or to do or be something | forced to do something by law, duty or a particular situation | prevented from going somewhere or from working normally by the conditions mentioned | travelling, or ready to travel, in a particular direction or to a particular place | to run with long steps, especially in an enthusiastic way | to form the edge or limit of an area | a high or long jump
Example: There are bound to be changes when the new system is introduced. | We are not bound by the decision. | Strike-bound travellers face long delays. | homeward bound (= going home) | The dogs bounded ahead. | The field was bounded on the left by a wood. | The dog cleared the gate in one bound.
Idioms: be bound together by/in something : to be closely connected ; communities bound together by customs and traditions | be bound up in something : very busy with something; very interested or involved in something ; He's too bound up in his work to have much time for his children. | bound and determined : very determined to do something ; I came here bound and determined to put the last 12 months behind me. | bound up with something : closely connected with something ; From that moment my life became inextricably bound up with hers. | (feel) honour-bound to do something : to feel that you must do something because of your sense of moral duty ; She felt honour-bound to attend as she had promised to. ; He felt honour bound to help her. | I’ll be bound : I feel sure ; They’re up to some mischief, I’ll be bound! | by/in leaps and bounds : very quickly; in large amounts ; Her health has improved in leaps and bounds. ; His technique has come on in leaps and bounds this season.
Synonym: certain | going to | certain | certain | certain | required | certain | certain | certain | required

Definition: a real or imagined line that marks the limits or edges of something and separates it from other things or places; a dividing line | a hit of the ball that crosses the boundary of the playing area and scores extra points
Example: After the war the national boundaries were redrawn. | He hit 13 boundaries from 183 balls.
Idioms: push the boundaries/limits : to attempt to go beyond what is allowed or thought to be possible ; We aim to push the boundaries of what we can achieve. ; She pushes her physical limits through various endurance challenges.

Definition: to bend your head or body forward , especially as a way of showing someone respect or expressing thanks to people who have watched you perform : | the movement of bending your head or body forward , especially as a way of showing someone respect or expressing thanks to people who have watched you perform : | the front part of a boat or ship | in the sport of rowing , the rowing position closest to the front of the boat : | in the sport of rowing , the person sitting closest to the front of the boat : | a knot with two curved parts and two loose ends , used as a decoration or to tie shoes : | a weapon for shooting arrows , made of a long , thin piece of wood or fibreglass bent into a curve by a tightly stretched string : | a long , thin piece of wood with hair from the tail of a horse stretched along it, used to play musical instruments that have strings : | to play an instrument that has strings , using a long , thin piece of wood with hair from the tail of a horse stretched along it: | to bend the head or body forward as a way of showing respect , expressing thanks , or greeting someone: | the act of bending the head or body forward : | the front part of a ship | a knot with two curved parts and two loose ends , which is used as a decoration or to tie shoes | a weapon for shooting arrows , often used for sport , made of a long , narrow piece of wood bent into a curve by a string that is stretched tightly between its two ends | a long , thin piece of wood with many hairs stretched between its ends , used to play musical instruments that have strings :
Example: They bowed to the Queen. | The audience applauded enthusiastically , and she came back on stage to take another bow. |  | She has moved from the number 5 seat to row at bow. | He was bow in his University's eight. | I tied the ribbon around the present in a pretty bow. | He made his own bow and arrows . | Violins are played with bows. | I'm not bowing, but using my fingers pizzicato . | We knelt and bowed our heads in prayer . | The troupe’s artistic director took a bow with his dancers at the final curtain last night . |  |  |  | a violin bow

Definition: a failure to do something that must be done by law | an action that breaks an agreement to behave in a particular way | a break in a relationship between people or countries | an opening that is created during a military attack or by strong winds or seas | to not keep to an agreement or not keep a promise | to make a hole in a wall, fence, etc. so that somebody/something can go through it
Example: a breach of contract/copyright/warranty | a breach of confidence/trust |  | They escaped through a breach in the wire fence. | The government is accused of breaching the terms of the treaty. | The dam had been breached.
Idioms: step into the breach : to do somebody’s job or work when they are suddenly or unexpectedly unable to do it ; She stepped into the breach caused by Stella's illness.

Tags: Oxford_5000 specialist old-fashioned Transport_by_car_or_lorry Engineering Difficulty_and_failure Mental_health
Definition: an occasion when a vehicle or machine stops working | a failure of a relationship, discussion or system | detailed information that you get by studying a set of figures | the process of a substance breaking into the parts of which it is made | a period of serious mental illness in which somebody is unable to deal with normal life
Example: a breakdown on the motorway | The only ground for divorce is the irretrievable breakdown of the marriage. | First, let's look at a breakdown of the costs. | the breakdown of proteins in the digestive system | It all became too much and he had some sort of breakdown.

Definition: an important development that may lead to an agreement or achievement | in which a performer or type of product is successful for the first time, when it is likely to be even more successful in the future
Example: to make/achieve a breakthrough | It was a breakthrough album for the band.

Definition: either of the two round soft parts at the front of a woman’s body that produce milk when she has had a baby | the similar, smaller part on a man’s body, which does not produce milk | the top part of the front of your body, below your neck | the part of a piece of clothing that covers your chest | the front part of a bird’s body | meat from the front part of the body of a bird or an animal | having the type of chest or breasts mentioned | the part of the body where the feelings and emotions are thought to be
Example: She put the baby to her breast. |  | He cradled the child against his breast. | A row of medals was pinned to the breast of his coat. | breast feathers | chicken/turkey breasts | a small-breasted/full-breasted woman | a troubled breast
Idioms: beat your breast : to show that you feel sorry about something that you have done, especially in public and in an exaggerated way | make a clean breast of something : to tell the truth about something so that you no longer feel guilty ; He made a clean breast of everything and admitted taking the money.

Tags: Oxford_5000 North_American_English informal often_offensive saying Birds Animals Farming
Definition: to have sex and produce young | to keep animals or plants in order to produce young ones for a particular purpose | to be the cause of something | to educate somebody in a particular way as they are growing up | a particular type of animal that has been developed by people in a certain way, especially a type of dog, cat or farm animal | a type of person
Example: Many animals breed only at certain times of the year. | The rabbits are bred for their long coats. | Nothing breeds success like success. | Fear of failure was bred into him at an early age. | Labradors and other large breeds of dog | He represents a new breed of politician.
Idioms: born and bred : born and having grown up in a particular place with a particular background and education ; He was born and bred in Boston. ; I'm a Londoner, born and bred. | breed like rabbits : to have a lot of babies in a short space of time | familiarity breeds contempt : knowing somebody/something very well may cause you to lose respect for them/it

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English old-fashioned informal Buildings Physics_and_chemistry Difficulty_and_failure
Definition: baked clay used for building walls, houses and other buildings; an individual block of this | a plastic or wooden block, used as a toy for young children to build things with | a friend that you can rely on when you need help
Example: The school is built of brick. |  | Thanks for looking after the children today—you're a real brick.
Idioms: be banging, etc. your head against a brick wall : to keep trying to do something that will never be successful ; Trying to reason with them was like banging my head against a brick wall. | be up against a brick wall : to be unable to make any progress because there is a difficulty that stops you | drop a brick/clanger : to say something that offends or embarrasses somebody, although you did not intend to | like a cat on hot bricks : very nervous ; She was like a cat on hot bricks before her driving test. | like a cat on a hot tin roof : very nervous ; She was like a cat on a hot tin roof before her driving test. | like a ton of bricks : very heavily; very severely ; Disappointment hit her like a ton of bricks. ; They came down on him like a ton of bricks (= criticized him very severely) . | make bricks without straw : to try to work without the necessary material, money, information, etc.
