Definition: lasting only a short time; short | using few words | short and not covering much of the body
Example: a brief visit/meeting/conversation | a brief description/summary/overview | a brief skirt
Idioms: in brief : in a few words, without details ; In brief, the meeting was a disaster. ; Now the rest of the news in brief.

Definition: for a short time | in few words
Example: He had spoken to Emma only briefly . | Briefly, the argument is as follows…

Tags: Oxford_3000 OPAL_written North_American_English British_English informal Language
Definition: wide | used after a measurement of distance to show how wide something is | including a great variety of people or things | general; not detailed | covering a wide area | if somebody has a broad accent (= a way of pronouncing the words of a language) , you can hear very easily which area they come from | if somebody gives a broad hint , they make it very clear what they are thinking or what they want | dealing with sex in a way that makes people laugh
Example: a broad street/avenue/river | The wardrobe stands one metre broad and two metres high. | a broad range of products | the broad outline of a proposal | a broad expanse of water | a broad Yorkshire accent |  | The movie mixes broad humor with romance.

Definition: a way of connecting to the internet that allows you to receive information, including pictures, etc., very quickly and that is always active (so that the user does not have to connect each time) | signals that use a wide range of frequencies
Example: plans to upgrade broadband in rural areas |

Tags: Oxford_3000 British_English North_American_English especially_British_English especially_North_American_English TV,_radio_and_news
Definition: to send out programmes on television or radio | to tell a lot of people about something
Example: The concert will be broadcast live (= at the same time as it takes place) tomorrow evening. | I don't like to broadcast the fact that my father owns the company.

Definition: a person whose job is presenting or talking on television or radio programmes | a company that sends out television or radio programmes
Example: She is a writer and broadcaster on environmental matters. | The country needs a national public service broadcaster like the BBC.

Definition: generally, without considering details | if you smile broadly , you smile with your mouth stretched very wide because you are very pleased or are enjoying something very much

Definition: to look, smile, etc. in a particular direction | to make light, a shadow, etc. appear in a particular place | to say, do or suggest something that makes people doubt something or think that somebody is less honest, good, etc. | to vote for somebody/something | to choose actors to play the different parts in a film, play, etc.; to choose an actor to play a particular role | to describe or present somebody/yourself in a particular way | to throw one end of a fishing line into a river, etc. | to throw somebody/something somewhere, especially using force | when a snake casts its skin, the skin comes off as part of a natural process | if a horse casts a shoe, the shoe comes off by accident | to shape hot liquid metal, etc. by pouring it into a hollow container (called a mould )
Example: She cast a welcoming smile in his direction. | Someone was standing in the dark shadow cast by the light. | This latest evidence casts serious doubt on his version of events. | He refused to cast a vote for either candidate. | The play is being cast in both the US and Britain. | He cast himself as the innocent victim of a hate campaign. |  | The priceless treasures had been cast into the Nile. |  |  | a statue cast in bronze
Idioms: cast/run an eye/your eyes over something : to look at or examine something quickly ; Could you just run your eyes over this report? ; I cast my eyes around the room but couldn't see any familiar faces. | cast/shed/throw light on something : to make a problem, etc. easier to understand ; Recent research has cast new light on the causes of the disease. | cast/shed/throw light on something : to make a problem, etc. easier to understand ; Recent research has thrown new light on the causes of the disease. | cast your mind back (to something) : to make yourself think about something that happened in the past ; I want you to cast your minds back to the first time you met. | cast your net wide : to consider a lot of different people, activities, possibilities, etc. when you are looking for something ; It’s worth casting your net wide when applying for your first job. | cast a spell (on somebody/something) : to use words that are thought to be magic and have the power to change or influence somebody/something | the die is cast : used to say that an event has happened or a decision has been made that cannot be changed | draw/cast lots (for something/to do something) : to use a method of choosing somebody/something that involves putting a number folded pieces of paper in a bag, one of them with a mark on it. Definition: not formal | not showing much care or thought; seeming not to be worried; not wanting to show that something is important to you | without paying attention to detail | not permanent; not done, or doing something regularly | without deep feelings | happening by chance; doing something by chance | informal clothes or shoes | a casual worker (= one who does not work permanently for a company)

Definition: a complete list of items, for example of things that people can look at or buy | a long series of things that happen (usually bad things) | to arrange a list of things in order in a catalogue ; to record something in a catalogue | to give a list of things connected with a particular person, event, etc.

Definition: care that you take in order to avoid danger or mistakes; the fact of not taking any risks | a warning that is given by the police to somebody who has committed a crime that is not too serious | a warning or a piece of advice about a possible danger or risk | to warn somebody about the possible dangers or problems of something | to warn somebody officially that anything they say may be used as evidence against them in court | to warn somebody officially that they will be punished if they do something wrong or illegal again
Example: extreme/great caution | As a first offender, she got off with a caution. | a word/note of caution | I would caution against getting too involved. | Suspects must be cautioned before any questions are asked. | She wasn't sent to the juvenile court; instead she was cautioned.
Idioms: throw/cast caution to the wind(s) : to stop caring about how dangerous something might be; to start taking risks ; He threw caution to the wind and dived into the water after the child.

Definition: being careful about what you say or do, especially to avoid danger or mistakes; not taking any risks

Definition: a large hole in the side of a hill or cliff or under the ground | to finally do what somebody wants after you have been strongly opposing them; to accept defeat

Definition: to stop happening or existing; to stop something from happening or existing

Definition: the power of pleasing or attracting people | a feature or quality that is pleasant or attractive | a small object worn on a chain or bracelet that is believed to bring good luck | an act or words believed to have magic power | to please or attract somebody in order to make them like you or do what you want | to control or protect somebody/something using magic, or as if using magic

Definition: to run, drive, etc. after somebody/something in order to catch them or it | to force somebody/something to run away | to try to obtain or achieve something, for example money, work or success | to try to persuade somebody to have a sexual relationship with you | to persuade somebody to do something that they should have done already | to rush or hurry somewhere | to cut patterns or designs on metal | an act of running or driving after somebody/something in order to catch them or it | a process of trying hard to get something | hunting animals as a sport | a long race in which horses have to jump over fences, water, etc. | a long race in which people run and jump over gates and water, etc. around a track

Idioms: chase your (own) tail : to be very busy but in fact achieve very little | cut to the chase : to stop wasting time and start talking about the most important thing ; Right, let's cut to the chase. Definition: to cut something into pieces with a sharp tool such as a knife | to suddenly stop providing or allowing something; to suddenly reduce something by a large amount | to hit somebody/something downwards with a quick, short movement | a thick slice of meat with a bone attached to it, especially from a pig or sheep | an act of cutting something in a quick movement downwards using an axe or a knife | an act of hitting somebody/something with the side of your hand in a quick movement downwards | the part of a person’s or an animal’s face around the mouth | the technical skill of an actor or a jazz or rock musician
Example: He was chopping logs for firewood. | Their training courses are to be chopped. | Guzman tried to chop the ball on the ground and use his speed. | a pork/lamb chop | Give the mushrooms a quick chop before adding them to the pan. | a karate chop | The dog sat licking its chops. | He has the acting chops to carry a major film.
Idioms: chop and change : to keep changing your mind or what you are doing | be for the chop : to be likely to be dismissed from a job ; Who's next for the chop? | be for the chop : to be likely to be stopped or ended | bust your chops/butt/hump (doing something/to do something) : to make a great effort to do something ; I busted my chops to get into law school. ; These guys were busting their butts on the field, trying to excel at athletics. ; It’s a problem which nobody is going to bust a gut trying to solve. | bust somebody's chops : to criticize somebody ; Your blog is great but I have to bust your chops a little over this post. | bust a gut (doing something/to do something) : to make a great effort to do something ; It’s a problem which nobody is going to bust a gut trying to solve. ; I busted my chops to get into law school. ; These guys were busting their butts on the field, trying to excel at athletics. | get/be given the chop : to be dismissed from a job ; The whole department has been given the chop. | get/be given the chop : to be stopped or ended ; Three more schemes have got the chop. | not much chop : not very good or useful

Definition: lasting for a long time; difficult to cure | having had a disease for a long time | lasting a long time; difficult to solve | very bad
Example: chronic bronchitis/arthritis/asthma | a chronic alcoholic/depressive | the country’s chronic unemployment problem | The film was just chronic.

Definition: a thick, solid piece that has been cut or broken off something | a fairly large amount of something | a phrase or group of words that can be learnt as a unit by somebody who is learning a language. Examples of chunks are ‘Can I have the bill, please?’ and ‘Pleased to meet you’.
Example: a chunk of cheese/masonry | I've already written a fair chunk of the article. | The listening texts consist of short, bite-sized chunks which are accessible to beginners.
Idioms: blow chunks : to vomit

Definition: a line, route or journey around a place | the complete path of wires and equipment along which an electric current flows | a series of games or matches in which the same players regularly take part | a track for cars or motorcycles to race around | a series of places or events of a particular kind at which the same people appear or take part | a series of different exercises that you do one after the other when doing circuit training in sport or fitness training | a regular journey made by a judge to hear court cases in each of the courts of law in a particular area
Example: The race ended with eight laps of a city centre circuit. | an electrical circuit | This is her biggest triumph in nine years on the women's tennis circuit. |  | the lecture/cabaret circuit | I do circuits in the gym twice a week. | a circuit court/judge
Synonym: beat | beat | beat | circumference | beat | beat | beat

Definition: when a liquid, gas or air circulates or is circulated , it moves continuously around a place or system | if a story, an idea, information, etc. circulates or if you circulate it, it spreads or it is passed from one person to another | to send goods or information to all the people in a group | to move around a group, especially at a party, talking to different people
Example: The condition prevents the blood from circulating freely. | Rumours began to circulate about his financial problems. | They circulated a petition for his release. |

Definition: the movement of blood around the body | the passing or spreading of something from one person or place to another | the usual number of copies of a newspaper or magazine that are sold each day, week, etc. | the movement of something (for example air, water, gas, etc.) around an area or inside a system or machine | the fact that somebody takes part in social activities at a particular time
Example: Regular exercise will improve blood circulation. | the circulation of money/information/ideas | a daily circulation of more than one million | Poor air circulation can cause condensation. | Anne has been ill but now she's back in circulation.

Definition: the conditions and facts that are connected with and affect a situation, an event or an action | the conditions of a person’s life, especially the money they have | situations and events that affect and influence your life and that are not in your control
Example: Police said there were no suspicious circumstances surrounding the boy's death. | We want to work towards improving the often difficult circumstances in which people find themselves. | a victim of circumstance (= a person who has suffered because of a situation that they cannot control)
Idioms: in/under the circumstances : used before or after a statement to show that you have thought about the conditions that affect a situation before making a decision or a statement ; Under the circumstances, it seemed better not to tell him about the accident. ; She did the job very well in the circumstances. ; In the circumstances, you'd better call the police. | in/under no circumstances : used to emphasize that something should never happen or be allowed ; Under no circumstances should you lend Paul any money. ; Don't open the door, in any circumstances. ; Under no circumstances should you leave the door unlocked. | pomp and circumstance : formal and impressive ceremony ; Funerals of famous people were conducted with great pomp and circumstance. | reduced circumstances : the state of being poorer than you were before. People say ‘ living in reduced circumstances ’ to avoid saying ‘ poor ’ .

Definition: to mention something as a reason or an example, or in order to support what you are saying | to speak or write the exact words from a book, an author, etc. | to order somebody to appear in court; to name somebody officially in a legal case | to mention somebody officially or publicly because they deserve special praise
Example: She cited examples of companies the city has helped relocate or expand. | He cites no primary sources for his claims. | He was cited for contempt of court. | He was cited for bravery.

Definition: a person who has the legal right to belong to a particular country | a person who lives in a particular place
Example: The defeat of the president did not change the lives of ordinary citizens for the better. | When you're old, people treat you like a second-class citizen .

Definition: the legal right to belong to a particular country | the state of being a citizen and accepting the responsibilities of it
Example: They were granted full French citizenship. | an education that prepares young people for citizenship

Definition: officially connected with a town or city | connected with the people who live in a town or city
Example: civic buildings/leaders | a sense of civic pride (= pride that people feel for their town or city)

Definition: connected with the people who live in a country | connected with the state rather than with religion or with the armed forces | involving personal legal matters and not criminal law | polite in a formal way but possibly not friendly
Example: civil unrest (= that is caused by groups of people within a country) | the recognition of civil unions for same-sex couples | a civil court | The less time I have to spend being civil to him the better!

Definition: a person who is not a member of the armed forces or the police | connected with people who are not members of the armed forces or the police
Example: Two soldiers and one civilian were killed in the explosion. | He left the army and returned to civilian life.

Definition: a state of human society that is very developed and organized | a society, its culture and its way of life during a particular period of time or in a particular part of the world | all the people in the world and the societies they live in, considered as a whole | a place that offers you the comfortable way of life of a modern society
Example: the technology of modern civilization | the civilizations of ancient Greece and Rome | Environmental damage threatens the whole of civilization. | It's good to be back in civilization after two weeks in a tent!
Synonym: civilized life or society | civilized life or society | civilized life or society | civilized life or society

Definition: to make something clearer or easier to understand | to make something, especially butter, pure by heating it
Example: to clarify a situation/problem/issue | clarified butter

Definition: the quality of being expressed clearly | the ability to think about or understand something clearly | if a picture, substance or sound has clarity , you can see or hear it very clearly, or see through it easily
Example: a lack of clarity in the law | clarity of thought/purpose/vision | the clarity of the sound on the recording

Definition: a short fight between two groups of people | an argument between two people or groups of people who have different beliefs and ideas | the difference that exists between two things that are opposed to each other | a situation in which two events happen at the same time so that you cannot go to or see them both | a loud noise made by two metal objects being hit together | an occasion when two teams or players compete against each other | the situation when two colours, designs, etc. look ugly when they are put together | to come together and fight or compete in a contest | to argue or disagree seriously with somebody about something, and to show this in public | to be very different and opposed to each other | to happen at the same time so that you cannot go to or see them both | to look ugly when put together | to hit together and make a loud ringing noise; to make two metal objects do this
Example: Clashes broke out between police and demonstrators. | There has been a head-on clash between the two candidates over education policy. | a clash of interests/opinions/cultures | a clash in the timetable/schedule | a clash of cymbals/swords | Bayern’s clash with Roma in the Champions League |  | The two sets of supporters clashed outside the stadium. | The leaders and members clashed on the issue. | His left-wing views clashed with his father's politics. | Unfortunately your party clashes with a wedding I'm going to. | The wallpaper clashes with the carpet. | The long blades clashed together.

Definition: accepted or deserving to be accepted as one of the best or most important of its kind | with all the features you would expect to find; very typical | attractive, but simple and traditional in style or design; not affected by changes in fashion | people say That’s classic! when they find something very funny, when they think somebody has been very stupid or when something annoying, but not surprising, happens
Example: a classic film/story/game | a classic example of poor communication | a classic grey suit | She's not going to help? Oh, that's classic!

Definition: the act or process of putting people or things into a group or class (= of classifying them) | a group, class, division, etc. into which somebody or something is put | the act of putting animals, plants, etc. into groups, classes or divisions according to their characteristics | a system of arranging books, tapes, magazines, etc. in a library into groups according to their subject
Example: a style of music that defies classification (= is like no other) | a document with the security classification ‘confidential’ | The classification of bony fish is extremely complicated. |

Definition: to arrange something in groups according to features that they have in common | to decide which type or group somebody/something belongs to | to state that information is officially secret
Example: The books in the library are classified according to subject. | Only eleven of these accidents were classified as major. | All the documents relating to the case have been classified.

Definition: a person whose job is to serve customers in a shop | a person whose job is dealing with people arriving at or leaving a hotel | a person whose job is to keep the records or accounts and do other routine duties in an office, shop, etc. | an official in charge of the records of a council, court, etc.

Definition: to hold on tightly to somebody/something | to stick to something | to stay close to somebody, especially because you need them emotionally

Definition: a building or part of a hospital where people can go for special medical treatment or advice | a period of time during which doctors give special medical treatment or advice | a private hospital or one that treats health problems of a particular kind | a building shared by a group of doctors who work together | an occasion in a hospital when medical students learn by watching a specialist examine and treat patients | an occasion at which a professional person, especially a sportsman or sportswoman gives advice and training

Definition: relating to the examination and treatment of patients and their illnesses | cold and calm and without feeling or sympathy | very plain; without decoration

Definition: a short part of a film that is shown separately | a small metal or plastic object used for holding things together or in place | a piece of jewellery that fastens to your clothes | the act of cutting something to make it shorter | a quick hit with your hand | a set of bullets in a metal container that is placed in or attached to a gun for firing | to fasten something to something else with a clip ; to be fastened with a clip | to cut something with scissors or shears , in order to make it shorter or neater; to remove something from somewhere by cutting it off | to hit the edge or side of something | to cut something out of something else using scissors
Example: Here is a clip from her latest movie. | a hair clip | a diamond clip | He gave the hedge a clip. | She gave him a clip round the ear for being cheeky. |  | He clipped the microphone (on) to his collar. | to clip a hedge | The car clipped the kerb as it turned. | to clip a coupon (out of the paper)
Idioms: at a fast, good, steady, etc. clip : quickly ; Land prices will rise at a healthy clip. | clip somebody’s wings : to limit a person’s freedom or power ; Having a new baby to take care of has clipped her wings.

Definition: near in space or time | near in family relationship | having a strong connection | in a way that shows you are very involved in the work or activities of somebody else, usually seeing and talking to them regularly | carefully | in a way that is very similar to somebody/something else | won or likely to be won by only a small amount or distance | with little or no space in between | cut very short, near to the skin

Definition: the situation when a factory, school, hospital, etc. shuts permanently | the temporary closing of a road or bridge | the feeling that a difficult or an unpleasant experience has come to an end or been dealt with in an acceptable way
Example: factory closures | There will be road closures and diversions in the area from 8 p.m. | The conviction of their son's murderer helped to give them a sense of closure.

Definition: a group of things of the same type that grow or appear close together | a group of people, animals or things close together | a group of consonants that come together in a word or phrase, for example / str / at the beginning of string | to come together in a small group or groups
Example: The telescope is focused on a dense cluster of stars at the edge of the galaxy. | a cluster of spectators | a consonant cluster | The children clustered together in the corner of the room.

Definition: a government formed by two or more political parties working together | a group formed by people from several different groups, especially political ones, agreeing to work together for a particular purpose

Definition: of or near a coast

Definition: a drink usually made from a mixture of one or more spirits (= strong alcoholic drinks) and fruit juice. It can also be made without alcohol. | a dish of small pieces of food, usually served cold | a mixture of different substances or elements, often ones that do not mix together well

Definition: connected with mental processes of understanding
Example: a child’s cognitive development

Definition: logical and well organized; easy to understand and clear | able to talk and express yourself clearly

Definition: to take place at the same time | to be the same or very similar | to meet; to share the same space

Definition: the fact of two things happening at the same time by chance, in a surprising way | the fact of things being present at the same time | the fact of two or more opinions, etc. being the same

Definition: to work together with somebody in order to produce or achieve something | to help the enemy who has taken control of your country during a war

Definition: the act of working with another person or group of people to create or produce something | a piece of work produced by two or more people or groups of people working together | the act of helping the enemy during a war when they have taken control of your country

Definition: to fall down or fall in suddenly, often after breaking apart | to fall down (and usually become unconscious), especially because you are very ill | to sit or lie down and relax, especially after working hard | to fail suddenly or completely | to decrease suddenly in amount or value | to fold something into a shape that uses less space; to be able to be folded in this way | if a lung or blood vessel collapses or is collapsed , it falls in and becomes flat and empty
Example: The roof collapsed under the weight of snow. | She collapsed and was rushed to hospital. | When I get home I like to collapse on the sofa and listen to music. | Talks between management and unions have collapsed. | Share prices collapsed after news of poor trading figures. | The table collapses for easy storage. |

Definition: done or shared by all members of a group of people; involving a whole group or society | used to refer to all members of a group | a group of people who own a business or a farm and run it together; the business that they run
Example: collective leadership/decision-making/responsibility | The collective name for mast, boom and sails on a boat is the ‘rig’. | an independent collective making films for television

Definition: a person who collects things, either as a hobby or as a job | (in India) the chief government official of a district, responsible for general administration, development programmes, etc.

Definition: an accident in which two vehicles or people crash into each other | a strong disagreement between two people or between ideas, opinions, etc. that are opposed to each other; the meeting of two things that are very different
Example: a collision between two trains | a collision between two opposing points of view
Idioms: be on a collision course (with somebody/something) : to be in a situation that is almost certain to cause an argument ; I was on a collision course with my boss over the sales figures. | be on a collision course (with somebody/something) : to be moving in a direction in which it is likely that you will crash into somebody/something ; A giant iceberg was on a collision course with the ship.

Definition: connected with or belonging to a country that controls another country | typical of or connected with the US at the time when it was still a British colony | a person who lives in a colony and who comes from the country that controls it
Example: a colonial power | life in colonial times | British colonials in India

Definition: a country or an area that is governed by people from another, more powerful, country | a group of people who go to live permanently in a colony | a group of people from the same place or with the same work or interests who live in a particular city or country or who live together | a small town set up by an employer or an organization for its workers | a group of plants or animals that live together or grow in the same place
Example: former British colonies | The colony was/were looking forward to fifty or more years of autonomy. | The American colony in Paris was/were looking forward to this event. |  | a colony of ants

Definition: full of bright colours or having a lot of different colours | interesting or exciting; full of variety, sometimes in a way that shocks people

Definition: a journalist who writes regular articles for a newspaper or magazine
Example: a newspaper columnist

Definition: fighting or a fight, especially during a time of war | to stop something unpleasant or harmful from happening or from getting worse | to fight against an enemy
Example: He was killed in combat. | measures to combat crime/inflation/unemployment/disease | The security forces were unwilling to combat the insurgents.

Definition: two or more things joined or mixed together to form a single unit | the act of joining or mixing together two or more things to form a single unit | a series of numbers or letters used to open a combination lock | a piece of underwear covering the body and legs, worn in the past
Example: The tragedy was due to a combination of factors . | The firm is working on a new product in combination with several overseas partners. | I can't remember the combination. |

Definition: the state of being physically relaxed and free from pain; the state of having a pleasant life, with everything that you need | a feeling of not suffering or worrying so much; a feeling of being less unhappy | a person or thing that helps you when you are suffering, worried or unhappy | a thing that makes your life easier or more comfortable
Example: The hotel offers a high standard of comfort and service. | I tried to offer a few words of comfort. | Her being with me was such a comfort. | The hotel has all modern comforts/every modern comfort.
Idioms: too close for comfort : so near that it is uncomfortable or dangerous ; When she leaves the stage, the fans can get a little too close for comfort. ; They got the win but it was a tight match, definitely too close for comfort.

Definition: humorous and making you laugh | connected with comedy (= entertainment that is funny and that makes people laugh) | a magazine, usually for children, that tells stories through pictures | the section of a newspaper that contains comic strips | a person who makes people laugh by telling jokes or funny stories
Example: a comic monologue/story | a comic opera | superhero comics |  |

Definition: an order given to a person or an animal | an instruction given to a computer | control and authority over a situation or a group of people | a part of an army, air force , etc. that is organized and controlled separately; a group of officers who give orders | your knowledge of something; your ability to do or use something, especially a language
Example: Begin when I give the command. | The computer executes commands successively in the order they arrive. | He has 1 200 men under his command. | Bomber Command | Applicants will be expected to have (a) good command of English.
Idioms: at your command : if you have a skill or an amount of something at your command , you are able to use it well and completely ; With all the words at my command, I could not express how I felt. ; The vast knowledge he has at his command will be invaluable in the job. | be at somebody’s command : to be ready to obey somebody ; I'm at your command—what would you like me to do? | your wish is my command : used to say that you are ready to do whatever somebody asks you to do

Definition: a person who is in charge of something, especially an officer in charge of a particular group of soldiers or a military operation | an officer of fairly high rank in the British or American navy | (in England) a London police officer of high rank
Example: military/allied/field/flight commanders |  |

Definition: to begin to happen; to begin something

Definition: a spoken description of an event that is given while it is happening, especially on the radio or television | a written explanation or discussion of something such as a book or a play | a criticism or discussion of something
Example: a sports commentary | a critical commentary on the final speech of the play | The petty quarrels were a sad commentary on the state of the government.

Definition: a person who describes an event while it is happening, especially on television or radio | a person who is an expert on a particular subject and talks or writes about it on television or radio, in a newspaper or on social media
Example: a television/sports commentator | She's a political commentator for the BBC.

Definition: trade, especially between countries; the buying and selling of goods and services
Example: Leaders of industry and commerce met at the summit in Paris.

Definition: an official group of people who have been given responsibility to control something, or to find out about something, usually for the government | an amount of money that is paid to somebody for selling goods and that increases with the amount of goods that are sold | an amount of money that is charged by a bank, etc. for providing a particular service | a formal request to somebody to design or make a piece of work such as a building or a painting; the fact of making such a request | the position of an officer in the armed forces, typically with the rank of lieutenant or higher | the act of doing something wrong or illegal

Definition: a member of a commission (= an official group of people who are responsible for controlling something or finding out about something) | the head of a particular police force in some countries | the head of a government department in some countries | (in the US) an official chosen by a sports association to control it
Example: the Church Commissioners (= the group of people responsible for controlling the financial affairs of the Church of England) |  | the agriculture/health, etc. commissioner | the baseball commissioner

Definition: a promise to do something or to behave in a particular way; a promise to support somebody/something; the fact of committing yourself | the desire to work hard and give your energy and time to a job or an activity | a thing that you have promised or agreed to do, or that you have to do | a promise to pay for something, especially regularly; a promise to use resources in order to achieve something
Example: I'm not ready to make a long-term commitment . | He has demonstrated exceptional commitment and dedication. | He's busy for the next month with filming commitments. | Taking on a mortgage is a huge financial commitment .

Definition: a group of people who are chosen, usually by a larger group, to make decisions or to deal with a particular subject
Example: an executive/advisory/organizing committee

Definition: a product or a raw material that can be bought and sold | a thing that is useful or has a useful quality

Definition: usually; very often; by most people

Definition: a person or an animal that travels with you or spends a lot of time with you | a person who shares your experiences, especially when these are particularly pleasant or unpleasant | a person, usually a woman, employed to live with and help somebody, especially somebody old or ill | one of a pair of things that go together or can be used together | used in book titles to describe a book giving useful facts and information on a particular subject

Definition: similar to somebody/something else and able to be compared

Definition: a strong feeling of sympathy for people or animals who are suffering and a desire to help them

Definition: able to be used together | able to exist or be used together without causing problems | if two people are compatible , they can have a good relationship because they have similar ideas, interests, etc.
Example: The new system will be compatible with existing equipment. | Are measures to protect the environment compatible with economic growth? | She found a compatible partner on an online dating site.

Definition: to force somebody to do something; to make something necessary | to cause a particular reaction
Example: The law can compel fathers to make regular payments for their children. | He spoke with an authority that compelled the attention of the whole crowd.

Definition: that makes you pay attention to it because it is so interesting and exciting | so strong that you cannot resist it | that makes you think it is true
Example: Her latest book makes compelling reading. | a compelling need/desire | There is no compelling reason to believe him.

Definition: to provide something good to balance or reduce the bad effects of damage, loss, etc. | to act in order to balance or correct something wrong or not normal | to pay somebody money because they have suffered some damage, loss, injury, etc.
Example: Nothing can compensate for the loss of a loved one. | In the second experiment, the temperature was raised to compensate for this bias. | Her lawyers say she should be compensated for the suffering she had been caused.
Synonym: make up for | make up for

Definition: something, especially money, that somebody gives you because they have hurt you, or damaged something that you own; the act of giving this to somebody | money that an employee receives for doing their job | a thing or things that make a bad situation better
Example: to claim/award/receive compensation | Send your CV and current compensation to Executive Search Consultant. | I wish I were young again, but getting older has its compensations.

Definition: the ability to do something well | the power that a court, an organization or a person has to deal with something | a skill that you need in a particular job or for a particular task
Example: to gain a high level of competence in English | The judge has to act within the competence of the court. | The syllabus lists the knowledge and competences required at this level.

Definition: having enough skill or knowledge to do something well or to the necessary standard | of a good standard but not very good | having the power to decide something
Example: He's very competent in his work. | Ron was a competent player—more than that, he was good! | The case was referred to a competent authority.

Definition: to produce a book, list, report, etc. by bringing together different items, articles, songs, etc.; to collect information in order to produce a book, list, etc. | to translate instructions from one computer language into another so that a particular computer can understand them
Example: We are trying to compile a list of suitable people for the job. |

Definition: to add to something in a way that improves it or makes it more attractive | a thing that adds new qualities to something in a way that improves it or makes it more attractive | the complete number or quantity needed or allowed | a word or phrase, especially an adjective or a noun, that is used after linking verbs such as be and become , and describes the subject of the verb. In some descriptions of grammar it is used to refer to any word or phrase that is governed by a verb and usually comes after the verb in a sentence.
Example: The excellent menu is complemented by a good wine list. | This vegetable's natural sweetness is a perfect complement to salty or rich foods. | We've taken our full complement of trainees this year. | In the sentences ‘I'm angry’ and ‘He became a politician’, ‘angry’ and ‘politician’ are complements.

Definition: the act or process of finishing something; the state of being finished and complete | the formal act of completing the sale of property, for example the sale of a house

Definition: the state of being formed of many parts; the state of being difficult to understand | the features of a problem or situation that are difficult to understand

Definition: to give something, especially money or goods, to help achieve or provide something | to be one of the causes of something | to increase, improve or add to something | to write things for a newspaper, book, website, etc. or a radio or television programme; to speak during a meeting or conversation, especially to give your opinion

Definition: the act of taking goods, letters, etc. to the people they have been sent to | the act of making a service or information available to people | the process of giving birth to a baby | the way in which somebody speaks, sings a song, etc. in public | a ball that is thrown, especially in cricket or baseball

Definition: to show something clearly by giving proof or evidence | to show by your actions that you have a particular quality, feeling or opinion | to show and explain how something works or how to do something | to take part in a public meeting or march (= an organized walk by many people) , usually as a protest or to show support for something

Definition: the distance from the top or surface to the bottom of something; a particular distance down from the surface of something | the distance from the front to the back of something | the strength and power of feelings | qualities that give somebody/something extra character and make them/it interesting | the quality of knowing or understanding a lot of details about something; the ability to provide and explain these details | the deepest, most extreme or serious part of something | the strength of a colour | the quality in a work of art or a photograph that makes it appear not to be flat

Idioms: be out of your depth : to be in water that is too deep to stand in with your head above water ; If you can't swim, don't go out of your depth. ; I don't like going out of my depth in the sea. | be out of your depth : to be unable to understand something because it is too difficult; to be in a situation that you cannot control ; He felt totally out of his depth in his new job. ; The writer seems a little out of her depth when dealing with the emotional issue involved. | in depth : in a detailed, careful and complete way ; I haven't looked at the report in depth yet. ; an in-depth study ; I studied phonology in depth at college. | plumb the depths of something : to be or to experience an extreme example of something unpleasant ; His latest novel plumbs the depths of horror and violence. ; It was at that stage in her life when she plumbed the depths of despair. ; The team's poor performances plumbed new depths last night when they lost 10–2. ; The story plumbed the depths of tabloid journalism. | strength in depth : the quality of being strong because you have a great variety of good people, ideas, etc. that can contribute to the success of something ; The French team does not have the strength in depth that the Australians have.

Definition: a calm and serious manner that deserves respect | the fact of being given honour and respect by people | a sense of your own importance and value

Idioms: beneath your dignity : below what you see as your own importance or worth ; He clearly regarded manual work as beneath his dignity. | stand on your dignity : to demand to be treated with the respect that you think that you deserve ; Our teacher never stands on her dignity with us, but treats us all as friends and colleagues. ; She was never one to stand on her dignity.

Definition: a situation that makes problems, often one in which you have to make a very difficult choice between things of equal importance

Definition: to put something quickly into a liquid and take it out again | to go downwards or to a lower level; to make something do this | if you dip your headlights when driving a car at night, you make the light from them point down so that other drivers do not have the light in their eyes | when farmers dip animals, especially sheep, they put them in a bath of a liquid containing chemicals in order to kill insects, etc. | a quick swim | a decrease in the amount or success of something, usually for only a short period | a place where a surface suddenly drops to a lower level and then rises again | a thick mixture into which pieces of food are dipped before being eaten | a liquid containing a chemical into which sheep and other animals can be dipped in order to kill insects on them | a quick look at something | a quick movement of something down and up

Definition: to give somebody official permission to leave the police or the armed forces; to make somebody leave the police or the armed forces | to allow somebody to leave hospital because they are well enough to leave | to allow somebody to leave prison or court | to allow a member of a jury to stop serving in a court case | when a gas or a liquid discharges or is discharged , or somebody discharges it, it flows somewhere | if part of the body discharges something, a liquid such as pus comes out of it | to release force or power | to do everything that is necessary to perform and complete a particular duty | to fire a gun, etc.; (of a gun, etc.) to be fired | the action of releasing a substance such as a liquid or gas; a substance that comes out from inside somewhere | the release of electricity from a charged object; a flow of electricity through air or other gas | the act of officially allowing somebody, or of telling somebody, to leave the police or the army | the act of allowing somebody to leave hospital because they are well enough to leave | the act of allowing somebody to leave prison or court | the act of performing a task or a duty or of paying money that is owed | the action of firing a gun, etc.

Definition: to give somebody information about something, especially something that was previously secret | to allow something that was hidden to be seen

Definition: the act of making something known or public that was previously secret or private | information or a fact that is made known or public that was previously secret or private
Example: the newspaper’s disclosure of defence secrets | startling disclosures about his private life

Definition: to try to prevent something or to prevent somebody from doing something, especially by making it difficult to do or by showing that you do not approve of it | to make somebody feel less confident or enthusiastic about doing something
Example: a campaign to discourage investing in fossil fuel development | Don't be discouraged by the first failure—try again!

Definition: a long and serious treatment or discussion of a subject in speech or writing | the use of language in speech and writing in order to produce meaning; language that is studied, usually in order to see how the different parts of a text are connected
Example: a discourse on issues of gender and sexuality | spoken/written discourse

Definition: separate and different from other things of the same type
Example: The organisms can be divided into discrete categories.

Definition: the freedom or power to decide what should be done in a particular situation | care in what you say or do, in order to keep something secret or to avoid causing difficulty for somebody or making them feel embarrassed; the quality of being discreet
Example: I'll leave it up to you to use your discretion . | This is confidential, but I know that I can rely on your discretion.
Idioms: at somebody’s discretion : according to what somebody decides or wishes to do ; Bail is granted at the discretion of the court. ; There is no service charge and tipping is at your discretion. | discretion is the better part of valour : you should avoid danger and not take unnecessary risks
Synonym: good judgment | good sense | good judgment | good judgment

Definition: to recognize that there is a difference between people or things; to show a difference between people or things | to treat one person or group worse/better than another in an unfair way
Example: The computer program was unable to discriminate between letters and numbers. | practices that discriminate against women and in favour of men

Definition: the practice of treating somebody or a particular group in society less fairly than others | the ability to judge what is good, true, etc. | the ability to recognize a difference between one thing and another; a difference that is recognized
Example: age/racial/gender/sex discrimination (= because of somebody’s age, race, gender or sex) | He showed great discrimination in his choice of friends. | to learn discrimination between right and wrong

Definition: not honest; intending to trick people
Example: Beware of dishonest traders in the tourist areas.

Definition: to decide that somebody/something is not important and not worth thinking or talking about | to put thoughts or feelings out of your mind | to officially remove somebody from their job | to send somebody away or allow them to leave | to say that a trial or legal case should not continue, usually because there is not enough evidence | to end the innings of a player or team
Example: to dismiss a suggestion/a claim/an idea | Dismissing her fears, she climbed higher. | She claims she was unfairly dismissed from her post. | At 12 o'clock the class was dismissed. | The judge dismissed the case for lack of evidence. |

Definition: the act of dismissing somebody from their job; an example of this | the failure to consider something as important | the act of not allowing a trial or legal case to continue, usually because there is not enough evidence | the act of sending somebody away or allowing them to leave | the end of the innings of a player or team
Example: He still hopes to win his claim against unfair dismissal . | Her casual dismissal of the threats seemed irresponsible. | the dismissal of the appeal |  |

Definition: a condition or illness that causes problems with the way part of the body or brain works | an untidy state; a lack of order or organization | violent behaviour of large groups of people
Example: a blood/bowel disorder | The room was in a state of disorder. | an outbreak of rioting and public disorder

Definition: to take the place of somebody/something | to force people to move away from their home to another place | to move something from its usual position | to remove somebody from a job or position | to take the place of an amount of liquid when put or floating in it, used as a way of measuring size
Example: Gradually factory workers have been displaced by machines. | Around 10 000 people have been displaced by the fighting. | Check for roof tiles that have been displaced by the wind. | displaced workers | The ship displaces 58 000 tonnes.

Definition: to put something in a place where people can see it easily; to show something to people | to show a quality, feeling, skill or type of behaviour; to show signs of something | to show information | to show a special pattern of behaviour that is intended to attract a female bird or animal

Definition: the act of getting rid of something | the sale of part of a business, property, etc. | a machine connected to the waste pipe of a kitchen sink , for cutting food waste into small pieces
Example: The council is responsible for waste disposal and street cleaning. | The next step is to think about disposal of your assets. |
Idioms: at your/somebody’s disposal : available for use as you prefer/somebody prefers ; He will have a car at his disposal for the whole month. ; Well, I'm at your disposal (= I am ready to help you in any way I can) .

Definition: an argument between two people, groups or countries; discussion about a subject on which people disagree | to question whether something is true or legally or officially acceptable | to argue or disagree strongly with somebody about something, especially about who owns something | to fight to get control of something or to win something
Example: industrial/pay disputes | These figures have been disputed. | I tried to dispute the bill with the waiter. | On the last lap three runners were disputing the lead.

Definition: to make it difficult for something to continue in the normal way | to cause significant change in an industry or market by means of innovation (= new ideas or methods)
Example: Demonstrators succeeded in disrupting the meeting. | Our radical new printing technology is disrupting traditional manufacturing.

Definition: a situation in which it is difficult for something to continue in the normal way; the act of stopping something from continuing in the normal way | significant change to an industry or market due to innovation (= new ideas or methods) in technology
Example: We aim to help you move house with minimum disruption to yourself. | The banking industry is primed for major disruption.

Definition: to mix with a liquid and become part of it | to make a solid become part of a liquid | to officially end a marriage, business agreement or parliament | to disappear; to make something disappear | to suddenly start laughing, crying, etc. | to remove or destroy something, especially by a chemical process; to be destroyed in this way
Example: Salt dissolves in water. | Dissolve the tablet in water. | Their marriage was dissolved in 2019. | When the ambulance had gone, the crowd dissolved. | When the teacher looked up, the children dissolved into giggles. | a new detergent that dissolves stains
Synonym: melt | melt | vanish | melt | melt | end

Definition: far away in space or time | not like something else | related to you but not closely | not friendly; not wanting a close relationship with somebody | not paying attention to something but thinking about something completely different
Example: the distant sound of music | Their life seemed utterly distant from his own. | a distant cousin/aunt/relative | Pat sounded very cold and distant on the phone. | There was a distant look in her eyes; her mind was obviously on something else.
Idioms: the (dim and) distant past : a long time ago ; stories from the dim and distant past ; In the distant past this land was covered in forests. | in the not too distant future : not a long time in the future but fairly soon ; I hope we’ll see each other again in the not too distant future.
Synonym: far | remote | unfriendly | unfriendly | unfriendly | unfriendly | remote

Definition: easily or clearly heard, seen, felt, etc. | clearly different or of a different kind | used to emphasize that you think an idea or situation definitely exists and is important
Example: There was a distinct smell of gas. | The results of the survey fell into two distinct groups. | Being tall gave Tony a distinct advantage.

Definition: a clear difference or contrast especially between people or things that are similar or related | the act of separating people or things into different groups | the quality of being something that is special | the quality of being excellent or important | a special mark or award that is given to somebody, especially a student, for excellent work
Example: distinctions between traditional and modern societies | The new law makes no distinction between adults and children (= treats them equally) . | She had the distinction of being the first woman to fly the Atlantic. | a writer of distinction | Naomi got a distinction in maths.
Idioms: a distinction without a difference : a point that somebody wants to suggest is a difference when in fact it is not different at all ; His government did not brand them terrorists, but rather ‘an entity that supports terrorism’. That may prove to be a distinction without a difference.
Synonym: difference | difference | difference | difference | difference | difference

Definition: having a quality or characteristic that makes something different and easily noticed
Example: clothes with a distinctive style

Definition: to recognize the difference between two people or things | to be a characteristic that makes two people, animals or things different | to be able to see or hear something | to do something so well that people notice and admire you
Example: At what age are children able to distinguish between right and wrong? | What was it that distinguished her from her classmates? | I could not distinguish her words, but she sounded agitated. | She has already distinguished herself as an athlete.

Definition: to change the shape, appearance or sound of something so that it is strange or not clear | to change facts, ideas, etc. so that they are no longer correct or true
Example: a fairground mirror that distorts your shape | Newspapers are often guilty of distorting the truth.

Definition: to take somebody’s attention away from what they are trying to do
Example: You're distracting me from my work.

Definition: a feeling of great worry or unhappiness; great mental pain | severe problems caused by not having enough money, food, etc. | a situation in which a ship, plane, etc. is in danger or difficulty and needs help | a state of physical pain and effort, especially difficulty in breathing | to make somebody feel very worried or unhappy

Definition: to give things to a large number of people; to share something between a number of people or groups | to send goods to shops and businesses so that they can be sold | to spread something, or different parts of something, over an area
Example: The leaflets have been widely distributed . | Who distributes our products in the UK? | Make sure your weight is evenly distributed .

Definition: the way that something is spread or exists over a particular area or among a particular group of people | the act of giving or sharing something out among a number of people | the system of transporting and delivering a product
Example: They studied the geographical distribution of the disease. | the distribution of food and medicines | They have systems in place for marketing, sales and distribution.

Definition: an area of a country or town, especially one that has particular features | one of the areas that a country, town or state is divided into for purposes of organization, with official boundaries (= borders)
Example: the City of London’s financial district | a tax district

Definition: to interrupt somebody when they are trying to work, sleep, etc. | to make somebody worry | to move something or change its position | to make something function differently from normal
Example: I'm sorry to disturb you, but can I talk to you for a moment? | The letter shocked and disturbed me. | Don't disturb the papers on my desk. | During a magnetic storm, the earth's magnetic field is disturbed.

Definition: making you feel anxious and upset or shocked
Example: a disturbing piece of news

Definition: to jump into water with your head and arms going in first | to swim underwater wearing breathing equipment, collecting or looking at things | to go to a deeper level underwater | to go steeply down through the air | to move or jump quickly in a particular direction, especially to avoid something, to try to catch a ball, etc. | to fall deliberately when somebody tackles you | to fall suddenly | a jump into deep water with your head first and your arms in front of you | an act of going underwater and swimming there with special equipment | an act of suddenly flying downwards | a bar, music club, etc. that is cheap, and perhaps dark or dirty | a deliberate fall that a player makes when somebody tackles them
Example: We dived into the river to cool off. | to dive for pearls | The submarine dived to avoid being seen. | The seagulls soared then dived. | We heard an explosion and dived for cover (= got into a place where we would be protected) . |  | The share price dived from 75p to an all-time low of 50p. | a spectacular high dive (= from high above the water) | a dive to a depth of 18 metres | The pilot seemed to be having difficulty in pulling out of the dive. | The band played in every smoky dive in town. | Kane's dive won England a penalty.

Definition: very different from each other and of various kinds

Definition: a rough written version of something that is not yet in its final form | a written order to a bank to pay money to somebody | the practice of ordering people by law to join the armed forces | a system in which professional teams in some sports choose players each year from among college students | a flow of cool air in a room or other small space | one continuous action of swallowing liquid (= making it go down your throat) ; the amount swallowed | medicine in a liquid form

There's a draft in here. | He took a deep draft of his beer. | a sleeping draft (= one that makes you sleep)

Definition: to pull somebody/something along with effort and difficulty | to take hold of something and pull it | to move yourself slowly and with effort | to persuade somebody to come or go somewhere they do not really want to come or go to | to pass very slowly | to move, or make something move, partly touching the ground | to search the bottom of a river, lake, etc. with nets or hooks | to move some text, an icon , etc. across the screen of a computer using the mouse
Example: The sack is too heavy to lift—you’ll have to drag it. | Desperately, Jinny dragged at his arm. | I managed to drag myself out of bed. | I'm sorry to drag you all this way in the heat. | Time dragged terribly. | This dress is too long—it drags on the ground when I walk. | They dragged the canal for the murder weapon. | Click on the file and drag it across.
Idioms: drag your feet/heels : to be deliberately slow in doing something or in making a decision | drag somebody through the mud/dirt | drag somebody's name through the mud/dirt : to criticize or say bad things about somebody in public, in a way that is unfair ; The paper has dragged his name through the mud. ; They feel their agency has been dragged through the mud by the press. ; Football's good name is being dragged through the dirt. | (drag somebody) kicking and screaming : if you drag somebody kicking and screaming to do something, they only do it with great protests because they don't really want to do it at all ; The president had to be dragged kicking and screaming to the signing ceremony. | pull/drag yourself up by your (own) bootstraps : to improve your situation yourself, without help from other people

Definition: to make something empty or dry by removing the liquid from it; to become empty or dry in this way | to make liquid flow away from something; to flow away | to empty a cup or glass by drinking everything in it | to make somebody/something weaker, poorer, etc. by using up their/its strength, money, etc. | a pipe that carries away dirty water or other liquid waste | a frame of metal bars over the opening to a drain in the ground | a hole in a bath, sink , etc. where the water flows away and into which a plug fits | a thing that uses a lot of the time, money, etc. that could be used for something else | the fact of resources, people, etc. being lost or wasted
Example: Drain and rinse the pasta. | We had to drain the oil out of the engine. | In one gulp, he drained the glass. | My mother's hospital expenses were slowly draining my income. | We had to call in a plumber to unblock the drain. |  |  | Military spending is a huge drain on the country's resources. | the drain of talented staff to the United States
Idioms: (go) down the drain : (to be) wasted; (to get) very much worse ; It's just money down the drain, you know. ; Safety standards have gone down the drain. | laugh like a drain : to laugh very loudly

Definition: to move along smoothly and slowly in water or air | to move or go somewhere slowly | to do something, happen or change without a particular plan or purpose | to go from one situation or state to another without realizing it | to be blown into large piles by the wind | to make something float somewhere | a slow steady movement from one place to another; a slow change or development from one situation to another, especially to something bad | the movement of a ship or plane away from its direction because of currents or wind | the movement of the sea or air | a large pile of something, especially snow, made by the wind | a large mass of something, especially flowers | the general meaning of what somebody says or writes

Definition: having a lot of energy and a strong personality | always changing and making progress | producing movement | describing an action rather than a state. Dynamic verbs (for example eat , grow , knock , die ) can be used in the progressive tenses. | the way in which people or things behave and react to each other in a particular situation | the science of the forces involved in movement | a force that produces change, action or effects | changes in volume in music
Example: a dynamic leader | The business has managed to change and remain dynamic. |  |  | the dynamics of political change | fluid dynamics |  | They play all the notes in the right place and have a scrupulous regard for the tempos and dynamics.

Definition: very interested and excited by something that is going to happen or about something that you want to do; showing this

Definition: the money that you earn for the work that you do | the profit that a company makes

Definition: lack of difficulty | the state of feeling relaxed or comfortable without worries, problems or pain | to become less unpleasant, painful or severe; to make something less unpleasant, etc. | to make something easier | to move slowly and carefully; to move somebody/something slowly and carefully | to make something less tight and more relaxed; to become less tight and more relaxed | to become or make something lower in price or value
Example: He passed the exam with ease. | In his retirement, he lived a life of ease. | The pain immediately eased. | Ramps have been built to ease access for people who use wheelchairs. | He eased slowly forwards. | Ease your grip on the wheel a little. | Share prices eased back from yesterday's levels.
Idioms: (stand) at ease : used as a command to soldiers to tell them to stand with their feet apart and their hands behind their backs | at (your) ease : relaxed and confident and not nervous or embarrassed ; I never feel completely at ease with him. ; She was at her ease straight away in the new job. | ill at ease : feeling uncomfortable and embarrassed ; I felt ill at ease in such formal clothes. | put somebody at (their) ease : to make somebody feel relaxed and confident, not nervous or embarrassed ; Try to put the candidate at ease by being friendly and informal. | put/set somebody’s mind at ease/rest : to do or say something to make somebody stop worrying about something
Synonym: comfort | comfort | easiness | naturalness | comfort | naturalness | comfort

Definition: if a sound echoes , it is reflected off a wall, the side of a mountain, etc. so that you can hear it again | to send back and repeat a sound; to be full of a sound | to repeat an idea or opinion because you agree with it | to repeat what somebody else has just said, especially because you find it surprising | the reflecting of sound off a wall or inside a particular space so that a noise appears to be repeated; a sound that is reflected back in this way | the fact of an idea, event, etc. being like another and reminding you of it; something that reminds you of something else | an opinion or attitude that agrees with or repeats one already expressed or thought
Example: Her footsteps echoed in the empty room. | The whole house echoed. | ‘More police, that’s what we need,’ he said, echoing his father’s views on the subject. | ‘He's gone!’ Viv echoed. | There was an echo on the phone and I couldn't hear clearly. | Yesterday's crash has grim echoes of previous disasters. | His words were an echo of what she had heard many times before.

Definition: connected with the relation of plants and living creatures to each other and to their environment | interested in and concerned about the ecology of a place

Definition: in a good and careful way, with no waste of time, money or energy

Definition: to get onto a ship or plane; to put somebody/something onto a ship or plane
