Definition: a person or thing that is not included in a general statement | a thing that does not follow a rule
Example: Most of the buildings in the town are modern, but the church is an exception. | Good writing is unfortunately the exception rather than the rule (= it is unusual) .
Idioms: the exception that proves the rule : people say that something is the exception that proves the rule when they are stating something that seems to be different from the normal situation, but they mean that the normal situation remains true in general ; Most electronics companies have not done well this year, but ours is the exception that proves the rule. | make an exception : to allow somebody not to follow the usual rule on one occasion or for one particular reason ; Children are not usually allowed in, but I'm prepared to make an exception in this case. ; No parking is allowed, but an exception is made for disabled drivers. ; We are making a special exception for Emma because of her condition. | take exception to something : to object strongly to something; to be angry about something ; I take great exception to the fact that you told my wife before you told me. ; No one could possibly take exception to his comments. | with the exception of somebody/something : except; not including ; All his novels are set in Italy with the exception of his last. ; The whole of the island was flooded with the exception of a small area in the north. | without exception : used to emphasize that the statement you are making is always true and everyone or everything is included ; All students without exception must take the English examination. ; Without exception, all employees must carry their identity card with them at all times.
Synonym: exclusion | deviation | deviation | deviation | exclusion | exclusion | exclusion

Definition: unusually good | very unusual
Example: At the age of five he showed exceptional talent as a musician. | This deadline will be extended only in exceptional circumstances .

Definition: more than is necessary, reasonable or acceptable | an amount by which something is larger than something else | the part of an insurance claim that a person has to pay while the insurance company pays the rest | the fact of doing too much of something, especially eating or drinking | extreme behaviour that is unacceptable, illegal or morally wrong | in addition to an amount that is necessary, usual or legal | required as extra payment
Example: You can throw away any excess. | We cover costs up to £600 and then you pay the excess. | There is an excess of £100 on each claim under this policy. | He gave in to bouts of alcoholic excess. | We need a free press to curb government excesses. | Excess food is stored as fat. | The full excess fare had to be paid.

Definition: greater than what seems reasonable or appropriate
Example: They complained about the excessive noise coming from the upstairs flat.

Definition: to deliberately not include something in what you are doing or considering | to prevent somebody/something from entering a place or taking part in something | to decide that something is not possible
Example: The cost of borrowing has been excluded from the inflation figures. | Women are still excluded from some London clubs. | We should not exclude the possibility of negotiation.
Synonym: keep out | keep out | keep out

Definition: the act of preventing somebody/something from entering a place or taking part in something | a person or thing that is not included in something | the act of deciding that something is not possible | a situation in which a child is banned from attending school because of bad behaviour
Example: He was disappointed with his exclusion from the England squad. | Check the list of exclusions in the insurance policy. | the exclusion of robbery as a motive | the exclusion of disruptive students from school
Synonym: keeping out | keeping out | keeping out | keeping out

Definition: only to be used by one particular person or group; only given to one particular person or group | not very willing to allow new people to become members, especially if they are from a lower social class | of a high quality and expensive and therefore not often bought or used by most people | not including anything else | not including somebody/something | not able to exist or be a true statement at the same time as something else | an item of news or a story about famous people that is published in only one newspaper or magazine or broadcast on only one TV or radio station
Example: The hotel has exclusive access to the beach. | He belongs to an exclusive club. | an exclusive hotel | He had an exclusive focus on success and making money. | The price is for accommodation only, exclusive of meals. | The two options are not mutually exclusive (= you can have them both) . | We feature an exclusive on the mothers whose babies were swapped at birth.
Synonym: expensive | select | expensive | expensive | expensive | expensive

Definition: for only one particular person, group or use | completely; without including anybody or anything else
Example: The resort caters almost exclusively for a high-society public. | a charity that relies almost exclusively on voluntary contributions

Definition: a reason, either true or invented, that you give to explain or defend your behaviour | a good reason that you give for doing something that you want to do for other reasons | a very bad example of something | a note written by a parent or doctor to explain why a student cannot go to school or somebody cannot go to work
Example: Late again! What's your excuse this time? | Eating ice cream for a sore throat isn't really a cure, but I like it, so any excuse ! | Why get involved with that pathetic excuse for a human being? |

Tags: Oxford_5000 formal law War_and_conflict Crime_and_punishment Art
Definition: to kill somebody, especially as a legal punishment | to do a piece of work, perform a duty, put a plan into action, etc. | to successfully perform an action or movement that requires skill | to make or produce a work of art | to follow the instructions in a legal document; to make a document legally acceptable
Example: He was executed for treason. | They drew up and executed a plan to reduce fuel consumption. | The pilot executed a perfect landing. | Picasso also executed several landscapes at Horta de San Juan. | His will was executed by his lawyers in 2008.
Synonym: put to death | perform | perform | perform | carry out

Definition: the act of killing somebody, especially as a legal punishment | the act of doing a piece of work, performing a duty, or putting a plan into action | skill in performing or making something, such as a piece of music or work of art | the act of following the instructions in a legal document, especially those in somebody’s will
Example: He faced execution by hanging for murder. | He had failed in the execution of his duty. | Her execution of the piano piece was perfect. | The solicitors are proceeding with the execution of her mother’s will.
Idioms: a stay of execution : a delay in following the order of a court ; to grant a stay of execution ; There will be no stay of execution and few mourners for this concrete tower block, due to be demolished next month.
Synonym: putting to death | performance | carrying out | performance | carrying out

Definition: a person who has an important job as a manager of a company or an organization | a group of people who run a company or an organization | the part of a government responsible for putting laws into effect
Example: company/corporate/business/industry executives | The union’s executive has/have yet to reach a decision. | Conservatives are by tradition believers in a strong executive.

Definition: to use power or influence to affect somebody/something | to make a big physical or mental effort
Example: He exerted all his authority to make them accept the plan. | In order to be successful he would have to exert himself.

Definition: to show something in a public place for people to enjoy or to give them information | to show clearly that you have or feel a particular quality, ability, feeling or symptom | an object or a collection of objects put in a public place, for example a museum, so that people can see it | a collection of things, for example works of art, that are shown to the public | the act of showing something, for example works of art, to the public | a thing that is used in court to prove that somebody is guilty or not guilty
Example: They will be exhibiting their new designs at the trade fairs. | The patient exhibited signs of fatigue and memory loss. | The museum contains some interesting exhibits on Spanish rural life. | The new exhibit will tour a dozen US cities next year. | She refused to allow the exhibit of her husband's work. | The first exhibit was a knife which the prosecution claimed was the murder weapon.

Definition: the state of being sent to live in another country that is not your own, especially for political reasons or as a punishment | a person who chooses, or is forced, to live away from his or her own country | to force somebody to leave their country, especially for political reasons or as a punishment; to send somebody into exile
Example: a place of exile | political exiles | He was exiled to the island of St Helena.

Definition: the state or fact of being real or living or of being present | a way of living, especially when this is difficult or boring
Example: I was unaware of his existence until today. | The family endured a miserable existence in a cramped apartment.

Definition: a way out of a public building or vehicle | a place where vehicles can leave a road to join another road | an act of leaving, especially of an actor from the stage | the fact of leaving a particular situation or activity | to go out; to leave a building, stage, vehicle, etc. | to finish using a computer program | used in the instructions printed in a play to say that an actor must leave the stage
Example: Where's the exit? | Leave the roundabout at the second exit. | The heroine made her exit to great applause. | They were disappointed by the team's early exit from the World Cup. | We exited through a corridor into a back alley. | To exit from this page, press the return key. | Exit Macbeth.
Synonym: way out | leave | way out | way out

Definition: from or in another country, especially a tropical one; seeming exciting and unusual because it seems to be connected with other countries
Example: brightly-coloured exotic flowers/plants/birds

Definition: an act of increasing or making something increase in size, amount or importance
Example: a period of rapid economic expansion

Definition: a belief that something will happen because it is likely | a hope that something good will happen | a strong belief about the way something should happen or how somebody should behave
Example: We are confident in our expectation of a full recovery. | She went to college with great expectations. | Unfortunately the new software has failed to meet expectations .

Definition: the act of spending or using money; an amount of money spent | the use of energy, time, materials, etc.
Example: a reduction in public/government/military expenditure | the expenditure of emotion

Definition: the money that you spend on something | something that makes you spend money | money spent in doing a particular job, or for a particular purpose | money that you spend while you are working and that your employer will pay back to you later
Example: The garden was transformed at great expense . | Running a car is a big expense. | living/medical/travel expenses | You can claim back your travelling/travel expenses.
Idioms: at somebody’s expense : paid for by somebody ; We were taken out for a meal at the company's expense. | at somebody’s expense : if you make a joke at somebody’s expense , you laugh at them and make them feel silly | at the expense of somebody/something : with loss or damage to somebody/something ; He built up the business at the expense of his health. ; an education system that benefits the brightest children at the expense of everyone else | go to the expense of something/of doing something | go to a lot of, etc. expense : to spend money on something ; They went to all the expense of redecorating the house and then they moved. | put somebody to the expense of something/of doing something | put somebody to a lot of, etc. expense : to make somebody spend money on something ; Their visit put us to a lot of expense.

Definition: based on new ideas, forms or methods that are used to find out what effect they have | connected with scientific experiments
Example: The school's experimental teaching methods include letting the children decide what to study. | experimental conditions/data/evidence

Definition: expert knowledge or skill in a particular subject, activity or job
Example: professional/scientific/technical, etc. expertise

Definition: to be no longer legally acceptable because the period of time for which it could be used has ended | to end | to die
Example: When does your driving licence expire? | His term of office expires at the end of June. | She had expired while bearing her son.
Synonym: come to an end | come to an end | die

Definition: clear and easy to understand, so that you have no doubt what is meant | saying something clearly, exactly and openly | describing or showing something, especially sexual activity, with a lot of detail
Example: He gave me very explicit directions on how to get there. | She was quite explicit about why she had left. | a sexually explicit film

Definition: clearly or directly, so that the meaning is easy to understand | with a lot of detail, especially in describing or showing sexual activity
Example: The report states explicitly that the system was to blame. | the explicitly sexual content of the image

Definition: to treat a person or situation as an opportunity to gain an advantage for yourself | to treat somebody unfairly by making them work and not giving them much in return | to use something well in order to gain as much from it as possible | to develop or use something for business or industry | a brave, exciting or interesting act
Example: He exploited his father's name to get himself a job. | What is being done to stop employers from exploiting young people? | She fully exploits the humour of her role in the play. | No minerals have yet been exploited in Antarctica. | the daring exploits of Roman heroes

Definition: a situation in which somebody treats somebody else in an unfair way, especially in order to make money from their work | the use of land, oil, minerals, etc. | the fact of using a situation in order to get an advantage for yourself
Example: the exploitation of children | commercial exploitation of the mineral resources in Antarctica | exploitation of the situation for his own purposes

Definition: the act of travelling through a place in order to find out about it or look for something in it | an examination of something in order to find out about it
Example: Budgets for space exploration have been cut back. | These findings merit further exploration.

Definition: easily able or likely to explode | likely to cause violence or strong feelings of anger or hate | often having sudden violent or angry feelings | increasing suddenly and rapidly | sudden and loud | a substance that is able or likely to cause an explosion
Example: an explosive device (= a bomb) | one of the most explosive issues of our time | an explosive temper | the explosive growth of the export market | There was an explosive crash and then everything fell silent. | plastic explosives
Synonym: liable to explode | loud | loud | loud | loud

Definition: to show something that is usually hidden | to tell the true facts about a person or a situation, and show them/it to be dishonest, illegal, etc. | to put somebody/something in a place or situation where they are not protected from something harmful or unpleasant | to let somebody find out about something by giving them experience of it or showing them what it is like | to allow light into a camera when taking a photograph | a person who exposes himself or herself, shows their sexual organs in public in a way that is offensive to other people | an account of the facts of a situation, especially when these shock people or have deliberately been kept secret
Example: He smiled suddenly, exposing a set of amazingly white teeth. | He threatened to expose the racism that existed within the police force. | This tactic allowed the rebels to attack without the risk of exposing themselves. | We want to expose the kids to as much art and culture as possible. |  |  | a damning exposé of police corruption
Synonym: reveal | bare | familiarize with | disclose | disclose | bare

Definition: the state of being in a place or situation where there is no protection from something harmful or unpleasant | the fact of having the true facts about somebody/something told after they have been hidden because they are bad, dishonest or illegal | the fact of being discussed or mentioned on television, in newspapers, etc. | the fact of experiencing something new or different | a medical condition caused by being out in very cold weather for too long without protection | the length of time for which light is allowed into the camera when taking a photograph | a length of film in a camera that is used to take a photograph | the act of showing something that is usually hidden
Example: prolonged exposure to harmful radiation | his exposure as a liar and a fraud | Her new movie has had a lot of exposure in the media. | We try to give our children exposure to other cultures. | Two climbers were brought in suffering from exposure. | I used a long exposure for this one. | There were three exposures left on the roll of film. |
Synonym: outlook | publicity | disclosure | disclosure | disclosure | disclosure | outlook | disclosure

Definition: to make something longer or larger | to make something last longer | to make a business, an idea, an influence, etc. cover more areas or operate in more places | to relate to or include somebody/something | to cover a particular area, distance or length of time | to make something reach something or stretch | to stretch part of your body, especially an arm or a leg, away from yourself | to offer or give something to somebody | to make somebody/something use all their effort, abilities, supplies, etc.
Example: to extend a fence/road/house | to extend a deadline/visa | The school is extending the range of subjects taught. | The offer does not extend to employees' partners. | Our land extends as far as the river. | to extend a rope between two posts | He extended his hand to (= offered to shake hands with) the new employee. | I'm sure you will join me in extending a very warm welcome to our visitors. | Jim didn't really have to extend himself in the exam.

Definition: the act of increasing the area of activity, group of people, etc. that is affected by something | a new room or rooms that are added to a house | a new part that is added to a building | an extra period of time allowed for something | a phone line with its own number, connected to a switchboard in a large building | the act of making something longer or larger; the thing that is made longer and larger | pieces of artificial hair that are added to somebody's own hair to make it longer | a part of a college or university that offers courses to students who are not studying full-time ; a programme of study for these students | the set of three letters that are placed after the dot (= small round mark) at the end of the name of a file and that show what type of file it is | an extra length of electric wire, used when the wire on an electrical device is not long enough
Example: the extension of new technology into healthcare | They're building an extension to their house. | a planned two-storey extension to the hospital | He's been granted an extension of the contract for another year. | What's your extension number? | The extension of the subway will take several months. | These extensions are very easy to use and won't damage your own hair. | La Salle Extension University | The extension .doc indicates a word-processing file. |
Idioms: by extension : taking the argument or situation one stage further ; The blame lies with the teachers and, by extension, with the Education Service.

Definition: covering a large area; great in amount | including or dealing with a wide range of information
Example: The house has extensive grounds. | Extensive research has been done into this disease.

Definition: in a way that covers a large area | in a way that includes or deals with a wide range of information | to a large degree; a great amount
Example: She has travelled extensively. | He has written extensively on contemporary art. | a spice used extensively in Eastern cooking

Definition: how large, important, serious, etc. something is | the physical size of an area
Example: It is difficult to assess the full extent of the damage. | You can't see the full extent of the beach from here.
Idioms: to… extent : used to show how far something is true or how great an effect it has ; To a certain extent , we are all responsible for this tragic situation. ; He had changed to such an extent (= so much) that I no longer recognized him. ; To some extent what she argues is true. ; The pollution of the forest has seriously affected plant life and, to a lesser extent , wildlife. ; To what extent is this true of all schools? ; The book discusses the extent to which (= how much) family life has changed over the past 50 years.

Definition: connected with or located on the outside of something/somebody | happening or coming from outside a place, an organization, your particular situation, etc. | coming from or arranged by somebody from outside a school, a university or an organization | connected with foreign countries
Example: the external walls of the building | A combination of internal and external factors caused the company to close down. | external examiners/assessors | The government is committed to reducing the country's external debt.

Definition: a short passage from a book, piece of music, etc. that gives you an idea of what the whole thing is like | a substance that has been obtained from something else using a particular process | to remove or obtain a substance from something, for example by using an industrial or a chemical process | to obtain information, money, etc., often by taking it from somebody who is unwilling to give it | to choose information, etc. from a book, a computer, etc. to be used for a particular purpose | to take or pull something out, especially when this needs force or effort | to get a particular feeling or quality from a situation
Example: The following extract is taken from her new novel. | yeast extract | a machine that extracts excess moisture from the air | Journalists managed to extract all kinds of information about her private life. | This article is extracted from his new book. | The dentist may decide that the wisdom teeth need to be extracted. | They are unlikely to extract much benefit from the trip.

Definition: unexpected, surprising or strange | not normal or ordinary; greater or better than usual | arranged for a special purpose and happening in addition to what normally or regularly happens | employed for a special purpose in addition to the usual staff
Example: What an extraordinary thing to say! | an extraordinary achievement | An extraordinary meeting was held to discuss the problem. | an envoy extraordinary

Definition: a person whose opinions, especially about religion or politics, are extreme, and who may do things that are violent or illegal for what they believe | connected with or belonging to people whose opinions, especially about religion or politics, are extreme, and who may do things that are violent or illegal for what they believe
Example: left-wing/right-wing/political/religious extremists | extremist groups

Definition: material made by weaving wool, cotton, silk, etc., used for making clothes, curtains, etc. and for covering furniture | the basic structure of a society, an organization, etc. that enables it to function successfully | the basic structure of a building, such as the walls, floor and roof

Definition: extremely good | very great | appearing in fables

Definition: to make an action or a process possible or easier
Example: The new trade agreement should facilitate more rapid economic growth.

Definition: buildings, services, equipment, etc. that are provided for a particular purpose | a place, usually including buildings, used for a particular purpose or activity | a special feature of a machine, service, etc. that makes it possible to do something extra | a natural ability to learn or do something easily
Example: leisure/sports facilities | the world’s largest nuclear waste facility | a bank account with an overdraft facility | She has a facility for languages.

Definition: a small group of people within a larger one, whose members have some different aims and beliefs to those of the larger group | opposition, disagreement , etc. that exists between small groups of people within an organization or political party | films, books, etc. that combine fact with fiction (= imaginary events)
Example: There are rival factions within the administration. | a party divided by faction and intrigue |

Definition: a department or group of related departments in a college or university | all the teachers in a faculty of a college or university | all the teachers of a particular university or college | any of the physical or mental abilities that a person is born with | a particular ability for doing something
Example: the Faculty of Law | the Law School faculty | faculty members | the faculty of sight | the faculty of understanding complex issues

Definition: to become or to make something become paler or less bright | to disappear gradually | if a sports player, team, actor, etc. fades , they stop playing or performing as well as they did before
Example: The curtains had faded in the sun. | Her smile faded. | Black faded on the final bend.
Idioms: be fading fast : to be disappearing quickly ; Hopes of a peace settlement were fading fast . | blend/fade into the woodwork : to behave in a way that does not attract any attention; to disappear or hide

Definition: not successful
Example: a failed writer

Definition: lack of success in doing or achieving something | a person or thing that is not successful | an act of not doing something, especially something that you are expected to do | the state of not working correctly or as expected; an occasion when this happens | a situation in which a business has to close because it is not successful | a situation in which crops do not grow correctly and do not produce food
Example: The success or failure of the plan depends on you. | The whole thing was a complete failure . | Failure to comply with the regulations will result in prosecution. | patients suffering from heart/kidney/liver failure | There has been an alarming increase in business failures. | Bad weather has resulted in crop failure.

Definition: the quality of treating people equally or in a way that is reasonable | a pale colour of skin or hair
Example: The fairness of the judicial system is being questioned. | A tan emphasized the fairness of her hair.
Idioms: in (all) fairness (to somebody) : used to introduce a statement that defends somebody who has just been criticized, or that explains another statement that may seem unreasonable ; In all fairness to him, he did try to stop her leaving. ; I have in fairness to say that, as yet, there is no hard evidence to support these charges. ; In all fairness to him, I should say that most of his story is true.

Definition: trust in somebody’s ability or knowledge; trust that somebody/something will do what has been promised | strong religious belief | a particular religion | the intention to do something right/wrong
Example: If the company can retain its customers' faith, it could become the market leader. | to have faith | the Christian/Catholic/Islamic/Muslim/Jewish faith | They handed over the weapons as a gesture of good faith.
Idioms: break/keep faith with somebody : to break/keep a promise that you have made to somebody; to stop/continue supporting somebody ; As club manager he was not prepared to keep faith with the players who had failed him. | in bad faith : knowing that what you are doing is wrong ; They had entered into the contract in bad faith. | in good faith : believing that what you are doing is right; believing that something is correct ; We printed the report in good faith but have now learnt that it was incorrect. ; He bought the painting in good faith (= he did not know that it had been stolen) . | a leap of faith : a belief in something that is not known or has not been done before ; These reforms are totally untested and will require a leap of faith on the part of teachers. | pin your faith on somebody/something : to rely on somebody/something completely for success or help ; She did not pin much faith on their chances of success.

Definition: not what somebody claims it is; appearing to be something it is not | made to look like something else | an object such as a work of art, a coin or a piece of jewellery that is not what somebody claims it is but has been made to look as if it is | a person who pretends to be what they are not in order to cheat people | to make something false appear to be real, especially in order to cheat somebody | to pretend to have a particular feeling, illness, etc.
Example: There were a few stalls selling fake designer clothing. | a fake fur jacket | All the paintings proved to be fakes. |  | She faked her mother's signature on the document. | She's not really sick—she's just faking it .

Definition: the state of being known and talked about by many people
Example: to achieve/win instant fame
Idioms: fifteen minutes of fame : a short period of being famous ; Everybody wants their fifteen minutes of fame. | shoot to fame/stardom : to suddenly become famous, especially as a singer or actor ; He shot to fame almost overnight. ; She shot to stardom in a Broadway musical.

Definition: a pleasant situation that you imagine but that is unlikely to happen | a product of your imagination | the act of imagining things; a person’s imagination | a type of story that is set in a world, or a version of our world, that does not really exist and involves magic, monsters , etc. | a competition in which you choose players to make your own imaginary team, and score points according to the performance of the real players
Example: He spoke of his childhood fantasies about becoming a famous football player. | Her books are usually escapist fantasies. | This is a work of fantasy. | She wrote a series of fantasy novels filled with wizards, witches and dragons. | In his personal time, Stephen blogs for several fantasy hockey leagues .

Definition: the money that you pay to travel by bus, plane, taxi, etc. | a passenger in a taxi | a range of food of a particular type | something that is offered to the public, especially as a form of entertainment
Example: bus/taxi fares | The taxi driver picked up a fare at the station. | The restaurant provides good traditional fare. | This movie is perfect family fare.

Definition: causing or ending in death | causing disaster or failure
Example: a fatal accident/blow/illness | a fatal error/mistake

Definition: the things, especially bad things, that will happen or have happened to somebody/something | the power that is believed to control everything that happens and that cannot be stopped or changed
Example: The fate of the three men is unknown. | Fate was kind to me that day.
Idioms: a fate worse than death : a terrible thing that could happen ; At the last minute she was saved from a fate worse than death. | tempt fate/providence : to do something too confidently in a way that might mean that your good luck will come to an end ; She felt it would be tempting fate to try the difficult climb a second time. ; It would be tempting fate to say that we will definitely win the game.

Definition: the responsibility for something wrong that has happened or been done | a bad or weak aspect of somebody’s character | something that is wrong or not perfect; something that is wrong with a machine or system that stops it from working correctly | a mistake made when serving | a place where there is a break that is longer than usual in the layers of rock in the earth’s crust
Example: Why should I say sorry when it's not my fault ? | He's proud of his children and blind to their faults. | The book's virtues far outweigh its faults. | He has served a number of double faults in this set. | the San Andreas fault
Idioms: find fault (with somebody/something) : to look for and discover mistakes in somebody/something; to complain about somebody/something ; My mother did nothing but find fault with my manners. ; My boss finds fault with everything I do. | to a fault : used to say that somebody has a lot, or even too much, of a particular good quality ; She is generous to a fault.

Definition: making people have a good opinion of somebody/something | positive and showing your good opinion of somebody/something | expressing your agreement; giving permission | good for something and making it likely to be successful or have an advantage | fairly good and not too expensive | blowing in the direction of travel
Example: She made a favourable impression in the interview. | The performance drew a lot of favourable comments from reviewers. | Their demands received a favourable response. | favourable economic conditions | They offered me a loan on very favourable terms . | They could not sail without a favourable wind.

Definition: an action or a piece of work that needs skill, strength or courage

Definition: one of the many soft light parts covering a bird’s body
Example: a peacock feather

Definition: having a system of government in which the individual states of a country have control over their own affairs, but are controlled by a central government for national decisions, etc. | (within a federal system , for example the US and Canada) connected with national government rather than the local government of an individual state

Definition: an amount of money that you pay for professional advice or services | an amount of money that you pay to join an organization, or to do something

Definition: advice, criticism or information about how good or useful something or somebody’s work is | the unpleasant noise produced by electrical equipment such as an amplifier when some of the power returns to the system

Definition: used to describe somebody who is the same as you in some way, or in the same situation

Definition: having or based on the belief that women should have the same rights and opportunities as men | a person who supports the belief that women should have the same rights and opportunities as men

Example: He has a high fever. | She caught a fever on her travels in Africa, and died. | He waited for her arrival in a fever of impatience. | Election fever suddenly gripped the nation.

Definition: the part of food that helps to keep a person healthy by keeping the bowels working and moving other food quickly through the body | a material such as cloth or rope that is made from a mass of natural or artificial threads | one of the many thin threads that form body tissue , such as muscle, and natural materials, such as wood and cotton

Definition: angry and aggressive in a way that is frightening | showing strong feelings or a lot of activity, often in a way that is violent | very strong in a way that could cause damage

Definition: a device containing paper, sand, chemicals, etc. that a liquid or gas is passed through in order to remove any materials that are not wanted | a device that allows only particular types of light or sound to pass through it | a program that processes information to leave out the types that are not wanted, or that stops particular types of electronic information, email, etc. from being sent to a computer | a tool on digital cameras, photo apps, etc. that allows you to change the appearance of an image | a light on a set of traffic lights showing that traffic can turn left (or right) while traffic that wants to go straight ahead or turn right (or left) must wait | to pass liquid, light, etc. through a special device, especially to remove something that is not wanted | to use a special program to check or sort the content of emails, websites or apps | to move slowly in a particular direction | to slowly become known | to come into a place slowly or in small amounts | to turn left (or right) at traffic lights while other vehicles wanting to go straight ahead or turn right (or left) must wait

Idioms: act/play the fool : to behave in a stupid way in order to make people laugh, especially in a way that may also annoy them ; Quit playing the fool and get some work done! | any fool can/could… : used to say that something is very easy to do ; Any fool could tell she was lying. | be no/nobody’s fool : to be too intelligent or know too much about something to be tricked by other people ; She's nobody's fool when it comes to dealing with difficult patients. ; She's nobody's fool. Example: It can take up to 72 hours for food to pass through the gut. | I'll only cook fish if the guts have been removed. | He had a bit of a gut on him, but otherwise he was quite skinny. | He doesn't have the guts to walk away from a well-paid job. | I had a feeling in my guts that something was wrong. | the guts of the problem |  | The hotel was completely gutted by fire last year. | The fish was already cleaned and gutted. | a gut feeling/reaction
Idioms: bust a gut (doing something/to do something) : to make a great effort to do something ; It’s a problem which nobody is going to bust a gut trying to solve. ; I busted my chops to get into law school. ; These guys were busting their butts on the field, trying to excel at athletics. | go with your gut : to make a decision based on something that you are certain about, even though you do not have any direct proof and cannot explain why you are certain ; I went with my gut and turned down the job. | greedy guts : used to refer to somebody who eats too much ; Make sure greedy guts doesn’t eat all the cake. | hate somebody’s guts : to dislike somebody very much | have somebody’s guts for garters : to be very angry with somebody and punish them severely for something they have done ; She’ll have your guts for garters if she ever finds out. | slog/sweat/work your guts out : to work very hard to achieve something ; I slogged my guts out for the exam. | spill your guts (to somebody) : to tell somebody everything you know or feel about something, because you are upset ; He has spilled his guts in his new autobiography.

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_British_English especially_North_American_English North_American_English The_environment Animals
Definition: the place where a particular type of animal or plant is normally found
Example: The panda's natural habitat is the bamboo forest.

Definition: to describe somebody/something as being very good or special, especially in newspapers, etc. | to signal to a taxi or a bus, in order to get the driver to stop | to call to somebody in order to say hello to them or attract their attention | when it hails , small balls of ice fall like rain from the sky | small balls of ice that fall like rain | a large number or amount of something that is aimed at somebody in order to harm them
Example: The conference was hailed as a great success. | to hail a taxi/cab | A voice hailed us from the other side of the street. | It's hailing! | We drove through hail and snow. | a hail of arrows/bullets

Definition: to stop; to make somebody/something stop | an act of stopping the movement or progress of somebody/something | a small train station in the country that has a platform but no buildings
Example: She walked towards him and then halted. | Work came to a halt when the machine broke down. |
Idioms: stop/halt somebody in their tracks | stop/halt/freeze in your tracks : to suddenly make somebody stop by frightening or surprising them; to suddenly stop because something has frightened or surprised you ; The question stopped Alice in her tracks. ; Suddenly he stopped dead in his tracks: what was he doing? ; The disease was stopped in its tracks by immunization programmes. | bring something to a grinding halt : to make something gradually go slower until it stops completely ; Roadworks brought traffic to a grinding halt. | grind to a halt | come to a grinding halt : to go slower gradually and then stop completely ; Production ground to a halt during the strike. ; Her career ground to a halt when the twins were born.

Definition: the amount of something that can be held in one hand | a small number of people or things | a person or an animal that is difficult to control
Example: a handful of rice | Only a handful of people came. | Her children can be a real handful.

Definition: to deal with a situation, a person, an area of work or a strong emotion | to touch, hold or move something with your hands | to control a vehicle, an animal, a tool, etc. | to be easy/difficult to drive or control | to buy or sell something | the part of a door, window, etc. that you use to open it | the part of an object, such as a cup, a bag or a tool, that you use to hold it or carry it | having the number or type of handle mentioned | a name or nickname | a person's username on a social media site or online forum
Example: A new man was appointed to handle the crisis. | Our cat hates being handled. | I wasn't sure if I could handle such a powerful car. | The car handles well in any weather. | They were arrested for handling stolen goods . | She turned the handle and opened the door. | the handle of a knife | a long-handled spoon |  | You don't have to use your real name—handles are fine.
Idioms: fly off the handle : to suddenly become very angry ; He seems to fly off the handle about the slightest thing these days. | get/have a handle on somebody/something : to understand or know about somebody/something, especially so that you can deal with it or them later ; I can't get a handle on these sales figures. | give somebody a handle (on something) : to give somebody enough facts or knowledge for them to be able to deal with something ; Visiting the country should give us a better handle on understanding the problems.

Definition: the way that somebody deals with or treats a situation, a person, an animal, etc. | the action of touching, feeling or holding something with your hands | the action of organizing or controlling something | the cost of dealing with an order, delivering goods, booking tickets, etc. | the way in which a vehicle can be controlled by the driver | the act or cost of transporting goods from one place to another
Example: I was impressed by his handling of the affair. | toys that can stand up to rough handling | data handling on computer | a small handling charge | a car designed for easy handling |
Synonym: management | management | management | management | management | management

Definition: easy to use or to do | located near to somebody/something; located or stored in a convenient place | able to use your hands or tools to make or repair things well
Example: a handy little tool | Always keep a first-aid kit handy. | to be handy around the house
Idioms: come in handy : to be useful ; The extra money came in very handy. ; Don't throw that away—it might come in handy. ; Take your penknife—you never know when it might come in handy.

Definition: the act of annoying or worrying somebody by putting pressure on them or saying or doing unpleasant things to them | the act of making repeated attacks on an enemy
Example: sexual/racial harassment | guerrilla harassment of the enemy

Definition: an area of water on the coast, protected from the open sea by strong walls, where ships can shelter | to hide and protect somebody who is hiding from the police | to keep feelings or thoughts, especially negative ones, in your mind for a long time | to contain something and allow it to develop
Example: Several boats lay at anchor in the harbour. | Police believe someone must be harbouring the killer. | The arsonist may harbour a grudge against the company. | Your dishcloth can harbour many germs.

Tags: Oxford_5000 computing also_British_English old-fashioned Computers Shopping
Definition: the machines and electronic parts in a computer or other electronic system | tools and equipment that are used in the house and garden | the equipment, machines and vehicles used to do something
Example: We supply computer hardware to businesses. | a hardware shop/store | tanks and other military hardware

Definition: damage or injury that is caused by a person or an event
Example: He would never frighten anyone or cause them any harm .
Idioms: it wouldn’t do somebody any harm (to do something) : used to suggest that it would be a good idea for somebody to do something ; It wouldn't do you any harm to smarten yourself up. | no harm done : used to tell somebody not to worry because they have caused no serious damage or injury ; Forget it, Dave, no harm done. | out of harm’s way : in a safe place where somebody/something cannot be hurt or injured or do any damage to somebody/something ; She put the knife in a drawer, out of harm’s way. ; I prefer the children to play in the garden where they’re out of harm’s way. | there is no harm in (somebody’s) doing something | it does no harm (for somebody) to do something : used to tell somebody that something is a good idea and will not cause any problems ; He may say no, but there's no harm in asking. ; It does no harm to ask.

Definition: causing damage or injury to somebody/something, especially to a person’s health or to the environment
Example: the harmful effects of alcohol

Tags: Oxford_5000 approving music Social_issues Music Art
Definition: a state of peaceful existence and agreement | the way in which different notes that are played or sung together combine to make a pleasant sound | an attractive combination of related things
Example: social/racial harmony | to sing in harmony | the harmony of colour in nature

Definition: cruel, severe and unkind | very difficult and unpleasant to live in | too strong and bright; ugly or unpleasant to look at | unpleasant to listen to | too strong and rough and likely to damage something
Example: The punishment was harsh and unfair. | a harsh winter/wind/climate | harsh colours | ‘Stop it!’ she said in a harsh voice. | harsh detergents

Definition: the time of year when the crops are gathered in on a farm, etc.; the act of cutting and gathering crops | the crops, or the amount of crops, cut and gathered | to cut and gather a crop; to catch a number of animals or fish to eat | to collect cells or tissue from somebody’s body for use in medical experiments or operations
Example: harvest time | the grain harvest | The nuts are ready to harvest in September. | She had her eggs harvested and frozen for her own future use.
Idioms: reap a/the harvest : to benefit or suffer as a direct result of something that you have done

Definition: a very strong feeling of dislike for somebody/something
Example: He looked at me with intense hatred.

Definition: if the ghost of a dead person haunts a place, people say that they have seen it there | if something unpleasant haunts you, it keeps coming to your mind so that you cannot forget it | to continue to cause problems for somebody for a long time | a place that somebody visits often or where they spend a lot of time
Example: A headless rider haunts the country lanes. | The memory of that day still haunts me. | That decision came back to haunt him . | The pub is a favourite haunt of artists.

Definition: something that can be dangerous or cause damage | to make a suggestion or guess that you know may be wrong | to risk something or put it in danger
Example: a fire/safety hazard | Would you like to hazard a guess ? | Careless drivers hazard other people's lives as well as their own.

Definition: a place from which an organization or a military operation is controlled; the people who work there
Example: The firm’s headquarters is/are in London.

Definition: to become healthy again; to make something healthy again | to cure somebody who is ill; to make somebody feel happy again | to put an end to something or make something easier to bear; to end or become easier to bear
Example: It took a long time for the wounds to heal. | the story of Jesus healing ten lepers of their disease | She was never able to heal the rift between herself and her father.

Definition: the service of providing medical care
Example: the costs of healthcare for the elderly

Tags: Oxford_3000 especially_North_American_English especially_US_English British_English North_American_English Law_and_justice
Definition: the ability to hear | an official meeting at which the facts about a crime, complaint, etc. are presented to the person or group of people who will have to decide what action to take | an opportunity to explain your actions, ideas or opinions
Example: Her hearing is poor. | a court/disciplinary hearing | to get/give somebody a fair hearing
Idioms: in/within (somebody’s) hearing : near enough to somebody so that they can hear what is said ; She shouldn't have said such things in your hearing. ; For God's sake, don't mention it in her hearing. ; I had no reason to believe there was anyone within hearing. | out of hearing : too far away to hear somebody/something or to be heard ; She had moved out of hearing. ; We were just out of the captain's hearing.

Tags: Oxford_3000 informal literary humorous_or_old_use Religion_and_festivals Feelings Danger Success
Definition: the place believed to be the home of God where good people go when they die, sometimes imagined to be in the sky | a place or situation in which you are very happy | the sky
Example: the kingdom of heaven | This isn't exactly my idea of heaven! | Four tall trees stretched up to the heavens.
Idioms: God | God almighty | God in heaven | good God | my God | oh (dear) God : used to emphasize what you are saying when you are surprised, shocked or annoyed ; God, what a stupid thing to do! | God/Heaven forbid (that…) : used to say that you hope that something will not happen ; ‘Maybe you'll end up as a lawyer, like me.’ ‘God forbid!’ | God/Heaven help somebody : used to say that you are afraid somebody will be in danger or that something bad will happen to them ; God help us if this doesn't work. | God/goodness/Heaven knows : used to emphasize that you do not know something ; God knows what else they might find. ; ‘Where are they?’ ‘Goodness knows.’ | God/goodness/Heaven knows : used to emphasize the truth of what you are saying ; She ought to pass the exam—goodness knows she's been working hard enough. | Heaven/God forfend (that)… : used to say that you are frightened of the idea of something happening ; Heaven forfend that students are encouraged to think! | (Good) Heavens! | Heavens above! : used to show that you are surprised or annoyed ; Good heavens, what are you doing? | the heavens open : it begins to rain heavily ; All at once the heavens opened and everyone rushed for cover. | in God’s/Heaven’s name | in the name of God/Heaven : used especially in questions to show that you are angry, surprised or shocked ; What in God's name was that noise? ; Where in the name of Heaven have you been? | in seventh heaven : extremely happy ; Now that he's been promoted he's in seventh heaven. | made in heaven : seeming to be perfect ; Everyone thought that theirs was a marriage made in heaven. | move heaven and earth : to do everything you possibly can in order to achieve something | smell, stink, etc. to high heaven : to have a strong unpleasant smell | smell, stink, etc. to high heaven : to seem to be very dishonest or morally unacceptable | thank God/goodness/heaven(s) (for something) : used to say that you are pleased about something ; Thank God you're safe! ; ‘Thank goodness for that!’ she said with a sigh of relief. ; Thank heavens I've found my keys. | to God/goodness/Heaven : used after a verb to emphasize a particular hope, wish, etc. ; I wish to God you'd learn to pay attention!

Tags: Oxford_3000 old-fashioned informal British_English especially_North_American_English literary Body Clothes_and_Fashion Feelings
Definition: the back part of the foot below the ankle | the part of a sock, etc. that covers the heel | the raised part on the bottom of a shoe, boot, etc. that makes the shoe, etc. higher at the back | having the type of heel mentioned | a pair of shoes that have high heels | the raised part of the inside of the hand where it joins the wrist | a man who is unpleasant to other people and cannot be trusted | to repair the heel of a shoe, etc. | to lean over to one side
Example: Apply the cream to dry, cracked heels before bed. |  | shoes with a low/high heel | high-heeled shoes | She doesn't often wear heels. |  |  |  | The boat heeled over in the strong wind.
Idioms: at/on somebody’s heels : following closely behind somebody ; He fled from the stadium with the police at his heels. ; She came up the path with two little dogs at her heels. | bring somebody/something to heel : to force somebody to obey you and accept discipline ; a non-violent means of bringing the rebels to heel | bring somebody/something to heel : to make a dog come close to you | come to heel : to agree to obey somebody and accept their orders | come to heel : to come close to the person who has called it | cool your heels : to have to wait for somebody/something | dig your heels/toes in : to refuse to do something or to change your mind about something ; They dug in their heels and would not lower the price. | drag your feet/heels : to be deliberately slow in doing something or in making a decision | hard/hot on somebody’s/something’s heels : very close behind somebody/something; very soon after something ; He turned and fled with Peter hot on his heels. ; They reached the border with the police hot on their heels. ; Further successes came hot on the heels of her first best-selling novel. ; News of rising unemployment followed hard on the heels of falling export figures. | head over heels in love : loving somebody very much ; He's fallen head over heels in love with his boss. | kick your heels : to have nothing to do while you are waiting for somebody/something ; We were kicking our heels, waiting for some customers. | kick up your heels : to be relaxed and enjoy yourself | take to your heels : to run away from somebody/something | tread on somebody’s heels : to follow somebody closely | turn/spin on your heel : to turn around suddenly so that you are facing in the opposite direction ; He turned on his heel and marched away angrily. | under the heel of somebody : completely controlled by somebody ; The island spent several centuries under the heel of the British Empire.

Definition: if a feeling or an effect heightens , or something heightens it, it becomes stronger or increases
Example: Tension has heightened after the recent bomb attack.

Tags: Oxford_3000 North_American_English informal offensive slang British_English old-fashioned saying Religion_and_festivals
Definition: the place believed to be the home of devils and where bad people go after death | a very unpleasant experience or situation in which people suffer very much | a swear word that some people use when they are annoyed or surprised or to emphasize something. Its use is offensive to some people.
Example: He was terrified of going to hell when he died. | The last three months have been hell. | Oh hell, I've burned the pan.
Idioms: all hell broke loose : suddenly there was a lot of noise, arguing, fighting, etc. ; There was a loud bang and then all hell broke loose. | beat/kick (the) hell out of somebody/something | knock hell out of somebody/something : to hit somebody/something very hard ; He was a dirty player and loved to kick hell out of the opposition. | bug the hell/crap/shit out of somebody : to annoy somebody very much ; The song just bugs the hell out of me. | catch hell : to be punished or spoken to angrily about something ; If your dad finds out you'll really catch hell! | the devil/hell to pay : a lot of trouble ; There'll be hell to pay when he finds out. | (just) for the hell of it : just for fun; for no real reason ; They stole the car just for the hell of it. | from hell : used to describe a very unpleasant person or thing; the worst that you can imagine ; They are the neighbours from hell. | get the hell out (of…) : to leave a place very quickly ; Let's get the hell out of here. | give somebody hell : to make life unpleasant for somebody ; He used to give his mother hell when he was a teenager. ; My new shoes are giving me hell (= are hurting me) . | give somebody hell : to shout at or speak angrily to somebody ; Dad will give us hell when he sees that mess. | go to hell in a handbasket : to get into a very bad state ; This firm's gone to hell in a handbasket since the new management took over. | hell for leather : as quickly as possible ; to ride hell for leather | hell hath no fury (like a woman scorned) : used to refer to somebody, usually a woman, who has reacted very angrily to something, especially the fact that her husband or partner has been unfaithful | a/one hell of a… | a/one helluva… : used to give emphasis to what a person is saying ; The firm was in a hell of a mess when he took over. ; This holiday is going to cost a hell of a lot of money. ; It must have been one hell of a party. ; It’s taken him a hell of a long time to get here. ; That's one helluva big house you've got. | (come) hell or high water : despite any difficulties ; I was determined to go, come hell or high water. | Hell’s teeth : used to express anger or surprise ; Hell’s teeth, I promised I’d be back by two. | like a bat out of hell : very fast ; She was driving like a bat out of hell. | like hell : used for emphasis ; She worked like hell for her exams. ; My broken finger hurt like hell. | like hell : used when you are refusing permission or saying that something is not true ; ‘I'm coming with you.’ ‘Like hell you are (= you certainly are not) .’ | not have/stand a cat in hell’s chance (of doing something) : to have no chance at all | not have a hope (in hell) (of doing something) : to have no chance at all ; She doesn't have a hope of winning. ; You don't have a hope in hell of finding a job. | not have a snowball’s chance in hell : to have no chance at all ; There isn't a snowball's chance in hell that I'll wear that thing! | play (merry) hell with something/somebody : to affect something/somebody badly ; Driving on these rough roads has played hell with my tyres. | raise hell : to protest angrily, especially in a way that causes trouble for somebody | the road to hell is paved with good intentions : it is not enough to intend to do good things; you must actually do them | scare, annoy, etc. the hell out of somebody : to frighten, annoy, etc. somebody very much | to hell and back : used to say that somebody has been through a difficult situation ; We'd been to hell and back together and we were still good friends. | to hell with somebody/something : used to express anger or dislike and to say that you no longer care about somebody/something and will take no notice of them ; ‘To hell with him,’ she thought, ‘I'm leaving.’

Definition: a type of hard hat that protects the head, worn, for example, by a police officer, a soldier or a person riding a bike or motorbike or playing some sports
Example: Police in riot helmets lined the streets.

Definition: for this reason
Example: We suspect they are trying to hide something, hence the need for an independent inquiry.
Idioms: … days, weeks, etc. hence : a number of days, etc. from now ; The true consequences will only be known several years hence.

Definition: a plant whose leaves, flowers or seeds are used to add taste to food, in medicines or for their pleasant smell. parsley , mint and oregano are all herbs . | a plant with a soft stem that dies down after flowering
Example: a herb garden |

Definition: the history, traditions, buildings and objects that a country or society has had for many years and that are considered an important part of its character | a person's ethnic or religious background; the countries, cultures, religious groups, etc. that a person's parents or ancestors come from | used to describe a traditional product, brand, type of animal or plant variety, especially one that is old-fashioned, rare and of high quality
Example: Spain’s rich cultural heritage | What's your heritage? | Cotswold sheep are considered a heritage breed in Canada.

Definition: to be slow to speak or act because you feel uncertain or nervous | to be worried about doing something, especially because you are not sure that it is right or appropriate
Example: She hesitated before replying. | Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any queries.
Idioms: he who hesitates (is lost) : if you delay in doing something you may lose a good opportunity

Definition: something that is hidden is kept or located in a place where it cannot be seen | secret
Example: Hidden dangers lurk in the ocean depths. | She felt sure the letter had some hidden meaning .

Definition: a system, especially in a society or an organization, in which people are organized into different levels of importance from highest to lowest | the group of people in control of a large organization or institution | a system that ideas or beliefs can be arranged into according to their importance
Example: the social/political hierarchy | It was on orders from the party hierarchy that she dropped the investigation. | a hierarchy of needs

Definition: receiving or involving a lot of attention and discussion in the media
Example: a high-profile campaign

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_North_American_English British_English formal North_American_English informal Transport_by_car_or_lorry
Definition: a main road for travelling long distances, especially one connecting and going through cities and towns | a public road
Example: Plans were being made for the construction of a new interstate highway system. | A parked car was obstructing the highway.
Idioms: highway robbery : the fact of somebody charging too much money for something | my way or the highway : used to say that somebody else has either to agree with your opinion or to leave
Synonym: main road | main road | main road | main road

Definition: extremely funny
Example: a hilarious joke/story

Definition: something that you say or do in an indirect way in order to show somebody what you are thinking | something that suggests what will happen in the future | a small amount of something | a small piece of practical information or advice | to suggest something in an indirect way
Example: He gave a broad hint (= one that was obvious) that he was thinking of retiring. | At the first hint of trouble, they left. | a hint of a smile | handy hints on saving money | What are you hinting at?
Idioms: take a/the hint : to understand what somebody wants you to do even though they tell you in an indirect way ; I thought they'd never go—some people just can't take a hint. ; Sarah hoped he'd take the hint and leave her alone. ; Can't you take a hint and leave me alone?

Tags: Oxford_5000 informal formal British_English disapproving both_informal often_disapproving Body
Definition: the area at either side of the body between the top of the leg and the middle part of the body; the joint at the top of the leg that connects it with the top part of the body | having hips of the size or shape mentioned | the red fruit that grows on some types of wild rose bush
Example: She stood with her hands on her hips. | large-hipped |
Idioms: hip, hip, hooray! : used by a group of people to show their approval of somebody. One person in the group says ‘ hip, hip ’ and the others then shout ‘ hooray ’ . ; ‘Three cheers for the bride and groom: Hip, hip…’ ‘Hooray!’ | shoot from the hip : to react quickly without thinking carefully first

Definition: a person who studies or writes about history; an expert in history
Example: No serious historian today accepts this theory.

Definition: having a hole or empty space inside | sinking deeply into the face | making a low sound like that made by an empty object when it is hit | not sincere | without real value | an area that is lower than the surface around it, especially on the ground | a hole or an empty space inside something | to make a flat surface go inward in a curve
Example: a hollow ball/centre/tube/tree | hollow eyes/cheeks | a hollow groan | I've had enough of their hollow promises . | He won something of a hollow victory when his opponent was forced to withdraw with a serious injury. | All but one of the ponds are empty, their sites marked only by muddy hollows. | The squirrel disappeared into a hollow at the base of the tree. | The stairs have been hollowed by centuries of use.
Idioms: ring hollow/false : to give the impression of not being sincere or true ; His expressions of support rang rather hollow. ; His promise rang hollow. | ring true/hollow/false : to give the impression of being sincere/true or not sincere/true ; It may seem a strange story but it rings true to me. ; His expressions of support rang rather hollow. ; His promise rang hollow.

Definition: connected with God or a particular religion | good in a moral and religious way | used to emphasize that you are surprised, afraid, etc.
Example: the Holy Bible | a holy life/man | Holy cow! What was that?

Definition: the country where a person was born | (in the Republic of South Africa under the apartheid system in the past) one of the areas with some self-government that were intended for a group of black African people to live in
Example: Many refugees have been forced to flee their homeland. | the Transkei homeland

Definition: having no home, and therefore typically living on the streets | people who have no home
Example: The scheme has been set up to help homeless people. | helping the homeless

Definition: the quality of being honest
Example: She answered all my questions with her usual honesty.
Idioms: in (all) honesty : used to state a fact or an opinion that, though true, may seem disappointing ; The book isn't, in all honesty, as good as I expected. ; In all honesty, the book was not as good as I expected. ; Who in honesty can blame her?

Tags: Oxford_3000 North_American_English formal saying Feelings Personal_qualities Education
Definition: great respect for somebody | something that you are very pleased or proud to do because people are showing you great respect | the quality of knowing and doing what is morally right | a good reputation; respect from other people | a person or thing that causes others to respect and admire something/somebody | an award, official title, etc. given to somebody as a reward for something that they have done | a university course that is of a higher level than a basic course (in the US also used to describe a class in school that is at a higher level than other classes) | if you pass an exam or graduate from a university or school with honours , you receive a special grade for having achieved a very high standard | a title of respect used when talking to or about a judge or a US mayor | the cards that have the highest value | to do something that shows great respect for somebody/something | to give public praise, an award or a title to somebody for something they have done | to do what you have agreed or promised to do
Example: the guest of honour (= the most important one) | It was a great honour to be invited here today. | a man of honour | upholding the honour of your country | She is an honour to the profession. | the New Year’s Honours list (= in Britain, a list of awards and titles given on January 1 each year) | an honours degree/course | He passed with second-class honours. | No more questions, Your Honour. |  | our honoured guests | She has never been properly honoured in her own country. | I have every intention of honouring our contract.
Idioms: do somebody an honour | do somebody the honour (of doing something) : to do something to make somebody feel very proud and pleased ; Would you do me the honour of dining with me? | do the honours : to perform a social duty or ceremony, such as pouring drinks, making a speech, etc. ; Would you do the honours and draw the winning ticket? | have the honour of something/of doing something : to be given the opportunity to do something that makes you feel proud and happy ; May I have the honour of the next dance? ; It was Tevez who had the honour of scoring the first goal. ; She had the honour of being the first living artist to have her work exhibited in the Louvre. ; I had the rare honour of being allowed into the artist's studio. ; Max was given the dubious honour (= it may not really have made him feel proud and happy) of organizing the children's party. | (there is) honour among thieves : used to say that even criminals have standards of behaviour that they respect | (feel) honour-bound to do something : to feel that you must do something because of your sense of moral duty ; She felt honour-bound to attend as she had promised to. ; He felt honour bound to help her. | the honours are even : no particular person, team, etc. is doing better than the others in a competition, an argument, etc. | in honour of somebody/something | in somebody’s/something’s honour : in order to show great respect for somebody/something ; a ceremony in honour of those killed in the explosion ; A banquet was held in her honour. | on your honour : used to promise very seriously that you will do something or that something is true ; I swear on my honour that I knew nothing about this. | on your honour : to be trusted to do something ; You're on your honour not to go into my room. | a point of honour : a thing that somebody considers to be very important for their honour or reputation | be/feel honoured (to do something) : to feel proud and happy ; I was honoured to have been mentioned in his speech. ; ‘Would you be my best man?’ ‘I’d be honoured.’

Tags: Oxford_5000 becoming_old-fashioned North_American_English informal British_English Sports:_ball_and_racket_sports Feelings Phones,_email_and_the_internet
Definition: a curved piece of metal, plastic or wire for hanging things on, catching fish with, etc. | a short hard hit that is made with the elbow bent | a way of hitting the ball so that it curves to the side instead of going straight ahead (usually by mistake in golf, but deliberately in cricket ) | a thing that is used to make people interested in something | to fasten or hang something on something else using a hook ; to be fastened or hanging in this way | to put something, especially your leg, arm or finger, around something else so that you can hold onto it or move it; to go around something else in this way | to catch a fish with a hook | to hit or kick a ball so that it goes to one side instead of straight ahead (usually by mistake in golf but deliberately in other sports)
Example: a picture/curtain/coat hook | a left hook to the jaw |  | The images are used as a hook to get children interested in science. | We hooked the trailer to the back of the car. | He hooked his foot under the stool and dragged it over. | It was the biggest pike I ever hooked. | He hooked his drive into the trees.
Idioms: by hook or by crook : using any method you can, even a dishonest one | hook, line and sinker : completely ; What I said was not true, but he fell for it (= believed it) hook, line and sinker. | off the hook : having got free from a difficult situation or a punishment ; I lied to get him off the hook. ; No way will she let you off the hook this time. ; I was finally off the hook. | off the hook : if you leave or take a landline phone off the hook , you take the receiver (= the part that you pick up) off the place where it usually rests, so that nobody can call you | on the hook for something : responsible for paying for something ; Citizens are increasingly on the hook for more of their own medical costs. | ring off the hook : to ring many times, with one phone call after another ; The phone has been ringing off the hook with offers of help. | sling your hook : to go away

Definition: believing that something you want will happen | showing hope | making you believe that something you want will happen; bringing hope | a person who wants to succeed at something
Example: I feel hopeful that we'll find a suitable house very soon. | a hopeful smile | The latest trade figures are a hopeful sign. | 50 young hopefuls are trying for a place in the England team.

Definition: used to express what you hope will happen | showing hope
Example: Hopefully, we'll arrive before dark. | ‘Are you free tonight?’ she asked hopefully.

Definition: the furthest that you can see, where the sky seems to meet the land or the sea | the limit of your desires, knowledge or interests
Example: The sun sank below the horizon. | She wanted to travel to broaden her horizons .
Idioms: on the horizon : likely to happen soon ; There's trouble looming on the horizon.
Synonym: limit of experience | limit of experience | limit of experience

Definition: a hard pointed part that grows, usually in pairs, on the heads of some animals, such as sheep and cows. Horns are often curved. | the hard substance of which animal horns are made | a device in a vehicle for making a loud sound as a warning or signal | a simple musical instrument that consists of a curved metal tube that you blow into | a brass musical instrument that consists of a long tube curled around in a circle with a wide opening at the end
Example: a large bull with curved horns | ornaments made of rhino horn | to honk your car horn | a hunting horn | a horn concerto
Idioms: blow/toot your own horn : to praise your own abilities and achievements | draw/pull your horns in : to start being more careful in your behaviour, especially by spending less money than before ; Small businesses have had to pull their horns in during the recession. | lock horns (with somebody) (over something) : to get involved in an argument with somebody ; The company has locked horns with the unions over proposed pay cuts. | on the horns of a dilemma : in a situation in which you have to make a choice between things that are equally unpleasant ; The medical profession’s eagerness for scientific advance had impaled it on the horns of a dilemma, forcing an unnatural choice between science and morality. ; The dire economic situation had placed the prime minister on the horns of a dilemma. | take the bull by the horns : to face a difficult or dangerous situation directly and with courage ; Nora decided to take the bull by the horns and organize things for herself.

Definition: a person who is captured and held prisoner by a person or group, and who may be injured or killed if people do not do what the person or group is asking
Example: Three children were taken hostage during the bank robbery.
Idioms: a hostage to fortune : something that you have, or have promised to do, that could cause trouble or worry in the future
