Definition: aggressive or unfriendly and ready to argue or fight | strongly rejecting something | making it difficult for something to happen or to be achieved | belonging to a military enemy | not wanted by the company that is to be bought
Example: The speaker got a hostile reception from the audience. | The proposals have provoked a hostile response from opposition parties. | hostile conditions for plants to grow in | hostile territory | a hostile takeover bid

Definition: aggressive or unfriendly feelings or behaviour | strong and angry opposition towards an idea, a plan or a situation | acts of fighting in a war
Example: There was a barely veiled hostility in her tone. | The proposal was met with outright hostility. | the start/outbreak of hostilities between the two sides

Definition: all the people living together in a house or flat
Example: Most households now own at least one car.

Definition: houses, flats, etc. that people live in, especially when referring to their type, price or condition | the job of providing houses, flats, etc. for people to live in | a hard cover that protects part of a machine
Example: rental/student housing | Tenants may only keep pets with the written consent of the housing department. | a car’s rear axle housing

Definition: connected with helping people who are suffering and improving the conditions that they are living in | describing an event or situation that causes or involves a lot of human suffering, especially one that requires aid to be provided to a large number of people | a person who tries to help people who are suffering and improve the conditions that they are living in
Example: to provide humanitarian aid to the war zone | a humanitarian crisis |

Definition: people in general | the state of being a person rather than a god, an animal or a machine | the quality of being kind to people and animals by making sure that they do not suffer more than is necessary; the quality of being humane | the subjects of study that are about the way people think and behave, for example literature, language, history and philosophy
Example: He was found guilty of crimes against humanity. | The story was used to emphasize the humanity of Jesus. | The judge was praised for his courage and humanity. | The college offers a wide range of courses in the arts and humanities.

Definition: showing you do not think that you are as important as other people | used to suggest that you are not as important as other people, but in a way that is not sincere or not very serious | having a low rank or social position | not large or special in any way | to make somebody feel that they are not as good or important as they thought they were | to easily defeat an opponent, especially a strong or powerful one | to show that you are not too proud to ask for something, admit that you have been wrong, etc.
Example: Be humble enough to learn from your mistakes. | In my humble opinion , you were in the wrong. | a man of humble birth/origins | a humble farmhouse | He was humbled by her generosity. | The world champion was humbled last night in three rounds. | He refused to humble himself and ask her forgiveness.
Idioms: eat humble pie : to say and show that you are sorry for a mistake that you made

Definition: funny; showing a sense of humour
Example: He gave a humorous account of their trip to Spain.

Definition: the quality in something that makes it funny; the ability to laugh at things that are funny | the state of your feelings or mind at a particular time | one of the four liquids that were thought in the past to be in a person’s body and to influence health and character | to agree with somebody’s wishes, even if they seem unreasonable, in order to keep the person happy
Example: It was a story full of gentle humour. | to be in the best of humours |  | She thought it best to humour them rather than get into an argument.

Definition: the state of not having enough food to eat, especially when this causes illness or death | the feeling caused by a need to eat | a strong desire for something
Example: Around fifty people die of hunger every day in the camp. | If you feel hunger pangs between meals, eat some fruit or nuts. | She has an insatiable hunger for knowledge.

Definition: going after and killing wild animals as a sport or for food | a sport in which foxes are hunted by specially trained dogs and by people on horses. Fox hunting with dogs is now illegal in the UK. | the process of looking for something
Example: He goes hunting every weekend. |  | How's the job-hunting going?

Definition: a chemical element. Hydrogen is a gas that is the lightest of all the elements. It combines with oxygen to form water.
an idea or explanation of something that is based on a few known facts but that has not yet been proved to be true or correct | guesses and ideas that are not based on certain knowledge
Example: to formulate/confirm a hypothesis | It would be pointless to engage in hypothesis before we have the facts.

Definition: a small symbol on a computer or smartphone screen that represents a program or a file | a famous person or thing that people admire and see as a symbol of a particular idea, way of life, etc. | a painting or statue of a holy person that is also thought of as a holy object
Example: Click on the printer icon with the mouse. | Madonna and other pop icons of the 1980s |

Definition: the part of the unconscious mind where many of a person’s basic needs, feelings and desires are supposed to exist | to identify somebody, usually a criminal or a dead body | to ask somebody to show a document that shows proof of their name, age, etc.
Example: | The police haven’t yet managed to ID the suspect. | The security guards ID’d everyone at the entrance to the festival.

Definition: similar in every detail | the same
Example: a row of identical houses | This is the identical room we stayed in last year.

Definition: the process of showing, proving or recognizing who or what somebody/something is | the process of recognizing that something exists, or is important | official papers or a document that can prove who you are | a strong feeling of sympathy, understanding or support for somebody/something | the process of making a close connection between one person or thing and another
Example: The identification of the crash victims was a long and difficult task. | The early identification of children with special educational needs is very important. | Can I see some identification, please? | her emotional identification with the play’s heroine | the voters’ identification of the Democrats with high taxes

Definition: based on or connected with an ideology
Example: ideological differences

Definition: a set of ideas that an economic or political system is based on | a set of beliefs, especially one held by a particular group, that influences the way people behave
Example: Marxist/capitalist ideology | the ideology of gender roles
Synonym: set of beliefs | set of beliefs

Definition: a rude way to refer to somebody who you think is very stupid | a word for a person with very low intelligence that is now considered offensive
Example: When I lost my passport, I felt such an idiot. |

Definition: a lack of knowledge or information about something
Example: They fought a long battle against prejudice and ignorance.
Idioms: blissful ignorance : the state of not knowing about something and so being unable to worry about it ; We preferred to remain in blissful ignorance of what was going on. | ignorance is bliss : if you do not know about something, you cannot worry about it ; Some doctors believe ignorance is bliss and don't give their patients all the facts.

Definition: not having or showing much knowledge or information about things; not educated | not having any knowledge or information about a particular thing | with very bad manners
Example: an ignorant person/question | At that time I was ignorant of events going on elsewhere. | a rude, ignorant person

Definition: a false idea or belief, especially about somebody or about a situation | something that seems to exist but in fact does not, or seems to be something that it is not
Example: She's under the illusion that (= believes wrongly that) she'll get the job. | Mirrors in a room often give an illusion of space.

Definition: to use pictures, photographs, diagrams, etc. in a book, etc. | to make the meaning of something clearer by using examples, pictures, etc. | to show that something is true or that a situation exists
Example: She illustrated her own books. | To illustrate my point , let me tell you a little story. | The incident illustrates the need for better security measures.

Definition: a drawing or picture in a book, magazine, for decoration or to explain something | the process of illustrating something | a story, an event or an example that clearly shows the truth about something
Example: 50 full-colour illustrations | the art of book illustration | The statistics are a clear illustration of the point I am trying to make.

Definition: language that produces pictures in the minds of people reading or listening | pictures, photographs, etc.
Example: poetic imagery | satellite imagery (= for example, photographs of the earth taken from space)

Definition: the ability to create pictures in your mind; the part of your mind that does this | something that you have imagined rather than something that exists | the ability to have new and exciting ideas
Example: a vivid/fertile imagination | She was no longer able to distinguish between imagination and reality. | His writing lacks imagination.
Idioms: leave nothing/little to the imagination : to allow more of somebody’s body to be seen than usual ; Her tight-fitting dress left nothing to the imagination. | not by any stretch of the imagination | by no stretch of the imagination : used to say strongly that something is not true, even if you try to imagine or believe it ; She could not, by any stretch of the imagination, be called beautiful. ; By no stretch of the imagination could the trip be described as relaxing.

Definition: extremely large or great
Example: There is still an immense amount of work to be done.

Definition: to come and live permanently in a country after leaving your own country
Example: About 6.6 million people immigrated to the United States in the 1970s.

Definition: the process of coming to live permanently in a different country from the one you were born in; the number of people who do this | the place at a port, an airport, etc. where the passports and other documents of people coming into a country are checked
Example: legal/illegal immigration | We landed at Heathrow and went through customs and immigration.

Definition: likely to happen very soon
Example: the imminent threat of invasion

Definition: that cannot catch or be affected by a particular disease or illness | not affected by something, especially something that you might expect to be harmful | protected from something and therefore able to avoid it
Example: Adults are often immune to German measles. | You'll eventually become immune to criticism. | No one should be immune from prosecution.

Definition: annoyed by somebody/something, especially because you have to wait for a long time | wanting to do something soon; wanting something to happen soon | unable or unwilling to accept something unpleasant
Example: I'd been waiting for twenty minutes and I was getting impatient. | She was clearly impatient to leave. | impatient of criticism

Definition: a tool or an instrument, often one that is quite simple and that is used outdoors | to make something that has been officially decided start to happen or be used
Example: agricultural implements | to implement changes/decisions/policies/reforms

Definition: the act of making something that has been officially decided start to happen or be used
Example: the implementation of the new system

Definition: to show or suggest that somebody is involved in something bad or criminal | to show or suggest that something is the cause of something bad
Example: He tried to avoid saying anything that would implicate him further. | The results implicate poor hygiene as one cause of the outbreak.
Idioms: be implicated in something : to be involved in a crime; to be responsible for something bad ; Senior officials were implicated in the scandal. ; These groups are very strongly implicated in the violence.

Definition: a possible effect or result of an action or a decision | something that is suggested or indirectly stated (= something that is implied) | the fact of being involved, or of involving somebody, in something, especially a crime
Example: They failed to consider the wider implications of their actions. | I resent the implication that I don't care about my father. | He resigned after his implication in a sex scandal.

Definition: suggested without being directly expressed | forming part of something (although perhaps not directly expressed) | complete and not doubted
Example: Implicit in his speech was the assumption that they were guilty. | The ability to listen is implicit in the teacher's role. | She had the implicit trust of her staff.

Definition: to suggest that something is true or that you feel or think something, without saying so directly | to make it seem likely that something is true or exists | to make something necessary in order to be successful
Example: Are you implying (that) I am wrong? | The survey implies (that) more people are moving house than was thought. | The project implies an enormous investment in training.

Definition: to introduce a new law, rule, tax, etc.; to order that a rule, punishment, etc. be used | to force somebody/something to have to deal with something that is difficult or unpleasant | to make somebody accept the same opinions, wishes, etc. as your own | to expect somebody to do something for you or to spend time with you, when it may not be convenient for them | to make somebody/something accept or be aware of your presence or ideas
Example: The UN Security Council imposed sanctions in 1992. | He described the limitations imposed by his disease. | She didn't want to impose her values on her family. | ‘You must stay for lunch.’ ‘Well, thanks, but I don’t want to impose…’ | European civilization was the first to impose itself across the whole world.

Definition: if a person or thing impresses you, you admire them or it | to make somebody understand how important, serious, etc. something is by emphasizing it | to have a great effect on something, especially somebody’s mind, imagination, etc.
Example: We interviewed a number of candidates but none of them impressed us. | He impressed on us the need for immediate action. | Her words impressed themselves on my memory.

Definition: admiring somebody/something because you think they are particularly good, interesting, etc.
Example: I must admit I am impressed.

Definition: to put somebody in a prison or another place from which they cannot escape

Definition: the act of putting somebody in a prison or another place from which they cannot escape; the state of being there
Example: to be sentenced to life imprisonment for murder

Definition: the fact of not being able to do something
Example: the government’s inability to provide basic services

Definition: not enough; not good enough | not able, or not confident enough, to deal with a situation
Example: inadequate supplies | I felt totally inadequate as a parent.

Definition: not suitable or appropriate in a particular situation
Example: inappropriate behaviour/language

Definition: something that encourages you to do something | a payment or concession (= a reduction in the amount of money that has to be paid) that encourages somebody to do something
Example: There is no incentive for people to save fuel. | tax incentives to encourage savings

Definition: a unit for measuring length or height, equal to 2.54 centimetres . There are 12 inches in a foot. | a small amount or distance | to move or make something move slowly and carefully in a particular direction
Example: She's a few inches taller than me. | He escaped death by an inch. | She moved forward, inching towards the rope.
Idioms: every inch : the whole of something ; The doctor examined every inch of his body. ; If they try to fire me I'll fight them every inch of the way . | every inch : completely ; In his first game the young player already looked every inch a winner. | give somebody an inch (and they’ll take a mile/yard) : used to say that if you allow some people a small amount of freedom or power they will see you as weak and try to take a lot more | inch by inch : very slowly and with great care or difficulty ; She crawled forward inch by inch. | not budge/give/move an inch : to refuse to change your position, decision, etc. even a little ; We tried to negotiate a lower price but they wouldn't budge an inch. | not trust somebody an inch : to not trust somebody at all ; He says he just wants to help you but I wouldn’t trust him an inch if I were you.

Definition: the extent to which something happens or has an effect | the way in which light meets a surface
Example: an area with a high incidence of crime | the angle of incidence

Definition: touching or hitting a surface | something that happens, especially something unusual or unpleasant | a serious or violent event, such as a crime, an accident or an attack | a disagreement between two countries, often involving military forces
Example: The technology relies on a beam of incident light passing through a prism. | His bad behaviour was just an isolated incident . | a violent/serious incident | a border/diplomatic incident

Definition: a slope | to tend to think or behave in a particular way; to make somebody do this | to bend your head forward, especially as a sign of agreement, welcome, etc. | to lean or slope in a particular direction; to make something lean or slope
Example: a steep/slight incline | I incline to the view that we should take no action at this stage. | He inclined his head in acknowledgement. | The land inclined gently towards the shore.

Definition: wanting to do something | tending to do something; likely to do something | used when you are expressing an opinion but do not want to express it very strongly | having a natural ability for something; preferring to do something | sloping; at an angle
Example: She was inclined to trust him. | He's inclined to be lazy. | I'm inclined to agree with you. | musically/academically inclined children |

Definition: the fact of including somebody/something; the fact of being included | a person or thing that is included | the fact or policy of providing equal opportunities and resources for people who might otherwise not get them, for example people who are disabled or belong to minority groups
Example: His inclusion in the team is in doubt. | There were some surprising inclusions in the list. | The company is actively promoting diversity and inclusion .

Definition: to include something so that it forms a part of something | to create a legally recognized company

Definition: not accurate or true | speaking or behaving in a way that does not follow the accepted standards or rules
Example: incorrect information/spelling |

Definition: more and more all the time

Definition: if you incur something unpleasant, you are in a situation in which you have to deal with it | if you incur costs, you have to pay them
Example: She had incurred the wrath of her father by marrying without his consent. | You risk incurring bank charges if you exceed your overdraft limit.

Definition: freedom from political control by other countries | the time when a country gains freedom from political control by another country | the freedom to organize your own life, make your own decisions, etc. without needing help from other people
Example: Argentina gained independence from Spain in 1816. | independence celebrations | He values his independence.

Definition: a list of names or topics that are referred to in a book, etc., usually arranged at the end of a book in alphabetical order or listed in a separate file or book | a box of cards with information on them, arranged in alphabetical order | a system that shows the level of prices and wages, etc. so that they can be compared with those of a previous date | a number giving a measurement of something compared with a particular standard | a sign or measure that something else can be judged by | a list of the records in a computer file with information about where each is located | the small number written above a larger number to show how many times that number must be multiplied by itself. In the equation 4 2 = 16, the number 2 is an index . | to make an index of documents, the contents of a book, etc.; to add something to a list of this type | to link wages, etc. to the level of prices of food, clothing, etc. so that they both increase at the same rate
Example: Look it up in the index. |  | the cost-of-living index | White bread has a high glycaemic index. | The number of new houses being built is a good index of a country's prosperity. | Search the index to find the address of the data file. |  | All publications are indexed by subject and title. | The basic state pension is indexed to inflation.
Synonym: reference list | reference list | reference list | reference list | reference list | reference list | reference list

Definition: a remark or sign that shows that something is happening or what somebody is thinking or feeling | a symptom that suggests that particular medical treatment is necessary
Example: They gave no indication of how the work should be done. | There was no clinical indication for such a test.

Tags: Oxford_5000 OPAL_written AWL British_English North_American_English informal Transport_by_car_or_lorry
Definition: a sign that shows you what something is like or how a situation is changing | a device on a machine that shows speed, pressure, etc. | a light on a vehicle that flashes to show that the vehicle is going to turn left or right
Example: The economic indicators are better than expected. | a depth indicator | Her right-hand indicator was flashing.

Definition: a sign that a system, society, etc. is very bad or very wrong | an official statement accusing somebody of a crime | the act of officially accusing somebody of a crime
Example: The poverty in our cities is a damning indictment of modern society. | She was convicted on an indictment for conspiracy. | This led to his indictment on allegations of conspiracy.

Definition: coming from a particular place and having lived there for a long time before other people came there; relating to, belonging to or developed by these people | having originally developed in a particular place, rather than having been brought there from somewhere else
Example: the indigenous peoples/languages of an area | The kangaroo is indigenous to Australia.

Tags: Oxford_5000 OPAL_written AWL formal medical Change,_cause_and_effect Life_stages
Definition: to persuade or influence somebody to do something | to cause something | to make a woman start giving birth to her baby by giving her special drugs; to make a baby start being born by giving the mother special drugs
Example: Nothing would induce me to take the job. | drugs that induce sleep | an induced labour

Definition: to allow yourself to have or do something that you like, especially something that is considered bad for you | to satisfy a particular desire, interest, etc. | to be too generous in allowing somebody to have or do whatever they like | to take part in an activity, especially one that is illegal
Example: They went into town to indulge in some serious shopping. | The inheritance enabled him to indulge his passion for art. | She did not believe in indulging the children with presents. |
Synonym: cater to | cater to | cater to | cater to

Definition: connected with industry | used by industries | having many industries | very great in extent or amount
Example: an industrial dispute | industrial chemicals | an industrial city/town | They had made industrial quantities of food.

Definition: the unfair difference between groups of people in society, when some have more wealth, status or opportunities than others
Example: We need to tackle inequality of opportunity wherever we find it.

Definition: that you cannot avoid or prevent | happening so often that you always expect it | something that is certain to happen
Example: It was an inevitable consequence of the decision. | the English and their inevitable cups of tea | You have to accept the inevitable.

Definition: as is certain to happen | as you would expect
Example: Inevitably, the press exaggerated the story. | Inevitably, it rained on the day of the wedding.

Definition: well known for being bad or evil
Example: a general who was infamous for his brutality

Definition: designed to be used by infants | new and not yet developed | a baby or very young child | a child at school between the ages of four and seven
Example: infant formula (= milk for babies) | infant industries | a nursery for infants under two | The majority of infant teachers are women.

Definition: to make a disease or an illness spread to a person, an animal or a plant | to make a substance contain harmful bacteria that can spread disease | to make a computer virus spread to another computer or program | to make somebody share a particular feeling
Example: It is not possible to infect another person through kissing. | The eggs were infected with salmonella. |  | She infected the children with her enthusiasm for music.

Definition: the act or process of causing or getting a disease | an illness that is caused by bacteria or a virus and that affects one part of the body
Example: to cause/prevent infection | an ear/a chest infection

Definition: to reach an opinion or decide that something is true on the basis of information that is available | to suggest indirectly that something is true
Example: Much of the meaning must be inferred from the context. | Are you inferring (that) I’m not capable of doing the job?

Definition: a fall in the value of money and a general increase in prices; the rate at which this happens | a general rise in the level of something that is awarded | the act or process of filling something with air or gas
Example: the fight against rising inflation | the gross inflation of executive salaries | life jackets with an automatic inflation device

Definition: to make somebody/something suffer something unpleasant
Example: They inflicted a humiliating defeat on the home team.

Definition: having a lot of influence on somebody/something
Example: a highly influential book

Definition: information | connected with information
Example: For more info, click here. | an infosheet

Definition: to tell somebody about something, especially in an official way | to find out information about something | to have an influence on something
Example: The government took this decision without consulting Parliament or informing the public . | We need time to inform ourselves thoroughly of the problem. | Religion informs every aspect of their lives.

Definition: the basic systems and services that are necessary for a country or an organization to run smoothly, for example buildings, transport and water and power supplies
a person or an animal that lives in a particular place
Example: the oldest inhabitant of the village

Definition: that is a basic or permanent part of somebody/something and that cannot be removed
Example: the difficulties inherent in a study of this type

Definition: to receive money, property, etc. from somebody when they die | to have qualities, physical features, etc. that are similar to those of your parents, grandparents, etc. | if you inherit a particular situation from somebody, you are now responsible for dealing with it, especially because you have replaced that person in their job
Example: She inherited a fortune from her father. | He has inherited his mother's patience. | policies inherited from the previous administration
Synonym: fall heir to | fall heir to | fall heir to

Definition: to prevent something from happening or make it happen more slowly or less frequently than normal | to make somebody nervous or embarrassed so that they are unable to do something
Example: A lack of oxygen may inhibit brain development in the unborn child. | The managing director's presence inhibited them from airing their problems.

Definition: happening at the beginning; first | the first letter of a person’s first name or middle name | the first letters of all the names of a person or thing | to mark or sign something with your initials
Example: There is an initial payment of £60 followed by ten instalments of £25. | ‘What initial is it, Mrs Owen?’ ‘It's J, J for Jane.’ | John Fitzgerald Kennedy was often known by his initials JFK. | Please initial each page and sign in the space provided.

Definition: at the beginning
Example: Initially, the system worked well.

Definition: a person who has been allowed to join a particular group, organization, or religion and is learning its rules and secrets | to make something begin | to explain something to somebody and/or make them experience it for the first time | to make somebody a member of a particular group, especially as part of a secret ceremony
Example: | to initiate legal proceedings against somebody | She was initiated into classical music by her mother. | Hundreds are initiated into the sect each year.
Synonym: set/put in motion | set/put in motion | set/put in motion

Definition: a new plan for dealing with a particular problem or for achieving a particular purpose | the ability to decide and act on your own without waiting for somebody to tell you what to do | the power or opportunity to act and gain an advantage before other people do | (in some states of the US) a process by which ordinary people can suggest a new law by signing a petition
Example: a United Nations peace initiative | He said I was lazy and had no initiative. | to seize/regain the initiative | a ballot initiative to establish a local minimum wage

Definition: to put a drug or other substance into a person’s or an animal’s body using a syringe | to put a liquid or other substance into something using a syringe or similar instrument | to add a particular quality to something | to give money to an organization, a project, etc. so that it can function
Example: Adrenaline was injected into the muscle. | The fruit is injected with chemicals to reduce decay. | His comments injected a note of humour into the proceedings. | They are refusing to inject any more capital into the industry.

Definition: an act of injecting somebody with a drug or other substance | a large sum of money that is spent to help improve a situation, business, etc. | an act of forcing a liquid or other substance into something
Example: to give somebody an injection | The theatre faces closure unless it gets an urgent cash injection. | a 2-litre direct injection engine

Definition: the fact of a situation being unfair and of people not being treated equally; an unfair act or an example of unfair treatment

Definition: serious and important | having a meaning that is easy to understand | intended to communicate or express something to somebody, without any words being spoken

Definition: an action, an object or a system by which a result is achieved; a way of achieving or doing something | the money that a person has
Example: Email is a highly effective means of communication . | a man of means (= a rich man)
Idioms: by all means : used to say that you are very willing for somebody to have something or do something ; ‘Do you mind if I have a look?’ ‘By all means.’ | by fair means or foul : using dishonest methods if honest ones do not work ; She’s determined to win, by fair means or foul. | by means of something : with the help of something ; The load was lifted by means of a crane. | by no means | not by any (manner of) means : not at all ; She is by no means an inexperienced teacher. ; We haven't won yet, not by any means. | the end justifies the means : bad or unfair methods of doing something are acceptable if the result of that action is good or positive ; He defended a morality in which the end justifies the means. ; That's only OK if you believe that the end justifies the means. | a means to an end : a thing or an action that is not interesting or important in itself but is a way of achieving something else ; He doesn't particularly like the work but he sees it as a means to an end. ; I don't enjoy studying computing—it's just a means to an end. | ways and means : the methods and materials available for doing something ; ways and means of raising money ; Newspapers have ways and means of getting hold of secret information.

Definition: in the period of time between two times or events | while something else is happening

Idioms: for the meantime/meanwhile : for a short period of time but not permanently ; I'm changing my email address but for the meantime you can use the old one. | in the meantime/meanwhile : in the period of time between two times or two events ; My first novel was rejected by six publishers. In the meantime I had written a play. ; The next programme starts in five minutes; in the meantime, here’s some music.

Definition: a kind or forgiving attitude towards somebody that you have the power to harm or right to punish | an event or a situation to be grateful for, usually because it stops something unpleasant

Definition: used when you want to emphasize how small, unimportant, etc. somebody/something is | used when you are saying that the fact that a particular thing is present in a situation is enough to have an influence on that situation | a small lake

Definition: used meaning ‘only’ or ‘simply’ to emphasize a fact or something that you are saying

Definition: very small in size or amount; as small as possible

Definition: to reduce something to the least possible level or amount : | to make something seem less important or smaller than it really is: | to make something small on a computer screen : | to reduce something to the smallest possible level or amount : | To minimize something is also to make it seem less important or smaller than it really is: | to reduce something to the smallest possible level or amount : | to make something seem less important or smaller than it really is: | to click on the corner of a computer window to reduce it to a small title and hide its contents :

Definition: any of the long fibres that carry messages between the brain and parts of the body, enabling you to move, feel pain, etc. | feelings of worry or stress | the courage to do something difficult or dangerous | a way of behaving that other people think is rude or not appropriate | to give yourself the courage or strength to do something difficult or unpleasant
Example: the optic nerve | Even after years as a singer, he still suffers from nerves before a performance. | It took a lot of nerve to take the company to court. | I don't know how you have the nerve to show your face after what you said! | He nerved himself to ask her out.
Idioms: be a bag/bundle of nerves : to be very nervous ; By the time of the interview, I was a bundle of nerves. | get on somebody’s nerves : to annoy somebody ; That music is starting to get on my nerves. ; It really gets on my nerves when people talk loudly on the phone in public. ; His endless whining really gets on my nerves. | have nerves of steel : to be able to remain calm in a difficult or dangerous situation ; You need nerves of steel to be a good poker player. | hit/touch/strike a (raw/sensitive) nerve : to mention a subject that makes somebody feel angry, upset, embarrassed, etc. ; You touched a raw nerve when you mentioned his first wife. ; My remarks about divorce had unwittingly touched a raw nerve. ; The article struck a raw nerve as it revived unpleasant memories. | strain every nerve/sinew (to do something) : to try as hard as you can to do something ; He strained every nerve to snatch victory from defeat. | strike a nerve (with somebody) : to have a strong effect ; His work strikes a nerve with people who are attracted to nostalgia. ; It seems I struck a nerve with my last post about cell phones. ; Nissan may well strike a popular nerve here. | a war of nerves : an attempt to defeat your opponents by putting pressure on them so that they lose courage or confidence ; The union has been fighting a war of nerves with the management over pay.

Definition: a hollow place or structure that a bird makes or chooses for laying its eggs in and sheltering its young | a place where insects or other small creatures live and produce their young | a secret place which is full of bad people and their activities | the home, thought of as the safe place where parents bring up their children | a group or set of similar things that are made to fit inside each other | to make and use a nest | to put types of information together, or inside each other, so that they form a single unit

Idioms: feather your (own) nest : to make yourself richer, especially at somebody else's expense, by spending money on yourself that should be spent on somebody/something else | fly the nest : to become able to fly and leave its nest | fly the nest : to leave home and live somewhere else ; Her children have flown the nest. | a hornets’ nest : a difficult situation in which a lot of people get very angry ; His letter to the papers stirred up a real hornets' nest . | a mare’s nest : a discovery that seems interesting but is found to have no value | a mare’s nest : a very complicated situation ; This area of the law is a veritable mare’s nest.

Definition: a small quick movement of the head down and up again | if you nod , nod your head or your head nods , you move your head up and down to show agreement, understanding, etc. | to move your head down and up once to say hello or goodbye to somebody or to give them a sign to do something | to move your head in the direction of somebody/something to show that you are talking about them/it | to let your head fall forward when you are sleeping in a chair

Idioms: be at odds (with somebody) (over/on something) : to disagree with somebody about something ; He's always at odds with his father over politics. | be at odds (with something) : to be different from something, when the two things should be the same ; These findings are at odds with what is going on in the rest of the country. | the cards/odds are stacked against you : you are unlikely to succeed because the conditions are not good for you | the cards/odds are stacked in your favour : you are likely to succeed because the conditions are good and you have an advantage | it makes no odds : used to say that something is not important ; It makes no odds to me whether you go or stay. | over the odds : more money than you would normally expect ; Many collectors are willing to pay over the odds for early examples of his work.

Definition: an amount of money paid regularly by the government or a private company to a person who does not work any more because they are too old or have become ill : | a sum of money paid regularly to a person who has retired (= stopped working because of having reached a certain age ) | a regular income paid by a government or a financial organization to someone who no longer works , usually because of their age or health :
Example: a government pension |  | They receive a generous pension, typically 75% of last pay drawn .

Definition: to understand or think of somebody/something in a particular way | to notice or become aware of something
Example: This discovery was perceived as a major breakthrough. | I perceived a change in his behaviour.

Definition: an idea, a belief or an image you have as a result of how you see or understand something | the way you notice things, especially with the senses | the ability to understand the true nature of something
Example: a campaign to change public perception of the police | Everyone's perception of reality is slightly different. | She showed great perception in her assessment of the family situation.

Definition: lasting for a long time or for all time in the future; existing all the time | a perm (= a way of changing the style of your hair by using chemicals to create curls that last for several months)
Example: She was unable to find a permanent job. |

Definition: in a way that lasts for a long time or for all time in the future; in a way that exists all the time

Definition: an official document that gives somebody the right to do something, especially for a limited period of time | to allow somebody to do something or to allow something to happen | to make something possible

Definition: to continue to do something despite difficulties or opposition, in a way that can seem unreasonable | to continue to exist

Definition: determined to do something despite difficulties, especially when other people are against you and think that you are being annoying or unreasonable | continuing for a long period of time, or repeated frequently, especially in a way that is annoying and cannot be stopped
Example: How do you deal with persistent salesmen who won't take no for an answer? | Persistent heavy rain held up work on the bridge for more than a week.

Definition: the people who work for an organization or one of the armed forces | the department in a company that deals with employing and training people

Definition: a particular attitude towards something; a way of thinking about something | the ability to think about problems and decisions in a reasonable way without making them seem worse or more important than they really are | the art of creating an effect of depth and distance in a picture by representing people and things that are far away as being smaller than those that are nearer the front | a view, especially one in which you can see far into the distance

Definition: a written document signed by a large number of people that asks somebody in a position of authority to do or change something | an official document asking a court to take a particular course of action | a formal prayer to God or request to somebody in authority | to make a formal request to somebody in authority, especially by sending them a petition | to formally ask for something in court

Definition: a fact or an event in nature or society, especially one that is not fully understood | a person or thing that is very successful or impressive

Definition: connected with philosophy | having a calm attitude towards a difficult or disappointing situation

Definition: a doctor, especially one who is a specialist in general medicine and not surgery

Definition: a number of things that have been placed on top of each other | a mass of something that is high in the middle and wider at the bottom than at the top | a lot of something | the short threads , pieces of wool, etc. that form the soft surface of carpets and some types of cloth such as velvet | a large wooden, metal or stone post that is fixed into the ground and used to support a building, bridge, etc. | a large impressive building | to put things one on top of another; to form a pile | to put something on/into something; to load something with something | to go somewhere quickly without order or control
Example: a pile of clothes/paper | piles of dirty washing | He made a pile of cash on the sale of his house. | a deep-pile carpet |  | a Victorian pile built as a private hospital | She piled the boxes one on top of the other . | The sofa was piled high with cushions. | The coach finally arrived and we all piled on.
Idioms: (at the) bottom/top of the pile : in the least/most important position in a group of people or things ; It’s been 20 years since a British player was top of the pile. ; The government is doing little to help those at the bottom of the social pile. | make a/your pile : to make a lot of money ; The family made its pile from oil. ; I bet they made an absolute pile out of the deal. | pile on the agony/gloom : to make an unpleasant situation worse ; Bosses piled on the agony with threats of more job losses.

Definition: a person who is the first to study and develop a particular area of knowledge, culture, etc. that other people then continue to develop | one of the first people to go to a particular place, especially in order to live and work there | when somebody pioneers something, they are one of the first people to do, discover or use something new

Definition: an area of ground specially prepared and marked for playing a sports game | how high or low a sound is, especially a musical note | the degree or strength of a feeling or activity; the highest point of something | talk or arguments used by a person trying to sell something or persuade people to do something | an act of throwing the ball; the way in which it is thrown | a black sticky substance made from oil or coal, used on roofs or the wooden boards of a ship to stop water from coming through | a place in a street or market where somebody sells things, or where somebody performs in order to entertain people outdoors | a place in a campsite where you can put up one tent or park one caravan , etc. | the movement of a ship up and down in the water or of an aircraft in the air | the degree to which a roof slopes | to throw somebody/something with force | to throw the ball to the person who is batting | to hit the ground; to make the ball hit the ground | to hit the ball in a high curve | to fall heavily in a particular direction | to move up and down on the water or in the air | to set something at a particular level | to aim or direct a product or service at a particular group of people | to try to persuade somebody to buy something, to give you something or to make a business deal with you | to produce a sound or piece of music at a particular level | to set up a tent or a camp for a short time
Example: a football pitch | A basic sense of rhythm and pitch is essential in a music teacher. | a frenetic pitch of activity | an aggressive sales pitch |  |  |  | Pitches to rent from only £15 per night. |  | The pitch of the roof is 45 degrees. | The explosion pitched her violently into the air. | He pitched against UCLA last week. | The ball pitched a yard short of the hole. |  | With a cry she pitched forward. | The sea was rough and the ship pitched and rolled all night. | They have pitched their prices too high. | The new software is being pitched at banks. | Representatives went to Japan to pitch the company's newest products. | You pitched that note a little flat. | We could pitch our tent in that field.

Definition: used to show that you are disappointed about something | a sad feeling caused by the pain and troubles of others | to feel sorry for somebody because of their situation; to feel pity for somebody

Definition: to move or make somebody/something move suddenly forwards and/or downwards | to decrease suddenly and quickly | to slope down steeply | to move up and down suddenly and violently | a sudden movement downwards or away from something | a sudden decrease in an amount or the value of something | the act of becoming involved in a situation or activity | an act of jumping or diving into water; a quick swim

Definition: to give a religious talk in a public place, especially in a church during a service | to tell people about a particular religion, way of life, system, etc. in order to persuade them to accept it | to give somebody advice on moral standards, behaviour, etc., especially in a way that they find annoying or boring
Example: She preached to the congregation about forgiveness. | to preach the word of God | I'm sorry, I didn't mean to preach.

Definition: rare and worth a lot of money | valuable or important and not to be wasted | loved or valued very much | used to show you are angry that another person thinks something is very important | very formal, exaggerated and not natural in what you say and do | used to emphasize the fact that there is very little of something or that there are very few of something

Definition: exactly | accurately; carefully | used to emphasize that something is very true or obvious | used to emphasize that you agree with a statement, especially because you think it is obvious or is similar to what you have just said

Definition: most important, famous or successful | used especially in newspapers, etc. to mean ‘prime minister’ | (in Canada) the first minister of a province or territory

Definition: the fact of being in a particular place | the fact of being in a particular place or thing | a group of people, especially soldiers, who have been sent to a place to deal with a particular situation | a person or spirit that you cannot see but that you feel is near | the quality of making a strong impression on other people by the way you talk or behave; a person who has this quality
Example: He hardly seemed to notice my presence. | The test can identify the presence of abnormalities in the unborn child. | The government is maintaining a heavy police presence in the area. | She felt a presence behind her. | a man of great presence
Idioms: in the presence of somebody | in somebody’s presence : with somebody in the same place ; The document was signed in the presence of two witnesses. ; She asked them not to discuss the matter in her presence. ; He should never have made those remarks in your presence. | in the presence of something : when something exists in a particular place ; Litmus paper turns red in the presence of an acid. | make your presence felt : to do something to make people very aware of the fact that you are there; to have a strong influence on a group of people or a situation ; She’s only been here a couple of weeks but she is already making her presence felt. ; He certainly made his presence felt in the boardroom.
Synonym: physical being | physical being | physical being | physical being | physical being | physical being | physical being | physical being

Definition: at the time you are speaking or writing; now | used to show that something happened after a short time | used to show that something will happen soon
Example: The crime is presently being investigated by the police. | Presently, the door opened again and three men stepped out. | She'll be here presently.

Definition: the act of keeping something in its original state or in good condition | the act of making sure that something is kept | the degree to which something has not been changed or damaged by age, weather, etc.
Example: building/environmental/food preservation | The central issue in the strike was the preservation of jobs. | The paintings were in an excellent state of preservation.

Definition: to keep a particular quality, feature, etc.; to make sure that something is kept | to keep something in its original state in good condition | to prevent something, especially food, from decaying (= being destroyed by natural processes) by treating it in a particular way | to keep somebody/something alive, or safe from harm or danger | an activity, a job, an interest, etc. that is thought to be suitable for one particular person or group of people | a type of jam made by boiling fruit with a large amount of sugar | a type of pickle made by cooking vegetables with salt or vinegar | a piece of land that is a protected area for animals, plants, etc. | an area of private land or water where animals and fish are kept for people to hunt
Example: He was anxious to preserve his reputation. | a perfectly preserved 14th-century house | Wax polish preserves wood and leather. | The society was set up to preserve endangered species from extinction. | Football is no longer the preserve of men. |  |  |  | He bought a 171-acre hunting preserve in upstate New York.
Synonym: keep safe | keep safe | keep safe | keep safe

Definition: to lead or be in charge of a meeting, ceremony, etc.
Example: the presiding judge

Definition: the job of being president of a country or an organization; the period of time somebody holds this job
Example: the current holder of the EU presidency

Definition: connected with the position or activities of a president
Example: a presidential campaign/candidate/election

Definition: respected and admired as very important or of very high quality
Example: a prestigious award

Definition: used to say that you think that something is probably true
Example: Presumably this is where the accident happened.

Definition: to suppose that something is true, although you do not have actual proof | to accept that something is true until it is shown not to be true, especially in court | to accept something as true or existing and to act on that basis | to behave in a way that shows a lack of respect by doing something that you have no right to do
Example: They are very expensive, I presume? | Twelve passengers are missing, presumed dead . | The course seems to presume some previous knowledge of the subject. | I wouldn't presume to tell you how to run your own business.

Definition: to exist or be very common at a particular time or in a particular place | to be accepted, especially after a struggle or an argument | to defeat an opponent, especially after a long struggle
Example: We were horrified at the conditions prevailing in local prisons. | Justice will prevail over tyranny. | In a one-sided final, Spain prevailed against title-holder Croatia 40–34.

Definition: the fact of existing or being very common at a particular time or in a particular place
Example: an increase in the prevalence of obesity in many countries

Definition: the act of stopping something bad from happening
Example: accident/crime prevention
Idioms: prevention is better than cure : it is better to stop something bad from happening rather than try to deal with the problems after it has happened ; Prevention is better than cure, so start looking after your heart now. ; Prevention is better than cure, so start taking care of yourself.

Definition: an animal, a bird, etc. that is hunted, killed and eaten by another | a person who is harmed or tricked by somebody, especially for dishonest purposes
Example: The lion will often stalk its prey for hours. | Socially isolated young people are easy prey for extremists.
Idioms: be/fall prey to something : to be killed and eaten by another animal or bird ; Many small birds and rodents fall prey to the domestic cat. | be/fall prey to something : to be harmed or affected by something bad ; Since the attack, he had fallen prey to irrational fears. ; She knew she must not fall prey to his charm. ; The new government has fallen prey to corruption and fraud. | prey on somebody’s mind : to make somebody think and worry about it all the time ; The thought that he could have helped more preyed on his mind. ; It's been preying on my mind ever since it happened.

Definition: a feeling of being pleased or satisfied that you get when you or people who are connected with you have done something well or own something that other people admire | a person or thing that makes people feel pleased or satisfied | the feeling of respect that you have for yourself | the feeling that you are better or more important than other people | a group of lions
Example: The sight of her son graduating filled her with pride. | The new sports stadium is the pride of the town. | Pride would not allow him to accept the money. | She was full of pride and arrogance and despised ordinary people. | This pride of lions is/are resting after a hunt.
Idioms: somebody’s pride and joy : a person or thing that causes somebody to feel very pleased or satisfied ; The car is her pride and joy. ; He loves that boat—it's his pride and joy. | pride comes/goes before a fall : if you have too high an opinion of yourself or your abilities, something will happen to make you look stupid | pride of place : the position in which something is most easily seen, that is given to the most important thing in a particular group ; The photo was given pride of place on the mantelpiece.
Synonym: be proud of | be proud of | be proud of | be proud of | be proud of | be proud of | satisfaction | satisfaction

Definition: mainly
Example: a course designed primarily for specialists

Definition: main; most important; basic | of the best quality; excellent | a prime example of something is one that is typical of it | most likely to be chosen for something; most suitable
Example: My prime concern is to protect my property. | prime (cuts of) beef | The building is a prime example of 1960s architecture. | The house is isolated and a prime target for burglars.

Definition: most important; main | the person who is in charge of a college or (in Scotland and Canada) a university | a teacher who is in charge of a school | an amount of money that you lend to somebody or invest to earn interest | the person who has the most important part in a play, an opera , etc. | a person that you are representing, especially in business or law
Example: The principal reason for this omission is lack of time. | Peter Brown, principal of St John’s College | Principal Ray Smith | The principal must be repaid in full within ten years. | The performance of the two principals is disappointing. | The shareholders are principals and the managers are agents.
Synonym: original sum | chief | head of a school | head of a school | head of a school | chief

Definition: a moral rule or a strong belief that influences your actions | a law, a rule or a theory that something is based on | a belief that is accepted as a reason for acting or thinking in a particular way | a general or scientific law that explains how something works or why something happens
Example: He has high moral principles . | The same principles apply to both humans and animals. | The welfare of the child is the guiding principle of the family courts. | O'Sullivan was among the first chemists to apply scientific principles to brewing beer.
Idioms: in principle : if something can be done in principle , there is no good reason why it should not be done although it has not yet been done and there may be some difficulties ; In principle there is nothing that a human can do that a machine might not be able to do one day. | in principle : in general but not in detail ; They have agreed to the proposal in principle but we still have to negotiate the terms. ; I agree with you in principle, but we'll need to discuss the details.

Definition: happening or existing before something else or before a particular time | already existing and therefore more important | before something | a person who is in charge of a group of monks or nuns living in a priory | (in an abbey ) a person next in rank below an abbot or abbess
Example: Although not essential, some prior knowledge of statistics is desirable. | They have a prior claim to the property. | during the week prior to the meeting |  |

Definition: something that you think is more important than other things and should be dealt with first | the most important place among various things that have to be done or among a group of people | the right of a vehicle to go before other traffic at a particular place on a road
Example: a high/low priority | Club members will be given priority . | Buses have priority at this junction.

Definition: the state of being alone and not watched or interrupted by other people | the state of being free from the attention of the public
Example: She was longing for some peace and privacy. | freedom of speech and the right to privacy

Definition: the act of selling a business or an industry so that it is no longer owned by the government
Example: There were fears that privatization would lead to job losses.

Definition: a special right or advantage that a particular person or group of people has | the rights and advantages that rich and powerful people in a society have | something that you are proud and lucky to have the opportunity to do | a special right to do or say things without being punished | to give somebody/something special rights or advantages that others do not have
Example: Education should be a universal right and not a privilege. | As a member of the nobility, his life had been one of wealth and privilege. | I hope to have the privilege of working with them again. | parliamentary privilege (= the special right of members of parliament to say particular things without risking legal action) | education policies that privilege the children of wealthy parents

Definition: how likely something is to happen | a thing that is likely to happen | a ratio showing the chances that a particular thing will happen
Example: The probability is that prices will rise rapidly. | A fall in interest rates is a strong probability in the present economic climate. | There is a 60 per cent probability that the population will be infected with the disease.
Idioms: (on) the balance of evidence/probability : (considering) the evidence on both sides of an argument, to find the most likely reason for or result of something ; The balance of evidence suggests the Liberal party's decline began before the First World War. ; The coroner thought that on the balance of probabilities, the pilot had suffered a stroke just before the crash. | (on) the balance of probability/evidence : (considering) the evidence on both sides of an argument, to find the most likely reason for or result of something ; The coroner thought that on the balance of probabilities, the pilot had suffered a stroke just before the crash. | in all probability… : it is very likely that ; In all probability he failed to understand the consequences of his actions. ; In all probability she wouldn't come even if we invited her.
Synonym: likelihood | chance | likelihood | likelihood | likelihood | chance

Definition: likely to happen, to exist or to be true | a person or an animal that is likely to win a race or to be chosen for a team
Example: the probable cause/explanation/outcome | All four players are probables for the national team.

Definition: a complete and careful investigation of something | a spacecraft without people on board that obtains information and sends it back to earth | a long, thin metal tool used by doctors for examining inside the body | a small device put inside something and used by scientists to test something or record information | to ask questions in order to find out secret or hidden information about somebody/something | to touch, examine or look for something, especially with a long, thin instrument
Example: a police probe into the financial affairs of the company |  |  |  | He didn't like the media probing into his past. | The doctor probed the wound for signs of infection.

Definition: difficult to deal with or to understand; full of problems; not certain to be successful

Definition: a way of doing something, especially the usual or correct way | the official or formal order or way of doing something, especially in business, law or politics | a medical operation

Definition: the money that you receive when you sell something or organize a performance, etc.; profits
Example: She sold her car and bought a piano with the proceeds.

Definition: a series of actions that you take in order to achieve a result : | a series of changes that happen naturally : | a method of producing goods in a factory by treating natural substances : | a part inside the body that grows out from another part or is attached to another part : | to deal with documents in an official way: | If a computer processes information , it performs a particular series of operations on the information , such as a set of calculations . | to prepare , change , or treat food or natural substances as a part of an industrial operation : | to think about a difficult or sad situation so that you can gradually accept it: | to make pictures from photographic film : | to walk slowly : | a series of actions or events performed to make something or achieve a particular result , or a series of changes that happen naturally : | A process is also a method of doing or making something, as in industry : | to deal with something according to a particular set of actions : | If a computer processes information , it performs a series of operations on it. | To process food or raw materials is to prepare , change , or treat them as part of an industrial operation . | a series of actions that are needed in order to do something or achieve a result : | a method of producing goods in a particular industry : | to have started doing something: | if something is happening , and something else happens in the process, the second thing happens as a result of the first: | to change raw materials , for example by adding chemicals or other substances to them, as part of the production of goods : | to deal with documents in an official way: | if a computer processes data , it uses a set of instructions to organize it and produce a particular result :
Example: the peace process | the digestive process | They have developed a new process for extracting aluminium from bauxite . | The xiphoid process is a small , bony projection at the lower end of the breastbone . | Visa applications take 28 days to process. |  | a waste processing plant | Returning soldiers need time to process what they have experienced in combat . | I need to get those films processed. | We watched them process down the aisle . | Completing his degree at night was a long process. | A new process has been developed for removing asbestos . | Your insurance claim will take about a month to process. |  |  | Management recognizes it is important to get the process right even if it means delaying the project start date . | an industrial / manufacturing / production process | We're in the process of reviewing the court's decision and evaluating our response . | A clash with their main supplier means they won't be able to honour their contracts and will lose millions of pounds in the process. | The two new oil refineries will be able to process 250,000 barrels of crude a day . | Insurance claims may take up to 30 days to process. | A bug in the system was preventing it from processing digital image files .

Definition: the treatment of raw material, food, etc. in order to change it, preserve it, etc. | the process of dealing officially with a document, request, etc.

Definition: a move to a more important job or rank in a company or an organization | a move by a sports team from playing in one group of teams to playing in a better group | activities done in order to increase the sales of a product or service; a set of advertisements for a particular product or service | activity that encourages people to believe in the value or importance of something, or that helps something to succeed

Definition: to make somebody decide to do something; to cause something to happen | to encourage somebody to speak by asking them questions or suggesting words that they could say | to follow the text of a play and remind the actors what the words are if they forget their lines | done without delay | acting without delay; arriving at the right time | a word or words said to an actor, to remind them what to say next when they have forgotten | a sign on a computer screen that shows that the computer has finished doing something and is ready for more instructions | an instruction given to an artificial intelligence program to tell it what you want it to produce | exactly at the time mentioned

Definition: very obvious, easy to notice or strongly expressed

Definition: information, documents, etc. that show that something is true | the process of testing whether something is true or a fact | a way of proving that a statement is true or that what you have calculated is correct | a copy of printed material which is produced so that mistakes can be corrected | a standard used to measure the strength of alcoholic drinks
Example: conclusive/definitive/scientific proof | Is the claim capable of proof? |  | She was checking the proofs of her latest novel. |
Idioms: be living proof of something/that… : to show by your actions or qualities that a particular fact is true ; These figures are living proof of the government's incompetence. ; He is living proof that age is no barrier to new challenges. | the proof of the pudding (is in the eating) | the proof is in the pudding : you can only judge if something is good or bad when you have tried it

Definition: ideas or statements that may be false or present only one side of an argument that are used in order to gain support for a political leader, party, etc.

Definition: a part or share of a whole | the relationship of one thing to another in size, amount, etc. | the correct relationship in size, degree, importance, etc. between one thing and another or between the parts of a whole | the measurements of something; its size and shape | the equal relationship between two pairs of numbers, as in the statement ‘4 is to 8 as 6 is to 12’

Only a small number of young people act in this way. | out of (all) proportion (to something) : larger, more serious, etc. in relation to something than is necessary or appropriate ; They earn salaries out of all proportion to their ability. ; The media have blown the incident up out of all proportion.

Definition: a formal suggestion or plan; the act of making a suggestion | an act of formally asking somebody to marry you

Definition: to suggest a plan, an idea, etc. for people to think about and decide on | to intend to do something | to ask somebody to marry you | to suggest something at a formal meeting and ask people to vote on it | to suggest an explanation of something for people to consider

Definition: an idea or a plan of action that is suggested, especially in business | a thing that you intend to do; a problem, task or person to be dealt with | (in the US) a suggested change to the law that people can vote on | a statement that expresses an opinion | a statement of a theorem , and an explanation of how it can be proved | to say in a direct way to somebody that you would like to have sex with them

Definition: to make water, air, gas, etc. flow in a particular direction by using a pump or something that works like a pump | to flow in a particular direction as if it is being forced by a pump | to move something quickly up and down or in and out | to move quickly up and down or in and out | to try to get information from somebody by asking them a lot of questions | a machine that is used to force liquid, gas or air into or out of something | a light simple sports shoe made of canvas (= strong cotton cloth) with a rubber sole | a woman’s formal shoe that is plain and does not cover the top part of the foot | a woman’s light, soft flat shoe worn for dancing or exercise; a similar style of shoe worn as a fashion item

Definition: an official design or mark, stamped on a document to show that it is real and carries the authority of a particular person or organization | a thing that makes something definite | a substance, piece of material, etc. used to fill a small opening so that air, liquid, etc. cannot get in or out | a piece of wax (= a soft substance produced by bees), soft metal or paper that is placed across the opening of something such as a letter or box and which has to be broken before the letter or box can be opened | a piece of metal, a ring, etc. with a design on it, used for stamping a wax or metal seal | a sea animal that eats fish and lives around coasts. Definition: the dark shape that somebody/something’s form makes on a surface, for example on the ground, when they are between the light and the surface | darkness in a place or on something, especially so that you cannot easily see who or what is there | a very small amount of something | the strong (often bad) influence of somebody/something | dark areas under somebody’s eyes, because they are tired, etc. | a person or an animal that follows somebody else all the time | a weaker or worse version of somebody/something | a thing that is not real or possible to obtain | to follow and watch somebody closely and often secretly | to be with somebody who is doing a particular job, so that you can learn about it | to cover something with a shadow
