Definition: the act of firing a gun; the sound this makes | a person who shoots a gun in a particular way (well, badly, etc.) | an attempt to score a goal or point in a game | an act of hitting the ball | the heavy ball that is used in the sports competition called shot-put | a photograph | a scene in a film that is filmed continuously by one camera | a large number of small metal balls that you fire together from a shotgun | a large stone or metal ball that was shot from a cannon or large gun in the past | a remark or an action that is usually one of a series, and is aimed against somebody/something that you are arguing or competing with | the act of trying to do or achieve something | a small amount of a drug that is put into your body using a syringe | a small amount of a drink, especially a strong alcoholic one | the process of sending a spacecraft into and through space | a horse, dog, etc. that has the particular chance of winning a race that is mentioned
Example: We heard some shots in the distance. | She is a crack shot with a rifle. | He took a shot from the edge of the box. | Good shot! |  | We stood there patiently while he snapped a few shots. | the opening shot of a character walking across a desert | Round shot whistled over our heads. |  | This statement was the opening shot in the argument. | The team are looking good for a shot at the title. | a flu shot (= to protect you against flu) | a shot of whisky | The space shot was shown live on television. | The horse is a 10–1 shot.
Idioms: a big noise/shot/name : an important person | call the shots/tune : to be the person who controls a situation | like a shot : very quickly and without hesitating ; If I had the chance to go there, I'd go like a shot. | a long shot : an attempt or a guess that is not likely to be successful but is worth trying ; It's a long shot, but it just might work. | not by a long shot : not nearly; not at all ; It's not over yet—not by a long shot. | parting shot : a final remark, especially an unkind one, that somebody makes as they leave ; As her parting shot she warned Pete never to come near her again. | a shot across the/somebody’s bows : something that you say or do as a warning to somebody about what might happen if they do not change, etc. | a shot in the arm : something that encourages somebody/something or gives them the help they need ; Their enthusiasm has been a shot in the arm for all of us. | a shot/stab in the dark : a guess; something you do without knowing what the result will be ; The figure he came up with was really just a shot in the dark. ; It was only a stab in the dark, but I hoped I might be right.

Definition: a psychiatrist or psychologist | to become smaller, especially when washed in water that is too hot; to make clothes, cloth, etc. smaller in this way | to become or to make something smaller in size or amount | to move back or away from something because you are frightened or shocked
Example: She sees her shrink once a week. | My sweater shrank in the wash. | The tumour had shrunk to the size of a pea. | He shrank back against the wall as he heard them approaching.
Idioms: a shrinking violet : a very shy person
Synonym: become smaller | become smaller | become smaller | become smaller

Definition: to raise your shoulders and then drop them to show that you do not know or care about something
Example: Sam shrugged and said nothing.

Definition: a brother or sister
Example: squabbles between siblings

Definition: to take and then let out a long deep breath that can be heard, to show that you are disappointed, sad, tired, etc. | to say something with a sigh | to make a long sound like a sigh | an act or the sound of sighing
Example: He sighed deeply at the thought. | ‘Oh well, better luck next time,’ she sighed. | the sighing of the wind through the trees | to give/heave/let out a sigh

Definition: your name as you usually write it, for example at the end of a letter | the act of signing something | a particular quality that makes something different from other similar things and makes it easy to recognize
Example: Someone had forged her signature on the cheque. | Two copies of the contract will be sent to you for signature. | Bright colours are his signature.

Definition: the importance of something, especially when this has an effect on what happens in the future | the meaning of something
Example: a decision of major political significance | She couldn't grasp the full significance of what he had said.

Definition: large or important enough to have an effect or to be noticed | having a particular meaning | having a special or secret meaning that is not understood by everyone
Example: There are no significant differences between the two groups of students. | The fact that her remarks were leaked to the media in advance is highly significant . | a significant look/smile

Definition: in a way that is large or important enough to have an effect on something or to be noticed | in a way that has a particular meaning | in a way that has a special or secret meaning
Example: significantly higher/lower/larger/smaller | Significantly, he did not deny that there might be an election. | She paused significantly before she answered.

Definition: used to tell people to be quiet | a complete lack of noise or sound | a situation when nobody is speaking | a situation in which somebody refuses to talk about something or to answer questions | a situation in which people do not communicate with each other by letter or phone
Example: Silence in court! | Their footsteps echoed in the silence. | an embarrassed/awkward/uncomfortable silence | She broke her public silence in a TV interview. | The phone call came after months of silence.
Idioms: a heavy silence/atmosphere : a situation when people do not say anything, but feel embarrassed or uncomfortable | a pregnant pause/silence : an occasion when nobody speaks, although people are aware that there are feelings or thoughts to express ; There was a pregnant pause before she replied. | silence is golden : it is often best not to say anything

Definition: fine soft thread produced by silkworms | a type of fine smooth cloth made from silk thread ; a piece of this cloth | thread made of silk used for sewing | clothes made of silk , especially the coloured shirts worn by people riding horses in a race (= jockeys ) | a type of lawyer who represents the government (= a King’s Counsel / Queen’s Counsel )
Example: The caterpillar spins the silk around its entire body. | a silk dress/blouse/scarf/tie/sari |  | ladies in silks and satins | to take silk (= to become this type of lawyer)
Idioms: (you can't) make a silk purse out of a sow’s ear : (you won't) succeed in making something good out of material that does not seem very good at all

Definition: to create particular conditions that exist in real life using computers, models, etc., usually for study or training purposes | to be made to look like something else | to pretend that you have a particular feeling
Example: Computer software can be used to simulate conditions on the seabed. | a gas heater that simulates a coal fire | I tried to simulate surprise at the news.

Definition: a situation in which a particular set of conditions is created artificially in order to study or experience something that could exist in reality | the act of pretending that something is real when it is not
Example: a computer simulation of how the planet functions | the simulation of concern

Definition: at the same time as something else
Example: The game will be broadcast simultaneously on TV and radio.

Tags: Oxford_5000 North_American_English informal especially_British_English humorous often_humorous old-fashioned_or_humorous
Definition: an offence against God or against a religious or moral law | the act of breaking a religious or moral law | an action that people think is very bad | to break a religious or moral law | sine
Example: to commit a sin | a life of sin | It's a sin to waste taxpayers' money like that. | Forgive me, Lord, for I have sinned. |
Idioms: be/do something for your sins : used to say that something that somebody does is like a punishment ; She works with us in Accounts, for her sins! | cover/hide a multitude of sins : to hide the real situation or facts when these are not good or pleasant ; It's a shabby old sofa but cushions and throws can hide a multitude of sins. | live in sin : to live together and have a sexual relationship without being married | (as) miserable/ugly as sin : used to emphasize that somebody is very unhappy or ugly

Definition: showing what you really think or feel | saying only what you really think or feel
Example: a sincere apology | He seemed sincere enough when he said he wanted to help.

Definition: in a particular place or position | in a particular situation or in particular circumstances
Example: My bedroom was situated on the top floor of the house. | Small businesses are well situated to benefit from the single market.

Tags: Oxford_5000 US_English especially_British_English North_American_English_usually British_English North_American_English Art Film_and_theatre TV,_radio_and_news Literature_and_writing
Definition: a simple picture that is drawn quickly and does not have many details | a short funny scene on television, in the theatre, etc. | a short report or story that gives only basic details about something | to make a quick drawing of somebody/something | to give a general description of something, giving only the basic facts
Example: The artist is making sketches for his next painting. | The drama group did a sketch about a couple buying a new car. | a biographical sketch of the prime minister | He quickly sketched the view from the window. | She sketched out her plan for tackling the problem.

Definition: having enough ability, experience and knowledge to be able to do something well | needing special abilities or training
Example: a skilled engineer/negotiator/craftsman | Furniture-making is very skilled work.

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English North_American_English especially_North_American_English figurative informal British_English_also Education
Definition: a skipping movement | a large open container for putting old, broken building materials, rubbish, etc. in. The skip is then loaded on a lorry and taken away. | to move forwards lightly and quickly making a little jump with each step | to jump over a rope which is held at both ends by yourself or by two other people and is passed again and again over your head and under your feet | to not do something that you usually do or should do | to leave out something that would normally be the next thing that you would do, read, etc. | to move from one place to another or from one subject to another very quickly | to leave a place secretly or suddenly | to make a flat stone jump across the surface of water
Example: She gave a skip and a jump and was off down the street. |  | She skipped happily along beside me. | He skips for about 20 minutes a day. | I often skip breakfast altogether. | You can skip the next chapter if you have covered the topic in class. | She kept skipping from one topic of conversation to another. | The bombers skipped the country shortly after the blast. | The boys were skipping stones across the pond.
Idioms: somebody’s heart misses/skips a beat : used to say that somebody has a sudden feeling of fear, excitement, etc. ; My heart missed a beat when I saw who it was. | jump/skip bail : to fail to appear at your trial after you have paid money to be allowed to go free until the trial ; He skipped bail and went on the run for two weeks. | skip it : used to tell somebody rudely that you do not want to talk about something or repeat what you have said ; ‘What were you saying?’ ‘Oh, skip it!’

Definition: the bone structure that forms the head and surrounds and protects the brain | the head or the brain
Example: a fractured skull | Her skull was crammed with too many thoughts.

Definition: an act of slamming something; the noise of something being slammed | a competition in which people perform their poems to an audience, which chooses a winner | a set of very important competitions in a particular sport in the same year in tennis, golf or rugby; one of the individual competitions that make up a Grand Slam | to shut, or to make something shut, with a lot of force, making a loud noise | to put, push or throw something into a particular place or position with a lot of force | to crash into something with a lot of force; to make somebody/something crash into something with a lot of force | to criticize somebody/something very strongly
Example: She gave the door a good hard slam. | A poetry slam with cash prizes will be among the social events. | She won her first slam with a dominant final. | I heard the door slam behind him. | She slammed down the phone angrily. | The car skidded and slammed into a tree. | The government has been slammed for failing to take firm action against drinking and driving.
Idioms: shut/slam the door in somebody’s face : to shut a door hard when somebody is trying to come in | shut/slam the door in somebody’s face : to refuse to talk to somebody or meet them, in a rude way
Synonym: bang | slap | bang | close | bang | bang

Definition: straight, and with great force | exactly | the action of hitting somebody/something with the flat part of your hand | the noise made by hitting somebody/something with the flat part of your hand; a similar noise made by something else | make-up (= coloured substances used on the skin to make yourself look more attractive or to change your appearance) | to hit somebody/something with the flat part of your hand | to put something on a surface in a quick, careless and often noisy way, especially because you are angry | to hit against something with the noise of somebody being slapped
Example: Storming out of her room, she went slap into Luke. | Their apartment is slap bang in the middle of town. | She gave him a slap across the face. | the gentle slap of water against the shore |  | She slapped his face hard. | He slapped the newspaper down on the desk. | The water slapped against the side of the boat.
Idioms: slap and tickle : enthusiastic kissing and cuddling between lovers | a slap in the face : an action that seems to be intended as a deliberate way of offending and showing lack of respect for somebody ; The closure of the school is a slap in the face to the local community. | a slap on the wrist : a warning or mild punishment ; We're hoping that she gets off with a slap on the wrist from the judge.

Definition: a sharp movement made with a knife, etc. in order to cut somebody/something | a long narrow wound or cut | the symbol (/) used to show alternatives, as in lunch and/or dinner and 4/5 people and to write fractions , as in ¾ | an act of urinating | to make a long cut with a sharp object, especially in a violent way | to reduce something by a large amount
Example: | a slash across his right cheek | He brought his girlfriend-slash-partner. | He's just nipped out to have a slash. | Someone had slashed the tyres on my car. | to slash spending/prices/costs

Definition: a person who is owned by another person and is forced to work for and obey them | a person who is so strongly influenced by something that they cannot live without it, or cannot make their own decisions | a device that is directly controlled by another one | to work very hard
Example: A former slave , he graduated from Claflin University in South Carolina. | We are slaves of the motor car. |  | I've been slaving away all day trying to get this work finished.

Definition: the state of being forced to work as a slave | the practice of owning and forcing people to work as slaves
Example: to be sold into slavery | the abolition of slavery

Definition: one page of an electronic presentation, that may contain text and images, that is usually viewed on a computer screen or projected onto a larger screen | a long, smooth movement on ice or a smooth surface | a change to a lower or worse condition | a structure with a steep slope that children use for sliding down | a sudden fall of a large amount of rock or earth down a hill | a small piece of film held in a frame that can be shown on a screen when you shine a light through it | a small piece of glass that something is placed on so that it can be looked at under a microscope | a part of a musical instrument or other device that slides backwards and forwards | a small attractive piece of metal or plastic used by women for holding their hair in place | to move easily over a smooth or wet surface; to make something move in this way | to move quickly and quietly, for example in order not to be noticed; to make something move in this way | to become gradually lower or of less value | to move gradually into a worse situation
Example: Here's a slide showing target markets. | Her car went into a slide. | a downward slide in the price of oil | to go down the slide | I was afraid of starting a slide of loose stones. | a talk with colour slides |  |  |  | As I turned left on a bend, my car started to slide. | He slid into bed. | Shares slid to a 10-year low. | The industry has slid into decline.

Definition: very small in degree | small and thin in size | not deserving serious attention | an act or a remark that criticizes something or offends somebody | to treat somebody rudely or without respect
Example: a slight increase/change/difference | a slight woman | This is a very slight novel. | Nick took her comment as a slight on his abilities as a manager. | She felt slighted because she hadn't been invited.
Idioms: not in the slightest : not at all ; He didn't seem to mind in the slightest. ; I’m not in the slightest bit interested.

Definition: a small mistake, usually made by being careless or not paying attention | a small piece of paper, especially one for writing on or with something printed on it | an act of slipping | a piece of underwear like a thin dress or skirt, worn under a dress | a player who stands behind and to one side of the batter and tries to catch the ball; the position on the field where this player stands | to slide a short distance by accident so that you fall or nearly fall | to slide out of position or out of your hand | to go somewhere quickly and quietly, especially without being noticed | to put something somewhere quickly, quietly or secretly | to fall to a lower level; to become worse | to pass into a particular state or situation, especially a difficult or unpleasant one | to put clothes on or to take them off quickly and easily | to get free; to make something/somebody/yourself free from something
Example: He recited the whole poem without making a single slip. | I wrote it down on a slip of paper. | One slip and you could fall to your death. |  | fielding in the slips | She slipped and landed flat on her back. | My hand slipped as I was slicing the bread and I cut myself. | She slipped out of the house before the others were awake. | Anna slipped her hand into his. | Standards have slipped in the last few years. | He began to slip into debt. | to slip into/out of a dress | The ship had slipped its moorings in the night.
Idioms: give somebody the slip : to get away from somebody who is following or running after you ; I managed to give him the slip by hiding behind a wall. | a slip of a boy, girl, etc. : a small or thin, usually young, person ; I was only a slip of a thing at the time. | a slip of the pen/tongue : a small mistake in something that you write or say ; Did I call you Richard? Sorry, Robert, just a slip of the tongue. ; I didn't mean to say that—it was just a slip of the tongue. | there’s many a slip ’twixt cup and lip : nothing is completely certain until it really happens because things can easily go wrong | let slip something : to give somebody information that is supposed to be secret ; I happened to let it slip that he had given me £1 000 for the car. ; She tried not to let slip what she knew. | let something slip (through your fingers) : to miss or fail to use an opportunity ; Don't let the chance to work abroad slip through your fingers. | (slip/be thrown) out of gear : (to become) out of control ; She said nothing in case her temper slipped out of gear. | roll/slip/trip off the tongue : to be easy to say or pronounce ; It's not a name that exactly trips off the tongue, is it? | slip your mind : if something slips your mind , you forget it or forget to do it ; I’m sorry I didn’t tell you. It completely slipped my mind. | slip/roll/trip off the tongue : to be easy to say or pronounce ; It's not a name that exactly slips off the tongue, is it? | slip one over on somebody : to trick somebody | slip through the net : when somebody/something slips through the net , an organization or a system fails to find them and deal with them ; We tried to contact all former students, but one or two slipped through the net.

Definition: a word or phrase that is easy to remember, used for example by a political party or in advertising to attract people’s attention or to suggest an idea quickly
Example: an advertising slogan

Definition: a surface or piece of land that slopes (= is higher at one end than the other) | an area of land that is part of a mountain or hill | the amount by which something slopes | to be at an angle so that it is higher at one end than the other | to be at an angle rather than going straight up or straight across
Example: The town is built on a slope. | the eastern slopes of the Andes | a steep slope | a sloping roof | It was a very old house with sloping walls.
Idioms: the/a slippery slope : a course of action that is difficult to stop once it has begun, and can lead to serious problems or disaster ; She realized he was on the slippery slope towards a life of crime.

Definition: a long, narrow opening, into which you put or fit something | a position, a time or an opportunity for somebody/something, for example in a list, a programme of events or a series of broadcasts | to put something into a space that is available or designed for it; to fit into such a space
Example: to put some coins in the slot | He has a regular slot on the late-night programme. | The bed comes in sections that can be quickly slotted together.
Idioms: slot/fall into place : if something complicated or difficult to understand slots or falls into place , it becomes organized or clear in your mind ; Then I found his diary and it all began to slot into place.

Definition: an act of breaking something noisily into pieces; the sound this makes | an accident in which a vehicle hits another vehicle | a way of hitting the ball downwards and very hard | a song, film or play that is very popular | to break something, or to be broken, violently and noisily into many pieces | to move with a lot of force against something solid; to make something do this | to hit something very hard and break it, in order to get through it | to hit something/somebody very hard | to break a record by a large amount | to destroy, defeat or put an end to something/somebody | to crash a vehicle | to hit a high ball downwards and very hard over the net
Example: The cup hit the floor with a smash. | a car smash | He can hit a powerful overhead smash. | her latest chart smash | Several windows had been smashed. | the sound of waves smashing against the rocks | They had to smash holes in the ice. | He smashed the ball into the goal. | She has smashed the world record. | Police say they have smashed a major drugs ring. | He’s smashed (up) his new car. |
Idioms: smash it : to do something very well or be very successful ; After all that training, I’m sure he’ll smash it in the competition tonight.

Tags: Oxford_5000 informal North_American_English sport Feelings Sports:_ball_and_racket_sports
Definition: made or done quickly and without careful thought or preparation | you say snap! in the card game called ‘ Snap ’ when two cards that are the same are put down | people say snap! to show that they are surprised when two things are the same | a sudden sharp noise, especially one made by something closing or breaking | a photograph, especially one taken quickly | a card game in which players take turns to put cards down and try to be the first to call out ‘snap’ when two similar cards are put down together | a type of button used for fastening clothes, consisting of two metal or plastic sections that can be pressed together | to break something suddenly with a sharp noise; to be broken in this way | to take a photograph | to move, or to move something, into a particular position quickly, especially with a sudden sharp noise | to speak or say something in an impatient, usually angry, voice | to try to bite somebody/something | to suddenly be unable to control your feelings any longer because the situation has become too difficult | to fasten a piece of clothing with a snap | to start play by passing the ball back between your legs
Example: It was a snap decision . |  | Snap! I've just bought curtains exactly like those. | She closed her purse with a snap. | holiday snaps |  |  | The wind had snapped the tree in two. | A passing tourist snapped the incident. | The lid snapped shut. | ‘Don't just stand there,’ she snapped. | The dogs snarled and snapped at our heels. | My patience finally snapped. |  | Time will not be resumed until the ball is snapped on the next play.
Idioms: be a snap : to be very easy to do ; This job's a snap. | bite/snap somebody’s head off : to shout at somebody in an angry way, especially without reason | snap your fingers : to make a sharp noise by moving your second or third finger quickly against your thumb , to attract somebody’s attention, or to mark the beat of music, for example ; He snapped his fingers for the waiter. ; She only has to snap her fingers and her assistant comes running. | snap out of it/something | snap somebody out of it/something : to make an effort to stop feeling unhappy or depressed; to help somebody to stop feeling unhappy ; You've been depressed for weeks. It's time you snapped out of it. | snap to it : used, especially in orders, to tell somebody to start working harder or more quickly
Synonym: break | break | break | break | break | break | break | break | break | break | break | break

Definition: used to show that you do not think that the word or phrase that is being used to describe somebody/something is appropriate | used to introduce the word that people usually use to describe something
Example: the opinion of a so-called ‘expert’ | artists from the so-called ‘School of London’

Definition: an act of leaving something in a liquid for a period of time; an act of making somebody/something wet | a period of time spent in a bath | to put something in liquid for a time so that it becomes completely wet; to become completely wet in this way | to make somebody/something completely wet | to enter or pass through something | to obtain a lot of money from somebody by making them pay very high taxes or prices
Example: Give the shirt a good soak before you wash it. | Relax with a nice long soak in a hot bath. | I usually soak the beans overnight. | A sudden shower of rain soaked the spectators. | Blood had soaked through the bandage. | He was accused of soaking his clients.

Definition: if the value, amount or level of something soars , it rises very quickly | to rise quickly and smoothly up into the air | to fly very high in the air or remain high in the air | to be very high or tall | when music soars , it becomes higher or louder
Example: soaring costs/prices/temperatures | The rocket soared (up) into the air. | an eagle soaring high above the cliffs | soaring mountains | soaring strings

Definition: connected with socialism | governed by or belonging to a party that believes in socialism | a person who believes in or supports socialism ; a member of a political party that believes in socialism
Example: socialist beliefs | a socialist country | His father was a leading socialist in Germany.

Tags: Oxford_3000 especially_British_English especially_North_American_English North_American_English Space Physics_and_chemistry The_environment
Definition: of or connected with the sun | using the sun’s energy
Example: solar radiation | solar heating

Definition: only; single | belonging to one person or group; not shared | the bottom surface of the foot | the bottom part of a shoe or sock, not including the heel | having the type of soles mentioned | a flat sea fish that is used for food | to repair a shoe by replacing the sole
Example: the sole surviving member of the family | She has sole responsibility for the project. | The hot sand burned the soles of their feet . | leather soles | rubber-soled shoes |  |

Definition: only; not involving somebody/something else
Example: She was motivated solely by self-interest.

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English North_American_English Law_and_justice Jobs Business
Definition: a lawyer who prepares legal documents, for example for the sale of land or buildings, advises people on legal matters, and can speak for them in some courts of law | a person whose job is to visit or phone people and try to sell them something | the most senior legal officer of a city, town or government department
Example: Her first step was to contact a solicitor for advice. |  |

Definition: support by one person or group of people for another because they share feelings, opinions, aims, etc.
Example: community solidarity

Definition: alone, without anyone helping you | without any other musicians; without other musical instruments | done by one person alone, without anyone helping them | connected with or played as a musical solo
Example: She wanted to fly solo across the Atlantic. | After three years with the band he decided to go solo . | his first solo flight | a solo artist (= for example a singer who sings on their own, not as part of a group)

Definition: in a way that is not known or certain | for a reason that you do not know or understand
Example: We must stop him from seeing her somehow. | Somehow, I don't feel I can trust him.

Definition: used to refer to what somebody used to be | used to refer to what somebody does occasionally | at a time that you do not know exactly or has not yet been decided
Example: Thomas Atkins, sometime vicar of this parish | a sometime contributor to this magazine | I saw him sometime last summer.

Definition: to some degree
Example: somewhat different/similar

Definition: clever and complicated in the way that it works or is presented | having a lot of experience of the world and knowing about fashion, culture and other things that people think are socially important | able to understand difficult or complicated ideas
Example: highly sophisticated computer systems | the sophisticated pleasures of city life | a sophisticated audience

Tags: Oxford_3000 North_American_English becoming_old-fashioned literary old-fashioned informal humorous British_English Religion_and_festivals Music Success
Definition: the spiritual part of a person, believed to exist after death | a person’s inner character, containing their true thoughts and feelings | the spiritual and moral qualities of humans in general | strong and good human feeling, especially that gives a work of art its quality or enables somebody to recognize and enjoy that quality | a perfect example of a good quality | a person of a particular type | a person | a type of music that expresses strong emotions, made popular by African American musicians
Example: He believed his immortal soul was in peril. | music that soothes your soul | the dark side of the human soul | It was a very polished performance, but it lacked soul. | He is the soul of discretion. | She's lost all her money, poor soul. | There wasn't a soul in sight (= nobody was in sight) . | a soul singer
Idioms: bare your soul (to somebody) : to tell somebody your deepest and most private feelings | body and soul : with all your energy ; She committed herself body and soul to fighting for the cause. | God rest his/her soul | God rest him/her : used to show respect when you are talking about somebody who is dead | good for the soul : good for you, even if it seems unpleasant ; ‘Want a ride?’ ‘No thanks. Walking is good for the soul.’ | heart and soul : with a lot of energy and enthusiasm ; They threw themselves heart and soul into the project. | keep body and soul together : to stay alive with just enough of the food, clothing, etc. that you need ; They barely have enough money to keep body and soul together. | the life and soul of the party, etc. : the liveliest and funniest person at a party, etc. | sell your soul (to the devil) : to do anything, even something really bad or dishonest, in return for money, success or power

Definition: complete power to govern a country | the state of being a country with freedom to govern itself
Example: The country claimed sovereignty over the island. | The declaration proclaimed the full sovereignty of the republic.

Definition: used to show that you think a word that is used to describe someone or something is not suitable or not correct : | used to introduce a new word or phrase that is not yet known by many people : | named or called in a particular way: | People or things that are so-called may not fit their name :
Example: It was her so-called friends that spread the rumours about her. | It isn't yet clear how dangerous these so-called "super-rats" are. | Married taxpayers are hit hard by the so-called marriage penalty . | These so-called experts don’t know anything.

Tags: Oxford_5000 informal disapproving Business Phones,_email_and_the_internet Food
Definition: advertising material sent by email to people who have not asked for it; advertising material on the internet that is not wanted | cooked meat that has been cut up in very small pieces that are pressed together in a container, usually sold in cans and served cold in slices | to send the same message to large numbers of internet users who have not requested the information
Example: to send/block spam |  | Some companies will keep spamming you until you change your email address.

Definition: the length of time that something lasts or is able to continue | a range or variety of something | the part of a bridge or an arch between one support and another | the measurement of something from one side to the other | to last all through a period of time or to cover the whole of it | to include a large area or a lot of things | to stretch right across something, from one side to the other
Example: I worked with him over a span of six years. | Managers have a wide span of control. | The bridge crosses the river in a single span. | The kite has a span of 1.5 metres. | His acting career spanned 55 years. | The operation, which spanned nine countries, resulted in 200 arrests. | a series of bridges spanning the river
Idioms: spick and span : neat and clean ; Their house is always spick and span.

Definition: available to do what you want with rather than work | that is not being used or is not needed at the present time | kept in case you need to replace the one you usually use; extra | thin, and usually quite tall | an extra thing that you keep in case you need to replace the one you usually use (used especially about a tyre of a car) | spare parts (= new parts that you buy to replace old or broken parts of a car, machine, etc.) | to make something such as time or money available to somebody or for something, especially when it requires an effort for you to do this | to save somebody/yourself from having to go through an unpleasant experience | to allow somebody/something to escape harm, damage or death, especially when others do not escape it | to do everything possible to achieve something or to do something well without trying to limit the time or money involved | to work as hard as possible
Example: He's studying music in his spare time . | We've got a spare bedroom, if you'd like to stay. | a spare key/tyre |  | to get the spare out of the boot/trunk | It can be difficult to get spares for some older makes of car. | I'd love to have a break, but I can't spare the time just now. | He wanted to spare his mother any anxiety. | They killed the men but spared the children. | He spared no effort to make her happy again. |
Idioms: go spare : to become very angry or upset ; Your mum would go spare if she knew! ; I’ll go spare if I don’t get out of the house soon! | spare somebody’s blushes : to save somebody from an embarrassing situation ; A last minute goal spared their blushes. | spare somebody’s feelings : to be careful not to do or say anything that might upset somebody ; I kept off the subject of divorce so as to spare her feelings. | to spare : if you have time, money, etc. to spare , you have more than you need ; I've got absolutely no money to spare this month. ; We arrived at the airport with five minutes to spare.
Synonym: save from death | safeguard | fleshless | save from death | | | use frugally | save from death | reserve | fleshless | conserve

Definition: a very small burning piece of material that is produced by something that is burning or by hitting two hard substances together | a small flash of light produced by an electric current | a small amount of a particular quality or feeling | a special quality of energy, intelligence or enthusiasm that makes somebody very clever, funny, etc. | an action or event that causes something important to develop, especially trouble or violence | feelings of anger or excitement between people | to cause something to start or develop, especially suddenly | to produce small flashes of fire or electricity
Example: A shower of sparks flew up the chimney. | sparks from a faulty light switch | a spark of hope | As a writer he seemed to lack creative spark. | These were the sparks of revolution. | Sparks flew at the meeting (= there was a lot of argument) . | The proposal would spark a storm of protest around the country. | a sparking, crackling fire
Idioms: a bright spark : a lively and intelligent person, especially somebody young ; Some bright spark (= stupid person) left the tap running all night.

Definition: having or involving expert knowledge of a particular area of work, study or medicine | a person who is an expert in a particular area of work or study | a doctor who has specialized in a particular area of medicine
Example: specialist knowledge/training/skills | a specialist in Japanese history | You need to see a specialist .

Definition: to become an expert in a particular area of work, study or business; to spend more time on one area of work, etc. than on others
Example: Many students prefer not to specialize too soon.

Definition: designed or developed for a particular purpose or area of knowledge
Example: specialized equipment

Definition: a group into which animals, plants, etc. that are able to have sex with each other and produce healthy young are divided, smaller than a genus and identified by a Latin name
Example: a conservation area for endangered species

Definition: a detailed description of how something is, or should be, designed or made
Example: the technical specifications of the new model (= of car)

Definition: to state something, especially by giving an exact measurement, time, exact instructions, etc.
Example: Remember to specify your size when ordering clothes.

Definition: a small amount of something that shows what the rest of it is like | a single example of something, especially an animal or a plant | a small quantity of blood, urine , etc. that is taken from somebody and tested by a doctor
Example: Astronauts have brought back specimens of rock from the moon. | The aquarium has some interesting specimens of unusual tropical fish. | to provide/take a specimen

Definition: two lenses in a frame that rests on the nose and ears. People wear spectacles in order to be able to see better or to protect their eyes from bright light. | a performance or an event that is very impressive and exciting to look at | a sight or view that is very impressive to look at | an unusual or surprising sight or situation that attracts a lot of attention
Example: a pair of spectacles | The carnival parade was a magnificent spectacle. | The sunset was a stunning spectacle. | I remember the sad spectacle of her standing in her wedding dress, covered in mud.
Idioms: make a spectacle of yourself : to draw attention to yourself by behaving or dressing in a silly way in public

Definition: very impressive | an impressive show or performance
Example: The coastal road has spectacular scenery. | a Christmas TV spectacular

Definition: a person who is watching an event, especially a sports event
Example: The new football stadium will hold 75 000 spectators.

Definition: a band of coloured lights in order of their wavelengths , as seen in a rainbow and into which light may be separated | a range of sound waves or several other types of wave | a complete or wide range of related qualities, ideas, etc.
Example: A spectrum is formed by a ray of light passing through a prism. | the electromagnetic/radio/sound spectrum | a broad spectrum of interests
Idioms: on the spectrum : having an autism spectrum disorder ; The chance of having a second autistic child is much higher for parents with one child on the spectrum.

Definition: to form an opinion about something without knowing all the details or facts | to buy goods, property, shares, etc., hoping to make a profit when you sell them, but with the risk of losing money
Example: We all speculated about the reasons for her resignation. | He likes to speculate on the stock market.

Definition: the act of forming opinions about what has happened or what might happen without knowing all the facts | the activity of buying and selling goods or shares in a company in the hope of making a profit, but with the risk of losing money
Example: His private life is the subject of much speculation. | speculation in oil

Definition: a solid figure that is completely round, with every point on its surface at an equal distance from the centre | any object that is completely round, for example a ball | an area of activity, influence or interest; a particular section of society | a region that surrounds a planet, especially the earth
Example: The Earth is not a perfect sphere. |  | the political sphere | ionosphere

Definition: one of the various types of powder or seed that come from plants and are used in cooking. Spices have a strong taste and smell. | extra interest or excitement | a powerful illegal synthetic (= artificial) drug that some people smoke for the physical and mental effects that it has on them | to add spice to food in order to give it more taste | to add interest or excitement to something
Example: common spices such as ginger and cinnamon | We need an exciting trip to add some spice to our lives. | Six men overdosed on the synthetic drug known as spice. | highly spiced dishes | He exaggerated the details to spice up the story.
Idioms: variety is the spice of life : having a range of different experiences makes life more interesting

Definition: an act of letting a liquid come or fall out of a container; the amount of liquid that comes or falls out | a long match, or a thin piece of twisted paper, used for lighting fires, oil lamps, etc. | a fall, especially from a bicycle or a boat | to flow over the edge of a container by accident; to make liquid do this | to come out of a place in large numbers and spread out
Example: Many seabirds died as a result of the oil spill. |  | to take a spill | Water had spilled out of the bucket onto the floor. | The doors opened and people spilled into the street.
Idioms: (the) thrills and spills : the excitement that is involved in dangerous activities, especially sports ; Experience the thrills and spills of surfing! | cry over spilt milk : to waste time worrying about something that has happened that you cannot do anything about ; As the saying goes— it's no use crying over spilt milk . | spill the beans/tea : to tell somebody something that should be kept secret or private ; We don't want to spill the beans yet. ; She made the mistake of spilling the tea on her sister's love life. | spill (somebody’s) blood : to kill or wound somebody ; Nothing can justify spilling innocent blood. | spill your guts (to somebody) : to tell somebody everything you know or feel about something, because you are upset ; He has spilled his guts in his new autobiography.
Synonym: cause to overflow | let flow | cause to overflow | disclose | cause to overflow | disclose

Definition: a very fast turning movement | if an aircraft goes into a spin , it falls and turns round rapidly | a short ride in a car for pleasure | the way you make a ball turn very fast when you throw it or hit it | a way of presenting information or a situation in a particular way, especially one that makes you or your ideas seem good | to turn round and round quickly; to make something do this | to turn round quickly once; to make somebody do this | to make thread from wool, cotton, silk, etc. by twisting it | to produce thread from its body to make a web or cocoon | to drive or travel quickly | to remove the water from clothes that have just been washed, in a spin dryer | to present information or a situation in a particular way, especially one that makes you or your ideas seem good
Example: Only in the last 50 years have we had clocks accurate enough to measure changes in the earth's spin. | He had to stop the helicopter from going into a spin. | Let's go for a spin . | She puts a lot of spin on the ball. | Politicians put their own spin on the economic situation. | The plane was spinning out of control. | He spun around to face her. | She sat by the window spinning. | a spider spinning a web | They went spinning along the roads on their bikes. |  | An aide was already spinning the senator's defeat as ‘almost as good as an outright win’.
Idioms: in a (flat) spin : very confused, worried or excited ; Her resignation put her colleagues in a spin. | your head is spinning | make your head spin : your head feels as though it is going round and round and you cannot balance, especially because you are ill or confused or have had a shock; to make you feel like this ; Her head was spinning from the pain. ; His theories on economics are enough to make your head spin. | spin (somebody) a yarn, tale, etc. : to try to make somebody believe a long story that is not true | turn/spin on your heel : to turn around suddenly so that you are facing in the opposite direction ; He turned on his heel and marched away angrily.

Definition: the row of small bones that are connected together down the middle of the back | the central feature of something or the main source of its strength | strength of character | any of the sharp, pointed parts like needles on some plants and animals | the narrow part of the cover of a book that the pages are joined to
Example: A shiver went down my spine. | These speeches form the spine of his election campaign. | Their protests stiffened the spines of party activists. | Porcupines use their spines to protect themselves. |

Definition: connected with the human spirit, rather than the body or physical things | connected with religion | a religious song of the type created by black Christians in the southern states of the US
Example: a spiritual experience | a spiritual leader |
Idioms: your spiritual home : the place where you are happiest, especially a country where you feel you belong more than in your own country because you share the ideas and attitudes of the people who live there ; When she moved to Italy, she knew she'd found her true spiritual home. ; The first time he visited New Orleans he knew he had found his spiritual home. ; I had always thought of India as my spiritual home.

Definition: a feeling of wanting to hurt or upset somebody | to deliberately annoy or upset somebody
Example: I'm sure he only said it out of spite. | They're playing the music so loud just to spite us.
Idioms: in spite of something : if you say that somebody did something in spite of a fact, you mean it is surprising that that fact did not prevent them from doing it ; In spite of his age, he still leads an active life. ; They went swimming in spite of all the danger signs. ; English became the official language for business in spite of the fact that the population was largely Chinese. | in spite of yourself : if you do something in spite of yourself , you do it although you did not intend or expect to ; He fell asleep, in spite of himself. ; In spite of herself, tears welled up in her eyes. | cut off your nose to spite your face : to do something when you are angry that is meant to harm somebody else but that also harms you

Definition: an argument or disagreement that divides a group of people or makes somebody separate from somebody else | a long opening made when something tears | a division between two or more things; one of the parts that something is divided into | a position in which you stretch your legs flat across the floor in opposite directions with the rest of your body sitting straight up | to divide, or to make something divide, into two or more parts | to divide something into two or more parts and share it between different people, activities, etc. | to divide, or to make a group of people divide, into smaller groups that have very different opinions | to tear, or to make something tear, along a straight line | to cut somebody’s skin and make it bleed | to leave somebody and stop having a relationship with them | to leave a place quickly
Example: Ten years after their acrimonious split, the band has reformed. | A blockage in the artery caused the split. | He demanded a 50–50 split in the profits. | a gymnast doing the splits | He was a member of the team that split the atom in 1932. | She split the money she won with her brother. | The committee split over government subsidies. | Her dress had split along the seam. | She split her head open on the cupboard door. | The singer split with his wife last June. | Let's split!
Idioms: split the difference : (when discussing a price, etc.) to agree on an amount that is at an equal distance between the two amounts that have been suggested ; I offered €200 but he wanted €300. In the end, we split the difference and I paid him €250. ; I wanted to leave early and Ian wanted to leave late, so we split the difference and left at noon. | split/divide (something) down the middle : to divide something into two equal parts; to divide into two equal parts ; The country was split down the middle over the strike (= half supported it, half did not) . ; Divide the cake down the middle. ; It would seem the community has divided down the middle, with some favouring expansion and some dead set against it. | split hairs : to pay too much attention in an argument to differences that are very small and not important | split an infinitive : to place an adverb between ‘to’ and the infinitive of a verb, for example to say ‘to strongly deny the report’. Some people consider this to be bad English style. | split your sides (laughing/with laughter) : to laugh a lot at somebody/something | split the ticket : to vote for candidates from more than one party

Definition: goods taken from a place by thieves or by an army that has won a battle or war | the profits or advantages that somebody gets from being successful | waste material that is brought up when a hole is dug, etc. | to change something good into something bad, unpleasant, etc. | to give a child everything that they ask for and not enough discipline in a way that has a bad effect on their character and behaviour | to make somebody/yourself happy by doing something special | to become bad so that it can no longer be eaten | to mark a ballot paper in a way that is not correct so the vote does not count, especially as a form of protest
Example: the spoils of war | the spoils of high office |  | Our camping trip was spoilt by bad weather. | She spoils those kids of hers. | Why not spoil yourself with a weekend in a top hotel? |  | The group called on its supporters to spoil their ballot papers.
Idioms: be spoiling for a fight : to want to fight with somebody very much | spoil the ship for a ha’p’orth/ha’pennyworth of tar : to cause something good to fail because you did not spend a small but necessary amount of money or time on a small but essential part of it | too many cooks spoil the broth : if too many people are involved in doing something, it will not be done well
Synonym: overindulge | ruin | ruin | go bad | ruin | ruin | overindulge | ruin

Definition: a person who speaks for a group or an organization
Example: a police spokesman

Definition: a person who speaks for a group or an organization
Example: a council spokesperson

Definition: a woman who speaks for a group or an organization
Example: a government spokeswoman

Definition: a person or company that pays for a radio or television programme, or for a concert or sporting event, usually in return for advertising | a person who agrees to give somebody money for a charity if that person succeeds in completing a particular activity | a person or company that supports somebody by paying for their training or education | a person, a group, an organization, etc. that provides money for an activity | a person who introduces and supports a proposal for a new law, etc. | a person who agrees to be officially responsible for another person | a person who presents a candidate for Christian baptism or confirmation | to pay the costs of a particular event, programme, etc. as a way of advertising | to agree to give somebody money for a charity if they complete a particular task | to support somebody by paying for their training or education | to provide money for a particular activity | to arrange for something official to take place | to introduce a proposal for a new law, etc.
Example: The programme is funded by a number of corporate sponsors . | Even though the race is still ten weeks away, we are already looking for sponsors. | Each student's tuition was paid by an individual sponsor. | the list of state sponsors of terrorism | the sponsor of the new immigration bill |  |  | Sports events are no longer sponsored by the tobacco industry. | They got their granny and aunts and other people to sponsor them. | The corporation is sponsoring several athletes and teams here in the US. | The group has been accused of sponsoring terrorism. | The US is sponsoring negotiations between the two sides. | The bill was sponsored by a Labour MP.

Definition: financial support from a sponsor | the act of supporting a person, organization or activity, especially financially | the act of introducing a proposal for a new law
Example: a $50 million sponsorship deal | Two million pounds were raised through sponsorship. | the senator’s sponsorship of the job training legislation

Definition: connected with sports | fair and generous in your treatment of other people, especially in a game or sport
Example: a major sporting event | The game was played in a very sporting spirit.
Idioms: a sporting chance : a reasonable chance of success

Definition: a light with a single, very bright beam that can be directed at a particular place or person, especially a performer on the stage | the area of light that is made by a spotlight | attention from newspapers, television and the public | to shine a spotlight on somebody/something | to give special attention to a problem, situation, etc. so that people notice it
Example: The room was lit by spotlights. | She stood alone on stage in the spotlight. | Unemployment is once again in the spotlight. | a spotlit stage | The programme spotlights financial problems in the health service.

Definition: a husband or wife
Example: Fill in your spouse’s name here.

Definition: a person who tries to get secret information about another country, organization or person, especially somebody who is employed by a government or the police | to collect secret information about another country, organization or person | to suddenly see or notice somebody/something
Example: He was denounced as a foreign spy. | He spied for his government for more than ten years. | In the distance we spied the Pacific for the first time.
Idioms: spy out the land : to collect information before deciding what to do

Definition: a section of a police force that deals with a particular type of crime | a group of players, runners, etc. from which a team is chosen for a particular game or match | a small group of soldiers working or being trained together | a group of people who have a particular task
Example: the drugs/fraud/bomb/riot squad | the Olympic/national squad | an elite combat squad | She was captain of the cheerleading squad.

Definition: an act of pressing something, usually with your hands | a small amount of liquid that is produced by pressing something | a situation where it is almost impossible for a number of people or things to fit into a small or limited space | a reduction in the amount of money, jobs, etc. available; a difficult situation caused by this | a boyfriend or girlfriend | to press something, especially with your fingers | to get liquid out of something by pressing or twisting it hard | to force somebody/something/yourself into or through a small space | to succeed in doing something with difficulty or by a small amount | to get something by putting pressure on somebody, threatening them, etc. | to strictly limit or reduce the amount of money that somebody/something has or can use
Example: He gave my hand a little squeeze. | a squeeze of lemon juice | It was a tight squeeze but we finally got everything into the case. | We're really feeling the squeeze since I lost my job. | Who's his main squeeze ? | to squeeze a tube of toothpaste | to squeeze the juice from a lemon | We managed to squeeze six people into the car. | The candidate squeezed through to the next round by just ten votes. | He's squeezing me for £500. | High interest rates have squeezed the industry hard.
Idioms: put the squeeze on somebody (to do something) : to put pressure on somebody to act in a particular way; to make a situation difficult for somebody ; You come straight to me if she tries to put the squeeze on you. ; Increased competition is really putting the squeeze on North American producers. ; The government is trying to put the squeeze on high earners. | squeeze somebody dry : to get as much money, information, etc. out of somebody as you can
Synonym: press | press | press | press | press | press | press

Definition: an act of stabbing or trying to stab somebody/something; a wound caused by stabbing | a sudden sharp pain or unpleasant feeling | an attempt to do something | to push a sharp, pointed object, especially a knife, into somebody, killing or injuring them | to make a short, aggressive or violent movement with a finger or pointed object
Example: He received several stabs in the chest. | She felt a sudden stab of pain in the chest. | He found the test difficult but nevertheless made a good stab at it. | He was stabbed to death in a racist attack. | He stabbed his finger angrily at my chest.
Idioms: a shot/stab in the dark : a guess; something you do without knowing what the result will be ; The figure he came up with was really just a shot in the dark. ; It was only a stab in the dark, but I hoped I might be right. | a stab in the back : an act that harms somebody, done by a person they thought was a friend ; Her refusal to support his campaign was a real stab in the back. | stab somebody in the back : to do or say something that harms somebody who trusts you

Definition: the quality or state of being steady and not changing or being upset in any way (= the quality of being stable)
Example: political/economic/social stability

Definition: to become or to make something become steady and unlikely to change; to make something stable
Example: The patient's condition stabilized.

Definition: fixed or steady; not likely to move, change or fail | calm and reasonable; not easily upset | staying in the same chemical or atomic state | a building in which horses are kept | an organization that keeps horses for a particular purpose | a group of racehorses owned or trained by the same person | a group of people who work or trained in the same place; a group of products made by the same company | to put or keep a horse in a stable
Example: The patient's condition is stable (= it is not getting worse) . | Mentally, she is not very stable. | chemically stable | The horse was led back to its stable. | a riding/racing stables | There have been just three winners from his stable this season. | actors from the same stable | Where do you stable your pony?
Idioms: close, lock, etc. the stable door after the horse has bolted : to try to prevent or avoid loss or damage when it is already too late to do so

Definition: money that somebody invests in a company | a part or share in a business, plan, etc. that will bring you money or other benefits if it succeeds | something that you risk losing, especially money, when you try to predict the result of a race, game, etc., or when you are involved in an activity that can succeed or fail | a wooden or metal post that is pointed at one end and pushed into the ground in order to support something, mark a particular place, etc. | a wooden post that somebody could be tied to in former times before being burnt to death (= killed by fire) as a punishment | the money that is paid to the winners in horse racing | used in the names of some horse races | to risk money or something important on the result of something | to support something with a stake
Example: a 20 per cent stake in the business | She has a personal stake in the success of the play. | How much was the stake (= how much did you bet) ? | Tall plants can be secured by tying them to stakes. | Joan of Arc was burnt at the stake . |  |  | He staked £25 on the favourite (= for example, in horse racing) . | to stake newly planted trees
Idioms: at stake : that can be won or lost, depending on the success of a particular action ; We cannot afford to take risks when people's lives are at stake. ; The prize at stake is a place in the final. | go to the stake over/for something : to be prepared to do anything in order to defend your opinions or beliefs ; He would have gone to the stake for his belief in his daughter’s innocence. | in the… stakes : used to say how much of a particular quality a person has, as if they were in a competition in which some people are more successful than others ; John doesn't do too well in the personality stakes. ; She was determined to win in the fashion stakes. | pull up stakes : to suddenly move from your house and go to live somewhere else ; He pulled up stakes and went back to France. | stake (out) a/your claim (to/for/on something) : to say or show publicly that you think something should be yours ; Adams staked his claim for a place in the Olympic team with his easy win yesterday.

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_North_American_English both_British_English North_American_English Shopping Farming Film_and_theatre
Definition: a table or small shop with an open front that people sell things from, especially at a market | a section inside a farm building that is large enough for one animal to be kept in | a small area in a room, surrounded by glass, walls, etc., that contains a shower or toilet | the seats that are nearest to the stage in a theatre | the seats at the front of a church where the choir (= singers) and priests sit | a situation in which a vehicle’s engine suddenly stops because it is not getting enough power | a situation in which an aircraft loses speed and goes steeply downwards | to stop suddenly because of a lack of power or speed; to make a vehicle or an engine do this | to try to avoid doing something or answering a question so that you have more time | to make somebody wait so that you have more time to do something | to stop something from happening until a later date; to stop making progress
Example: He works on a market stall in the Square. | We had to muck out stalls and groom the horses. | a bathroom stall | the front row of the stalls |  |  | The plane went into a stall. | The car stalled and refused to start again. | They are still stalling on the deal. | See if you can stall her while I finish searching her office. | There have been several attempts to revive the stalled peace plan.
Synonym: stand | booth | booth | stop | booth | stand | stand

Definition: the opinions that somebody has about something and expresses publicly | the way in which somebody stands, especially when playing a sport
Example: What is the newspaper's stance on the war? | Widen your stance (= move your feet wider apart) for greater stability when hitting the ball.

Definition: done from a position in which you are standing rather than sitting or running | existing or arranged permanently, not formed or made for a particular situation | the position or reputation of somebody/something within a group of people or in an organization | the period of time that something has existed | a list of people, teams, etc. showing their positions in a sports competition
Example: a standing jump/start | a standing army | the high/low standing of politicians with the public | a friendship of many years’ standing | He remains at the top of the championship standings.
Idioms: from a standing start : from an initial position in which none of the necessary elements are in place ; The space project went from a standing start to the moon in ten years.

Definition: an act of looking at somebody/something for a long time, especially in a way that is unfriendly or that shows surprise | to look at somebody/something for a long time
Example: She gave him a blank stare. | I screamed and everyone stared.
Idioms: be staring somebody in the face : used to describe something that you have failed to see, even though it was obvious and should have been easy to see ; The answer was staring us in the face but we never saw it until it was too late. | be staring somebody in the face : to be certain to happen ; Defeat was staring them in the face. | be staring something in the face : to be unable to avoid something ; They were staring defeat in the face.

Definition: unpleasant; real, and impossible to avoid | very different from something in a way that is easy to see | looking severe and without any colour or decoration | complete and total | wearing no clothes
Example: The author paints a stark picture of life in a prison camp. | stark differences | I think white would be too stark for the bedroom. | The children watched in stark terror. |
Idioms: (stark) raving mad/bonkers : completely crazy

Definition: to suffer or die because you do not have enough food to eat; to make somebody suffer or die in this way | not having something that you need
Example: The animals were left to starve to death . | supply-starved rebels
Idioms: be starving (for something) : to feel very hungry ; When's the food coming? I'm starving!
Synonym: perish from hunger | perish from hunger | perish from hunger

Definition: connected with or based on statistics
Example: statistical analysis

Definition: the legal position of a person, group or country | the social or professional position of somebody/something in relation to others | high rank or social position | the level of importance that is given to something | the situation at a particular time during a process
Example: They were granted refugee status . | She achieved celebrity status overnight. | The job brings with it status and a high income. | This reflects the high status accorded to science in our culture. | What is the current status of our application for funds?

Definition: gradually and in an even and regular way | without changing or being interrupted
Example: The company's exports have been increasing steadily. | He looked at her steadily.

Definition: developing, growing, etc. gradually and in an even and regular way | not changing and not interrupted | fixed in a place or position, supported or balanced; controlled and not shaking or likely to fall down | sensible; who can be relied on | in a way that is steady and does not change or shake | used to tell somebody to be careful about what they are saying or doing, for example because it is extreme or not appropriate | used to tell somebody to be careful | to stop yourself/somebody/something from moving, shaking or falling; to stop moving, shaking or falling | to stop changing and become regular again | to make somebody/something calm
Example: We've had five years of steady economic growth . | His breathing was steady. | He held the boat steady as she got in. |  | In trading today the dollar held steady against the yen. | Steady on! You can't say things like that about somebody you've never met. | Steady! Don't fall off. | She steadied herself against the wall. | Her heartbeat steadied. | He took a few deep breaths to steady his nerves .
Idioms: ready, steady, go! : what you say to tell people to start a race | (as) steady as a rock : extremely steady and calm; that you can rely on | go steady (with somebody) : to have a romantic or sexual relationship with somebody, in which you see the other person regularly ; They’ve been going steady for a year now.
Synonym: constant | constant | constant | constant | constant | constant

Definition: the hot gas that water changes into when it boils | the power that is produced from steam under pressure, used to operate engines, machines, etc. | very small drops of water that form in the air or on cold surfaces when warm air suddenly cools | to send out steam | to place food over boiling water so that it cooks in the steam ; to be cooked in this way | to move using the power produced by steam | to go somewhere very quickly
Example: Steam rose from the boiling kettle. | the introduction of steam in the 18th century | She wiped the steam from her glasses. | Our damp clothes steamed in the heat. | steamed fish | The boat steamed across the lake. | He spotted her steaming down the corridor towards him.
Idioms: blow/let off steam : to get rid of your energy, anger or strong emotions by doing something active or noisy ; I went for a long walk to let off steam. ; I like blowing off steam and I like saying things that shock people. | full speed/steam ahead | (at) full steam : with as much speed or energy as possible ; Business picked up at the beginning of the year and now it is full steam ahead. ; The team is working full steam on the next release. ; All of the major players are operating at full steam. | get, etc. somewhere under your own steam : to go somewhere without help from other people ; I’ll get to the party under my own steam. | get up/pick up steam : to become gradually more powerful, active, etc. ; His election campaign is beginning to get up steam. | get up/pick up steam : to increase speed gradually | head of steam : the pressure produced by steam in a small space ; The old engine still manages to build up a good head of steam. | head of steam : a situation in which somebody/something becomes gradually more active or successful ; This campaign has been steadily building up a head of steam . ; United have recently gathered a head of steam in the title race. | run out of steam : to lose energy and enthusiasm and stop doing something, or do it less well | be/get (all) steamed up (about/over something) : to be/become very angry or excited about something ; You’re getting all steamed up about nothing!
Synonym: go | go | go | go | go | go | go | go | go | go | go

Definition: a strong, hard metal that is made of a mixture of iron and carbon | the industry that produces steel | a long, thin, straight piece of steel with a rough surface, used for rubbing knives on to make them sharp | weapons that are used for fighting | to prepare yourself to deal with something unpleasant
Example: the iron and steel industry | Steel used to be important in South Wales. |  | the clash of steel | As she waited, she steeled herself for disappointment.
Idioms: of steel : having a quality like steel, especially a strong, cold or hard quality ; She felt a hand of steel (= a strong, firm hand) on her arm. ; You need a cool head and nerves of steel (= great courage) . ; There was a hint of steel in his voice (= he sounded cold and firm) .

Definition: rising or falling quickly, not gradually | sudden and very big | too much; unreasonable | if you steep food in a liquid or leave it to steep , you put it in the liquid and leave it for some time so that it becomes soft and takes in the taste of the liquid
Example: a steep hill/slope | a steep decline in the birth rate | £4 for a cup of coffee seems a little steep to me. | Steep the fruit in brandy overnight.
Idioms: be steeped in something : to have a lot of a particular quality ; a city steeped in history

Definition: a piece of advice or information that helps you do something or avoid a problem | a bull (= a male cow) that has been castrated (= had part of its sex organs removed) , kept for its meat | to control the direction in which a boat, car, etc. moves | to move in a particular direction | to take control of a situation and influence the way in which it develops
Example: Can anyone give me a steer on this? |  | He steered the boat into the harbour. | The ship steered a course between the islands. | He managed to steer the conversation away from his divorce.
Idioms: keep/stay/steer clear (of somebody/something) : to avoid a person or thing because they may cause problems ; Steer clear of the centre of town at this time of the evening.

Definition: the main long, thin part of a plant above the ground from which the leaves or flowers grow; a smaller part that grows from this and supports flowers or leaves | the long, thin part of a wine glass between the bowl and the base | the thin tube of a tobacco pipe | having one or more stems of the type mentioned | the main part of a word that stays the same when endings are added to it | to stop something that is flowing from spreading or increasing
Example: long, trailing stems of ivy |  |  | a long-stemmed rose | ‘Writ’ is the stem of the forms ‘writes’, ‘writing’ and ‘written’. | The cut was bandaged to stem the bleeding.
Idioms: from stem to stern : all the way from the front of a ship to the back

Definition: a fixed idea or image that many people have of a particular type of person or thing, but which is often not true in reality and may cause hurt and offence | to form a fixed idea about a person or thing which may not really be true
Example: cultural/gender/racial stereotypes | Children from certain backgrounds tend to be stereotyped by their teachers.

Definition: made of or covered in a substance that sticks to things that touch it | with glue (= a sticky substance) on one side so that you can stick it to a surface | hot and slightly wet | feeling hot and uncomfortable | difficult or unpleasant | so interesting and well organized that the people who visit it stay there for a long time | a small piece of sticky paper that you use for writing a note on, and that can be easily removed
Example: sticky fingers covered in jam | sticky tape | a sticky, humid afternoon | She felt hot and sticky after six hours on the bus. | I always seem to get into sticky situations on holiday. |  | Never write your password on a sticky note!
Idioms: a bad/sticky end : something unpleasant that happens to somebody, for example punishment or a violent death, usually because of their own actions ; He'll come to a sticky end one of these days if he carries on like that. | have sticky fingers : to be likely to steal something | a sticky wicket : a difficult situation

Definition: difficult to bend or move | when a person or a part of their body is stiff , their muscles hurt when they move them | thick; difficult to stir (= move around with a spoon) | more difficult or severe than usual | not friendly or relaxed | costing a lot or too much | strong; containing a lot of alcohol | very much; to an extreme degree | very cold and hard because the water has become ice | the body of a dead person | to cheat somebody or not pay them what you owe them, especially by not leaving any money as a tip
Example: a sheet of stiff black cardboard | I'm really stiff after that bike ride yesterday. | Whisk the egg whites until stiff. | It was a stiff climb to the top of the hill. | The speech he made to welcome them was stiff and formal. | There's a stiff $30 entrance fee to the exhibition. | a stiff whisky | be bored/scared/worried stiff | The clothes on the washing line were frozen stiff. |  | These people are taxed on the tips they make. When you stiff ’em, you cost them money.
Idioms: (keep) a stiff upper lip : to keep calm and hide your feelings when you are in pain or in a difficult situation ; He was taught to keep a stiff upper lip and never to cry in public. ; Their reaction contrasts sharply with the stiff upper lip of the English.

Definition: to make something develop or become more active; to encourage something | to make somebody interested and excited about something | to make a part of the body function
Example: The exhibition has stimulated interest in her work. | Parents should give children books that stimulate them. | The women were given fertility drugs to stimulate the ovaries.

Definition: something that helps somebody/something to develop better or more quickly | something that produces a reaction in a human, an animal or a plant
Example: Books provide children with ideas and a stimulus for play. | sensory/verbal/visual stimuli

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English North_American_English especially_North_American_English informal disapproving old-fashioned Cooking_and_eating Feelings
Definition: excitement, anger or shock that is felt by a number of people | the action of stirring something | to move a liquid or substance around, using a spoon or something similar, in order to mix it completely | to move, or to make something move, slightly | to move, or to make somebody move, in order to do something | to make somebody excited or make them feel something strongly | to begin to be felt | to try to cause trouble
Example: Her resignation caused quite a stir . | Could you give the rice a stir? | She stirred her tea. | She heard the baby stir in the next room. | You haven't stirred from that chair all evening! | It's a book that really stirs the imagination. | A feeling of guilt began to stir in her. | You're just stirring it!
Idioms: stir the blood : to make somebody excited ; The atmosphere in a theatre never failed to stir her blood. | stir your stumps : to begin to move; to hurry

Tags: Oxford_3000 OPAL_written especially_British_English especially_North_American_English British_English North_American_English informal formal Shopping Money Food
