Definition: a stock excuse, answer, etc. is one that is often used because it is easy and convenient, but that is not very original | usually available for sale in a shop | a supply of goods that is available for sale in a shop | a supply of something that is available for use | the value of the shares in a company that have been sold | a share that somebody has bought in a company or business | money that is lent to a government at a fixed rate of interest; an official document that gives details of this | farm animals, such as cows and sheep, that are kept for their meat, wool, etc. | having the type of family or ancestors mentioned | a liquid made by cooking bones, meat, etc. in water, used for making soups and sauces | a wooden structure with holes for the feet, used in the past to lock criminals in as a form of punishment, especially in a public place | the degree to which somebody is respected or liked by other people | the part of a gun that you hold against your shoulder when firing it | a garden plant with brightly coloured flowers with a sweet smell | a theatre company that does several different plays in a season; a repertory company | to keep a supply of a particular type of goods to sell | to fill something with food, books, etc.
Example: ‘No comment,’ was the actor's stock response. | stock sizes | We have a fast turnover of stock. | Food stocks are running low. | The company's stock hit an all-time high of $94.66. | stock prices | government stock | breeding stock |  | vegetable stock |  | Their stock is high/low . |  |  |  | Do you stock green tea? | The pond was well stocked with fish.
Idioms: lock, stock and barrel : including everything ; He sold the business lock, stock and barrel. | on the stocks : in the process of being made, built or prepared ; Our new model is already on the stocks and will be available in the spring. | put stock in something : to have a particular amount of belief in something ; She no longer puts much stock in their claims. | take stock (of something) : to stop and think carefully about the way in which a particular situation is developing in order to decide what to do next ; It was time to stand back and take stock of his career.

Definition: the process of keeping something in a particular place until it is needed; the space where things can be kept | the process of keeping information, etc. on a computer; the way it is kept | the process of paying to keep furniture, etc. in a special building until you want it
Example: tables that fold flat for storage | the storage and retrieval of information | When we moved we had to put our furniture in storage for a while.

Definition: easy to do or to understand; not complicated | honest and open; not trying to trick somebody or hide something
Example: It's a relatively straightforward process. | She's nice: very straightforward and easy to get on with.

Definition: pressure on a system or relationship because great demands are being placed on it | mental pressure or worry felt by somebody because they have too much to do or manage; something that causes this pressure | the pressure that is put on something when a physical force stretches, pushes, or pulls it | an injury to a part of your body, such as a muscle, that is caused by using it too much or by twisting it | a particular type of plant or animal, or of a disease caused by bacteria, etc. | a particular feature of the character of a person or group, or a quality in their manner | the sound of music being played or sung | to injure yourself or part of your body by making it work too hard | to make an effort to do something, using all your mental or physical strength | to try to make something do more than it is able to do | to push hard against something; to pull hard on something | to pour food, etc. through something with very small holes in it, for example a sieve , in order to separate the solid part from the liquid part
Example: The transport service cannot cope with the strain of so many additional passengers. | You will learn to cope with the stresses and strains of public life. | The rope broke under the strain. | a calf/groin/leg strain | a new strain of mosquitoes resistant to the poison | He had a definite strain of snobbery in him. | She could hear the strains of Mozart through the window. | to strain a muscle | I strained my ears (= listened very hard) to catch what they were saying. | The sudden influx of visitors is straining hotels in the town to the limit. | She strained against the ropes that held her. | Use a colander to strain the vegetables.
Idioms: creak under the strain : if a system or service creaks under the strain , it cannot deal effectively with all the things it is expected to do or provide | strain at the leash : to want to do something very much ; Like all youngsters, he's straining at the leash to leave home. | strain every nerve/sinew (to do something) : to try as hard as you can to do something ; He strained every nerve to snatch victory from defeat.

Definition: a single thin piece of thread , wire, hair, etc. | one of the different parts of an idea, a plan, a story, etc. | the land along the edge of the sea or ocean, or of a lake or river | to leave somebody in a place from which they have no way of leaving | to make a boat, fish, whale , etc. be left on land and unable to return to the water
Example: He pulled at a loose strand of wool in his sweater. | We heard every strand of political opinion. |  | The strike left hundreds of tourists stranded at the airport. | The ship was stranded on a sandbank.

Definition: done as part of a plan that is meant to achieve a particular purpose or to gain an advantage | connected with getting an advantage in a war or other military situation | intended to be fired at an enemy’s country rather than used in a battle
Example: strategic planning | Malta was of vital strategic importance during the war. | strategic missiles

Definition: a small, narrow river | a continuous flow of liquid or gas | a continuous flow of people or vehicles | a continuous supply of something; a large number of things that happen one after the other | a continuous flow of video or sound sent over the internet; a continuous flow of computer data or instructions | a group of students of the same age and level of ability in some schools | to move or pour out in a continuous flow; to produce a continuous flow of liquid or gas | to move somewhere in large numbers, one after the other | to move freely, especially in the wind or water | to put school students into groups according to their ability | to play video or sound on a computer by receiving it as a continuous stream, from the internet for example, rather than needing to wait until the whole of the material has been downloaded
Example: a mountain stream | a jet stream | I've had a steady stream of visitors. | The agency provided me with a steady stream of work. | You can listen to the live audio stream . | She was put into the fast stream. | Tears streamed down his face. | People streamed across the bridge. | Her scarf streamed behind her. | Pupils are streamed for French and maths. | You can stream the song for a single listen.
Idioms: be/come on stream : to be in operation or available ; The new computer system comes on stream next month.

Definition: to become more powerful or effective; to make somebody/something more powerful or effective | to become physically stronger | to make a feeling, an opinion or a relationship stronger; to become stronger | to become stronger | to become stronger; to make a currency or an economy stronger
Example: Her position in the party has strengthened in recent weeks. | The exercises are designed to strengthen your stomach muscles. | This temporary setback merely strengthened her resolve . | The wind had strengthened overnight. | Yesterday the pound strengthened against the dollar.
Synonym: beef up | beef up | beef up | beef up | beef up

Definition: an area of land or water, especially a long one | a continuous period of time | a period of time that somebody spends in prison | an act of stretching out your arms or legs or your body and pulling the muscles tight; the state of being stretched | the ability to be made longer or wider without breaking or tearing | a straight part at the end of a racing track | to make something longer, wider or looser, for example by pulling it; to become longer, etc. in this way | to become bigger or longer when you pull it and return to its original shape when you stop | to pull something so that it is smooth and tight | to put your arms or legs out straight and contract your muscles | to put out an arm or a leg in order to reach something | to spread over an area of land | to continue over a period of time | to be enough to buy or pay for something | to make use of a lot of your money, supplies, time, etc. | to increase your lead over somebody in a game or competition | to make use of all somebody’s skill, intelligence, etc. | to use something in a way that would not normally be considered fair, acceptable, etc.
Example: a particularly dangerous stretch of road | They worked in four-hour stretches. | He did a ten-year stretch for fraud. | We got out of the car and had a good stretch . | You need a material with plenty of stretch in it. | the finishing stretch | Is there any way of stretching shoes? | The jeans stretch to provide a perfect fit. | Stretch the fabric tightly over the frame. | He stretched and yawned lazily. | She stretched across the table for the butter. | Fields and hills stretched out as far as we could see. | The town's history stretches back to before 1500. | I need a new car, but my savings won't stretch to it. | The pandemic has stretched healthcare resources to the limit . | A minute later, Kelly stretched their lead to eight points. | I need a job that will stretch me. | He admitted that he had maybe stretched the truth a little (= not been completely honest) .
Idioms: at full stretch : using as much energy as possible, or the greatest possible amount of supplies ; Fire crews have been operating at full stretch. | not by any stretch of the imagination | by no stretch of the imagination : used to say strongly that something is not true, even if you try to imagine or believe it ; She could not, by any stretch of the imagination, be called beautiful. ; By no stretch of the imagination could the trip be described as relaxing. | bend/stretch the rules : to change the rules to suit a particular person or situation ; Couldn't they just bend the rules and let us in without a ticket? | stretch your legs : to go for a short walk after sitting for some time ; It was good to get out of the car and stretch our legs. | stretch a point : to allow or do something that is not usually acceptable, especially because of a particular situation ; I was hoping they would stretch a point and let me stay on for a while.

Definition: that must be obeyed exactly | demanding that rules, especially rules about behaviour, should be obeyed | obeying the rules of a particular religion, belief, etc. exactly | very exact and clearly defined
Example: strict rules/regulations | a strict teacher/parent/disciplinarian | a strict follower of the faith | It wasn’t illegal in the strict sense (of the word) .

Definition: with a lot of control and rules that must be obeyed | used to emphasize that something happens or must happen in all circumstances | in all details; exactly | used to emphasize that something only applies to one particular person, thing or situation
Example: She was brought up very strictly. | Smoking is strictly forbidden. | This is not strictly true. | We'll look at the problem from a strictly legal point of view.
Idioms: strictly speaking : if you are using words or rules in their exact or correct sense ; Strictly speaking, the book is not a novel, but a short story. ; Using the word in that context is not, strictly speaking, correct.

Tags: Oxford_3000 formal informal especially_British_English British_English saying figurative War_and_conflict Social_issues Working_life Success
Definition: a period of time when an organized group of employees of a company stops working because of an argument over pay or conditions | a military attack, especially by aircraft dropping bombs | an act of hitting or kicking something/somebody | an unsuccessful attempt to hit the ball | a situation in tenpin bowling when a player knocks down all the pins with the first ball | a sudden discovery of something valuable, especially oil | a bad thing or action that damages somebody/something’s reputation | to hit somebody/something hard or with force | to hit somebody/something with your hand or a weapon | to hit or kick a ball, etc. | to attack somebody/something, especially suddenly | to happen suddenly and have a harmful or damaging effect on somebody/something | to come into somebody’s mind suddenly | to give somebody a particular impression | to refuse to work because of an argument over pay or conditions | to fall on a surface | to put somebody suddenly into a particular state | to rub something such as a match against a surface so that it produces a flame; to produce a flame when rubbed against a rough surface | to show the time by making a ringing noise, etc. | to produce a musical note, sound, etc. by pressing a key or hitting something | to discover gold, oil, etc. by digging or drilling | to go somewhere with great energy or purpose
Example: the miners'/firefighters'/teachers' strike | They decided to launch a pre-emptive strike . | His spectacular strike in the second half made the score 2–0. |  |  |  | The amount of fuel that this car uses is a big strike against it. | The ship struck a rock. | She struck him in the face. | He walked up to the penalty spot and struck the ball firmly into the back of the net. | The lion crouched ready to strike. | Two days later tragedy struck . | An awful thought has just struck me. | Another part of his comment struck me. | Over 100 000 civil servants are set to strike on Tuesday. | The windows sparkled as the sun struck the glass. | to be struck dumb/deaf/blind | to strike a match on a wall | Did you hear the clock strike? | She struck a chord on the piano and the children began to sing. | They had struck oil! | We left the road and struck off across the fields.
Idioms: three strikes and you’re out | the three strikes rule : used to describe a law that says that people who commit three crimes will go straight to prison | be struck by/on/with somebody/something : to be impressed or interested by somebody/something; to like somebody/something very much ; I was struck by her youth and enthusiasm. ; We're not very struck on that new restaurant. | drive/strike a hard bargain : to argue in an aggressive way and force somebody to agree on the best possible price or arrangement | hit/strike home : if a remark, etc. hits/strikes home , it has a strong effect on somebody, in a way that makes them realize what the true facts of a situation are ; Her face went pale as his words hit home. | hit/strike pay dirt : to suddenly be in a successful situation, especially one that makes you rich ; The band really hit pay dirt with their last album. | hit/strike the right/wrong note : to do, say or write something that is suitable/not suitable for a particular occasion ; It is a bizarre tale and the author hits just the right note of horror and disbelief. ; Unfortunately, the president struck the wrong note in his speech, ignoring the public mood. | hit/strike the woodwork : to hit the wooden frame of the goal in the game of football (soccer), instead of scoring a goal ; She hit the woodwork twice before scoring. | lightning never strikes (in the same place) twice : an unusual or unpleasant event is not likely to happen in the same place or to the same people twice | sound/strike a note (of something) : to express feelings or opinions of a particular kind ; She sounded a note of warning in her speech. ; The touch of cynicism struck a slightly sour note. | strike a balance (between A and B) : to manage to find a way of being fair to two things that are opposed to each other; to find an acceptable position that is between two things ; We need to strike a balance between these conflicting interests. | strike a bargain/deal (with somebody/something) : to make an agreement with somebody in which both sides have an advantage ; Perhaps we could strike a bargain. ; The United States has struck a historic deal with India. | strike a blow for/against/at something : to do something in support of/against a belief, principle, etc. ; He felt that they had struck a blow for democracy. | strike/touch a chord (with somebody) : to say or do something that makes people feel sympathy or enthusiasm ; The speaker had obviously struck a chord with his audience. | strike fear, etc. into somebody/sb’s heart : to make somebody be afraid, etc. ; The sound of gunfire struck fear into the hearts of the villagers. | strike gold : to find or do something that brings you a lot of success or money ; He has struck gold with his latest novel. | strike/hit home : if a remark, etc. strikes/hits home , it has a strong effect on somebody, in a way that makes them realize what the true facts of a situation are ; His face went pale as her words struck home. | strike it rich : to get a lot of money, especially suddenly or unexpectedly | strike (it) lucky : to have good luck ; We certainly struck it lucky with the weather. | strike a nerve (with somebody) : to have a strong effect ; His work strikes a nerve with people who are attracted to nostalgia. ; It seems I struck a nerve with my last post about cell phones. ; Nissan may well strike a popular nerve here. | strike/hit pay dirt : to suddenly be in a successful situation, especially one that makes you rich ; The band really struck pay dirt with their last album. | strike a pose/an attitude : to hold your body in a particular way to create a particular impression ; to strike a dramatic pose ; He strikes an attitude of bravery and lonely defiance. | strike/hit the right/wrong note : to do, say or write something that is suitable/not suitable for a particular occasion ; It is a bizarre tale and the author strikes just the right note of horror and disbelief. | strike while the iron is hot : to make use of an opportunity immediately | within striking distance (of something) : near enough to be reached or attacked easily; near enough to reach or attack something easily ; The beach is within striking distance. ; The cat was now within striking distance of the duck.

Definition: interesting and unusual enough to attract attention | very attractive, often in an unusual way
Example: a striking feature | striking good looks

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English North_American_English both_British_and_North_American_English British_English_also informal Sports:_ball_and_racket_sports Transport_by_car_or_lorry
Definition: a long narrow piece of paper, metal, cloth, etc. | a long narrow area of land, sea, etc. | the uniform that is worn by the members of a sports team when they are playing | an act of taking your clothes off, especially in a sexually exciting way and in front of an audience | a street that has many shops, stores, restaurants, etc. along it | a series of drawings inside boxes that tell a story and are often published in newspapers | to take off all or most of your clothes or another person’s clothes | to take off your clothes as a form of entertainment; to perform a striptease | to remove a layer from something, especially so that it is completely exposed | to remove all the things from a place and leave it empty | to separate a machine, etc. into parts so that they can be cleaned or repaired | to take away property or honours from somebody, as a punishment
Example: a strip of material | the Gaza Strip | Juventus in their famous black and white strip | to do a strip | Sunset Strip |  | I stripped and washed myself all over. |  | Strip off all the existing paint. | We had to strip out all the old wiring and start again. | He strips and cleans his rifle every morning. | He was disgraced and stripped of his title.
Idioms: tear somebody off a strip | tear a strip off somebody : to speak angrily to somebody who has done something wrong

Definition: to try very hard to achieve something
Example: We encourage all members to strive for the highest standards.

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_British_English figurative British_English Sports:_ball_and_racket_sports Success Health_problems Transport_by_water Sports:_water_sports
Definition: an act of hitting a ball, for example with a bat or racket | a single movement of the arm when hitting somebody/something | a single successful action or event | a sudden serious illness when a blood vessel (= tube) in the brain bursts (= breaks open) or is blocked, which can cause death or the loss of the ability to move or to speak clearly | any of a series of repeated movements in swimming or rowing | a style of swimming | the person who sets the speed at which everyone in a boat rows | an act of moving your hand gently over a surface, usually several times | a mark made by moving a pen, brush, etc. once across a surface | each of the sounds made by a clock or bell giving the hours | to move your hand gently and slowly over an animal’s fur or hair | to move your hand gently over a surface, somebody’s hair, etc. | to move something somewhere with a gentle movement | to be very nice to somebody, especially to get them to do what you want
Example: What a beautiful stroke! | His punishment was six strokes of the cane. | Your idea was a stroke of genius . | to have/suffer a stroke | She took a few more strokes to reach the bank. | Butterfly is the only stroke I can't do. |  | He gave the cat a stroke. | to paint with fine brush strokes | At the first stroke it will be 9 o'clock exactly. | He's a beautiful dog. Can I stroke him? | He stroked her hair affectionately. | She stroked away his tears. |
Idioms: at a (single) stroke | at one stroke : with a single immediate action ; They threatened to cancel the whole project at a stroke. ; They lost half their fortune at a stroke. | put somebody off their stroke : to make somebody make a mistake or hesitate in what they are doing ; My speech was going well until I was put off my stroke by an interruption.
Synonym: medical brain event | | paralytic stroke | movement | blow

Definition: connected with the way in which something is built or organized
Example: Storms have caused structural damage to hundreds of homes.

Definition: a hard fight in which people try to obtain or achieve something, especially something that somebody else does not want them to have | a physical fight between two people or groups of people, especially when one of them is trying to escape, or to get something from the other | something that is difficult for you to do or achieve | to try very hard to do something when it is difficult or when there are a lot of problems | to move somewhere or do something with difficulty | to fight against somebody/something in order to prevent a bad situation or result | to fight somebody or try to get away from them | to compete or argue with somebody/something, especially in order to get something
Example: Marx wrote about the class struggle . | There were no signs of a struggle at the murder scene. | It was a real struggle to be ready on time. | life as a struggling artist (= one who is very poor) | I struggled up the hill with the heavy bags. | Chloe continued to struggle , but her eyes began to close. | I struggled and screamed for help. | rival leaders struggling for power

Definition: a problem or temporary failure when you are on the way to achieving something | an act of falling or almost falling, especially because you hit your foot against something | a mistake that causes you to stop when you are reading, speaking or playing music | to hit your foot against something while you are walking or running and almost fall | to walk or move in an unsteady way | to make a mistake or mistakes and stop while you are speaking, reading to somebody or playing music
Example: There were good reasons for the team's first-round stumble against Los Angeles. | After a slight stumble backwards, he regained his balance. | verbal stumbles | The child stumbled and fell. | Max stumbled out of bed sleepily. | In her nervousness she stumbled over her words.

Definition: to make a person or an animal unconscious for a short time, especially by hitting them on the head | to surprise or shock somebody so much that they cannot think clearly or speak | to impress somebody very much
Example: The fall stunned me for a moment. | Her words stunned me—I had no idea she felt that way. | They were stunned by the view from the summit.
Synonym: knock out | knock out | knock out

Definition: extremely attractive or impressive | that surprises or shocks you very much
Example: You look absolutely stunning! | He suffered a stunning defeat in the election.

Definition: the act of accepting that somebody has defeated you and that you must obey them | the act of giving a document, proposal, etc. to somebody in authority so that they can study or consider it; the document, etc. that you give | a statement that is made to a judge in court
Example: a gesture of submission | When is the final date for the submission of proposals? | All parties will have the opportunity to make submissions relating to this case.

Definition: to give a document, proposal, etc. to somebody in authority so that they can study or consider it | to accept the authority, control or greater strength of somebody/something; to agree to something because of this | to say or suggest something
Example: to submit an application/a claim/a proposal | She refused to submit to threats. | Counsel for the defence submitted that the evidence was inadmissible.

Definition: having less power or authority than somebody else in a group or an organization | less important than something else | a person who has a position with less authority and power than somebody else in an organization | to treat somebody/something as less important than somebody/something else
Example: In some societies women are still subordinate to men. | All other issues are subordinate to this one. | the relationship between subordinates and superiors | Safety considerations were subordinated to commercial interests.
Synonym: lower in rank | lower in rank

Definition: a person who pays money, usually once a year, to receive regular copies of a magazine or newspaper or have access to it online | a person who pays to receive a service | a person who gives money regularly to help the work of an organization such as a charity
Example: subscribers to ‘New Scientist’ | subscribers to cable television | subscribers to Oxfam

Definition: an amount of money that you pay regularly to receive a service, be a member of a club, support a charity or receive regular copies of a newspaper or magazine; the act of paying this money | the act of people paying money for something to be done
Example: an annual subscription | A statue in his memory was erected by public subscription .

Definition: happening or coming after something else
Example: subsequent generations

Definition: afterwards; later; after something else has happened
Example: The original interview notes were subsequently lost.

Definition: money that is paid by a government or an organization to reduce the costs of services or of producing goods so that their prices can be kept low
Example: agricultural subsidies

Definition: large in amount, value or importance | large and solid; strongly built
Example: substantial sums of money | a substantial house

Definition: very much; a lot | mainly; in most details, even if not completely
Example: The costs have increased substantially. | What she says is substantially true.

Definition: a person or thing that you use or have instead of the one you normally use or have | a player who replaces another player in a sports game | to take the place of somebody/something else; to use somebody/something instead of somebody/something else
Example: a meat substitute | He was brought on as (a) substitute after half-time. | Nothing can substitute for the advice your doctor is able to give you.

Definition: an act of using one person or thing in the place of another
Example: Two substitutions were made during the game.

Definition: not very obvious or easy to notice | behaving or organized in a clever way, and using indirect methods, in order to achieve something | good at noticing and understanding things
Example: subtle colours/flavours/smells, etc. | I decided to try a more subtle approach. | The job required a subtle mind.

Tags: Oxford_5000 especially_North_American_English British_English North_American_English informal Geography Buildings
Definition: an area where people live that is outside the centre of a city
Example: a suburb of London

Definition: in or connected with a suburb | boring and ordinary
Example: suburban areas | a suburban lifestyle

Definition: a number of people or things that follow each other in time or order | the regular pattern of one thing following another thing | the act of taking over an official position or title; the right to take over an official position or title, especially to become the king or queen of a country
Example: a succession of visitors | the succession of the seasons | He became chairman in succession to Bernard Allen.

Definition: following immediately one after the other
Example: This was their fourth successive win.

Definition: a person or thing that comes after somebody/something else and takes their/its place
Example: Who's the likely successor to him as party leader?

Definition: an act of sucking | to take liquid, air, etc. into your mouth by using the muscles of your lips | to keep something in your mouth and pull on it with your lips and tongue | to take liquid, air, etc. out of something | to pull somebody/something with great force in a particular direction | used to say that something is very bad
Example: The lamb staggered to its feet and took its first suck of milk. | to suck the juice from an orange | The baby sucked at its mother's breast. | The pump sucks air out through the valve. | The canoe was sucked down into the whirlpool. | Their new album sucks.
Idioms: milk/suck somebody/something dry : to get from somebody/something all the money, help, information, etc. they have, usually giving nothing in return ; By earning millions from racing and giving pennies back, the bookmakers are sucking the sport dry. | suck it and see : used to say that the only way to know if something is suitable is to try it | suck it up : to accept something bad and deal with it well, controlling your emotions | teach your grandmother to suck eggs : to tell or show somebody how to do something that they can already do well, and probably better than you can
Synonym: draw | draw | draw | draw | draw | draw | draw | draw | draw

Definition: to make a claim against a person or an organization in court about something that they have said or done to harm you | to formally ask for something, especially in court
Example: They threatened to sue if the work was not completed. | to sue for divorce
Synonym: bring a civil action against | bring a civil action against

Definition: physical or mental pain | feelings of pain and unhappiness
Example: Death finally brought an end to her suffering. | The hospice aims to ease the sufferings of the dying.

Definition: enough for a particular purpose; as much as you need
Example: Allow sufficient time to get there.

Definition: enough for a particular purpose; as much as you need
Example: The following day she felt sufficiently well to go to work.

Definition: the act of killing yourself deliberately | a course of action that is likely to destroy your career, position in society, etc. | a person who commits suicide
Example: to commit suicide | It would have been political suicide for him to challenge the allegations in court. |

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English computing Holidays Houses_and_homes Music Computers
Definition: a set of rooms, especially in a hotel | a set of matching pieces of furniture | a piece of music made up of three or more related parts, for example pieces from an opera | a set of related computer programs
Example: a hotel/private/honeymoon suite | a bathroom/bedroom suite | Stravinsky’s Firebird Suite | a suite of software development tools

Tags: Oxford_3000 OPAL_written AWL formal Money Maths_and_measurement
Definition: an amount of money | the number you get when you add two or more numbers together | all of something, especially when you think that it is not very much | a simple problem that involves calculating numbers
Example: You will be fined the sum of £200. | The sum of 7 and 12 is 19. | This is the sum of my achievements so far. | to do a sum in your head
Idioms: be greater/more than the sum of its parts : to be better or more effective as a group than you would think just by looking at the individual members of the group ; The team is greater than the sum of its parts. | in sum : used to introduce a short statement of the main points of a discussion, speech, etc. ; In sum, we have no chance of winning the case.

Tags: Oxford_5000 figurative US_English British_English North_American_English Geography Politics
Definition: the highest point of something, especially the top of a mountain | an official meeting or series of meetings between the leaders of two or more governments at which they discuss important matters
Example: We reached the summit at noon. | a summit in Moscow

Definition: extremely good | especially; particularly | a superintendent in the police | a superintendent of a building | extremely; more or better than normal | above; over
Example: a super meal | He's been super understanding. |  |  | super-rich | superstructure

Definition: excellent; of very good quality
Example: a superb player

Definition: better in quality than somebody/something else; greater than somebody/something else | higher in rank, importance or position | showing by your behaviour that you think you are better than others | of very good quality; better than other similar things | a person of higher rank, status or position | used in titles for the head of a religious community
Example: vastly superior | my superior officer | a superior manner | superior apartments | your social superiors | Mother Superior

Definition: to be in charge of somebody/something and make sure that everything is done correctly, safely, etc.
Example: to supervise building work

Definition: the work or activity involved in being in charge of somebody/something and making sure that everything is done correctly, safely, etc. | a meeting between a student and their tutor or supervisor that involves teaching and discussion of the student's work
Example: Very young children should not be left to play without supervision. | I have weekly supervisions.

Definition: a person who supervises somebody/something
Example: I have a meeting with my supervisor about my research topic.

Definition: a thing that is added to something else to improve or complete it | an extra separate section, often in the form of a magazine, that is sold with a newspaper | a book or a section at the end of a book that gives extra information or deals with a special subject | an amount of money that you pay for an extra service or item, especially in addition to the basic cost of a holiday | to add something to something in order to improve it or make it more complete
Example: vitamin/dietary supplements (= vitamins and other foods eaten in addition to what you usually eat) | the Sunday colour supplements | the supplement to the Oxford English Dictionary | There is a £10 supplement for a single room. | a diet supplemented with vitamin pills

Definition: encouraging somebody or giving them help or sympathy
Example: a supportive family

Definition: according to what is generally thought or believed but not known for certain
Example: The novel is supposedly based on a true story.

Definition: to put an end, often by force, to a group or an activity that is believed to threaten authority | to prevent something from being published or made known | to prevent yourself from having or expressing a feeling or an emotion | to prevent something from growing, developing or continuing
Example: The rebellion was brutally suppressed. | The police were accused of suppressing vital evidence. | to suppress a smile | drugs that suppress the appetite

Definition: highest in rank or position | very great or the greatest in degree
Example: the Supreme Commander of the armed forces | to make the supreme sacrifice (= die for what you believe in)

Definition: a sudden increase of a strong feeling | a sudden increase in the amount or number of something | a sudden, strong movement forward or upwards | a sudden increase in the flow of electrical power through a system | to move quickly and with force in a particular direction | to fill somebody with a strong feeling | to suddenly increase in value | to increase suddenly
Example: She felt a sudden surge of anger. | a surge in consumer spending | a tidal surge | An electrical surge damaged the computer's disk drive. | The gates opened and the crowd surged forward. | Relief surged through her. | Share prices surged. |

Definition: a doctor who is trained to perform surgery (= medical operations that involve cutting open a person’s body)
Example: a brain/heart surgeon

Definition: medical treatment of injuries or diseases that involves cutting open a person’s body and often removing or replacing some parts; the branch of medicine connected with this treatment | a place where a doctor, dentist or vet sees patients | the time during which a doctor, dentist or vet is available to see patients | a time when people can meet their Member of Parliament to ask questions and get help
Example: major/minor surgery | a doctor’s/dentist’s/vet's surgery | morning/afternoon/evening surgery | a constituency surgery

Definition: used in or connected with surgery | very accurate
Example: surgical procedures | They launched surgical strikes against enemy targets.
Synonym: = for patients having operations | = for patients having operations

Definition: more than is needed or used | an amount that is extra or more than you need | the amount by which the amount of money received is greater than the amount of money spent
Example: surplus cash | food surpluses | a surplus of £400 million

Definition: an act of admitting that you have been defeated and want to stop fighting | the fact of allowing yourself to be controlled by something | an act of giving something to somebody else even though you do not want to, especially after a battle, etc. | to admit that you have been defeated and want to stop fighting; to allow yourself to be caught, taken prisoner, etc. | to give up something/somebody when you are forced to
Example: They demanded (an) unconditional surrender . | They accused the government of a surrender to business interests. | They insisted on the immediate surrender of all weapons. | The rebel soldiers were forced to surrender. | He agreed to surrender all claims to the property.

Definition: a border or an area around the edge of something, especially one that is decorated | to be all around something/somebody | to move into position all around somebody/something, especially so as to prevent them from escaping; to move somebody/something into position in this way | to be closely connected with something/somebody | to choose to have particular people or things near you all the time
Example: a fireplace with a tiled surround | Tall trees surround the lake. | Police surrounded the building. | publicity surrounding the divorce | I like to surround myself with beautiful things.

Definition: that is near or around something
Example: Oxford and the surrounding area

Definition: the act of carefully watching a person suspected of a crime or a place where a crime may be committed
Example: The police are keeping the suspects under constant surveillance .

Definition: the state of continuing to live or exist, often despite difficulty or danger | something that has continued to exist from an earlier time
Example: the struggle/battle/fight for survival | The ceremony is a survival from pre-Christian times.
Idioms: the survival of the fittest : the principle that only the people or things that are best adapted to their environment will continue to exist

Definition: a person who continues to live, especially despite being nearly killed or experiencing great danger or difficulty
Example: the sole/only survivor of the massacre

Definition: that may be false and that cannot be relied on | that you suspect to be dangerous or illegal | a person who is suspected of a crime or of having done something wrong | to have an idea that something is probably true or likely to happen, especially something bad, but without having definite proof | to have an idea that somebody is guilty of something, without having definite proof | to feel that something is not completely right, legal or honest, without having any proof; to not trust something
Example: Some of the evidence they produced was highly suspect. | a suspect package (= one that may contain drugs, a bomb, etc.) | a murder suspect | As I had suspected all along, he was not a real policeman. | He resigned after being suspected of theft. | I suspected her motives in offering to help.
Idioms: the usual suspects : the people you would usually expect to do something or be included in something ; She thanked all the usual suspects in her acceptance speech: her family, the director, the cast and the crew.

Definition: to hang something from something else | to officially stop something for a time; to prevent something from being active, used, etc. for a time | to officially delay something; to arrange for something to happen later than planned | to officially prevent somebody from doing their job, going to school, etc. for a time, as a punishment or while a complaint against them is investigated | to float in liquid or air without moving
Example: A lamp was suspended from the ceiling. | Production has been suspended while safety checks are carried out. | The introduction of the new system has been suspended until next year. | The police officer was suspended while the complaint was investigated. | Small droplets are held suspended in the atmosphere.

Tags: Oxford_5000 AWL specialist Education Transport_by_car_or_lorry Physics_and_chemistry
Definition: the act of officially removing somebody from their job, school, team, etc. for a period of time, usually as a punishment | the act of delaying something for a period of time, until a decision has been taken | the system of springs, etc. by which a vehicle is supported on its wheels and that makes it more comfortable to ride in when the road surface is not even | a liquid with very small pieces of solid matter floating in it; the state of such a liquid
Example: suspension from school | These events have led to the suspension of talks. | the front/rear suspension | The suspension was passed through a filter to separate out the solid particles.

Definition: a feeling that somebody has done something wrong, illegal or dishonest, even though you have no proof | a feeling or belief that something is true, even though you have no proof | the feeling that you cannot trust somebody/something | a small amount of something
Example: They drove away slowly to avoid arousing suspicion . | I have a horrible suspicion that we've come to the wrong station. | Their offer was greeted with some suspicion. | His mouth quivered in the suspicion of a smile.
Idioms: above/beyond suspicion : too good, honest, etc. to have done something wrong, illegal or dishonest ; Nobody who was near the scene of the crime is above suspicion. ; They thought the head teacher was beyond suspicion. | the finger of blame/suspicion : if the finger of blame/suspicion points or is pointed at somebody, they are suspected of having committed a crime, being responsible for something, etc. ; The inquiry pointed the finger of blame at the driver of the crashed coach. ; The finger of suspicion was pointed at the chicken served for lunch. | under suspicion (of something) : suspected of doing something wrong, illegal or dishonest ; The whole family is currently under suspicion of her murder. ; A number of doctors came under suspicion of unethical behaviour. ; He fell under suspicion of tax evasion.
Synonym: conjecture | conjecture | conjecture | conjecture | conjecture | conjecture | conjecture

Definition: feeling that somebody has done something wrong, illegal or dishonest, without having any proof | making you feel that something is wrong, illegal or dishonest | not willing or able to trust somebody/something
Example: They became suspicious of his behaviour and contacted the police. | Didn't you notice anything suspicious in his behaviour? | I was suspicious of his motives.

Tags: Oxford_5000 OPAL_written AWL formal law Health_problems
Definition: to provide enough of what somebody/something needs in order to live or exist | to make something continue for some time without becoming less | to experience something bad | to provide evidence to support an opinion, a theory, etc. | to support a weight without breaking or falling | to decide that a claim, etc. is true or legally valid
Example: Which planets can sustain life? | She managed to sustain everyone's interest until the end of her speech. | to sustain damage/an injury/a defeat | The evidence is not detailed enough to sustain his argument. | The ice will not sustain your weight. | The court sustained his claim that the contract was illegal.

Definition: involving the use of natural products and energy in a way that does not harm the environment | that can continue or be continued for a long time
Example: sustainable forest management | sustainable economic growth

Definition: a small bird, with long pointed wings and a tail with two points, that migrates (= moves from one part of the world to another according to the season) | an act of swallowing ; an amount of food or drink that is swallowed at one time | to make food, drink, etc. go down your throat into your stomach | to move the muscles of your throat as if you were swallowing something, especially because you are nervous | to take somebody/something in or completely cover them/it so that they/it cannot be seen or exist separately any longer | to use up something completely, especially an amount of money | to accept that something is true; to believe something | to hide your feelings | to accept offensive remarks, criticisms, etc. without complaining or protesting
Example: | She took a swallow of coffee. | Always chew food well before swallowing it. | She swallowed hard and told him the bad news. | I watched her walk down the road until she was swallowed by the darkness. | Most of my salary gets swallowed (up) by the rent and bills. | I found her excuse very hard to swallow . | to swallow your doubts | I was surprised that he just sat there and swallowed all their remarks.
Idioms: one swallow doesn’t make a summer : you must not take too seriously a small sign that something is happening or will happen in the future, because the situation could change | a bitter pill (for somebody) (to swallow) : a fact or an event that is unpleasant and difficult to accept ; The election defeat was a bitter pill for the party to swallow. | like the cat that got/ate/swallowed the canary : very pleased with yourself ; She looked like the cat that’s swallowed the canary. She was almost purring with pleasure.
Synonym: ingest | ingest | mouthful | believe | believe | ingest | believe | believe | smug

Definition: to use rude or offensive language, usually because you are angry | to make a serious promise to do something | to promise that you are telling the truth | to make a public or official promise, especially in court | to make somebody promise not to tell something to anyone
Example: She fell over and swore loudly. | He swore revenge on the man who had killed his father. | She swore (that) she’d never seen him before. | Witnesses were required to swear on the Bible. | Everyone was sworn to secrecy about what had happened.
Idioms: swear blind : to say that something is definitely true ; He swore blind that he’d already paid back the money he owed me. ; He swore blind that you knew all about it. | swear like a trooper : to often use very rude or offensive language

Tags: Oxford_3000 figurative North_American_English Houses_and_homes Appearance Feelings
Definition: an act of cleaning a room, surface, etc. using a broom | a smooth curving movement | a long, often curved, piece of road, river, coast, etc. | the range of an idea, a piece of writing, etc. that considers many different things | a movement over an area, for example in order to search for something or attack something | a person whose job is to clean the inside of chimneys | a sweepstake (= a type of betting in which the winner gets all the money bet by everyone else) | a series of games that a team wins against another team; the fact of winning all the parts of a contest | a time when television companies examine their programmes to find out which ones are the most popular, especially in order to calculate advertising rates | to clean a room, surface, etc. using a broom (= a type of brush on a long handle) | to remove something from a surface using a brush, your hand, etc. | to move or push somebody/something suddenly and with a lot of force | to move suddenly and/or with force over an area or in a particular direction | to move quickly and/or smoothly, especially in a way that impresses or is intended to impress other people | to move something, especially your hand or arm, quickly and smoothly in a particular direction | to suddenly affect somebody strongly | to spread quickly | to move over an area, especially in order to look for something | to move, or move something, over a surface, touching it lightly | to brush, comb , etc. your hair in a particular direction | to form a long, smooth curve | to win all the games in a series of games against another team or all the parts of a contest
Example: Give the room a good sweep. | He indicated the door with a sweep of his arm. | the broad sweep of white cliffs around the bay | Her book covers the long sweep of the country's history. | The rescue helicopter made another sweep over the bay. |  |  | a World Series sweep | the May ratings sweeps | to sweep the floor | She swept the crumbs into the wastebasket. | The little boat was swept out to sea. | Rain swept in through the broken windows. | Without another word she swept out of the room. | He rushed to greet her, sweeping his arms wide. | A wave of tiredness swept over her. | Rumours of his resignation swept through the company. | His eyes swept around the room. | Her dress swept the ground as she walked. | Her hair was swept back from her face. | The hotel gardens sweep down to the beach. | The Blue Jays have a chance to sweep the series.
Idioms: (make) a clean sweep (of something) : to remove all the people or things from an organization that are thought to be unnecessary or need changing ; The new manager made a clean sweep of the department. | (make) a clean sweep (of something) : to win all the prizes or parts of a game or competition; to win an election completely ; China made a clean sweep of the medals in the gymnastics events. ; The opinion poll suggests a clean sweep for the Democrats. | sweep the board : to win all the prizes, etc. in a competition | sweep somebody off their feet : to make somebody fall suddenly and deeply in love with you ; She’s waiting for some hero to come and sweep her off her feet. | sweep (somebody) to power : to win an election by a large number of votes; to make somebody win an election with a large number of votes | sweep to victory : to win a contest easily ; Macron swept to victory in 2017. | sweep something under the carpet : to try to stop people from finding out about something wrong, illegal, embarrassing, etc. that has happened or that you have done ; An earlier report, implicating the government, had been conveniently swept under the carpet.

Tags: Oxford_5000 informal North_American_English old-fashioned British_English Success
Definition: a swinging movement or rhythm | a change from one opinion or situation to another; the amount by which something changes | a seat for swinging on, hung from above on ropes or chains | the swinging movement you make with your arms and body when you hit the ball in the game of golf | a type of jazz with a smooth rhythm, played especially by big dance bands in the 1930s | a quick journey, especially one made by a politician, in which somebody visits several different places in a short time | to move backwards or forwards or from side to side while hanging from a fixed point; to make something do this | to move from one place to another by holding something that is fixed and pulling yourself along, up, etc. | to move or make something move with a wide curved movement | to change or make somebody/something change from one opinion, mood, etc. to another | to turn or change direction suddenly; to make something do this | to try to hit somebody/something | to succeed in getting or achieving something, sometimes in a slightly dishonest way | to have a strong rhythm | if a party, etc. is swinging , there are a lot of people there having a good time
Example: He took a wild swing at the ball. | Voting showed a 10 per cent swing to Labour. | The kids were playing on the swings. | I need to work on my swing. |  | a three-day campaign swing through California | His arms swung as he walked. | The gunshot sent monkeys swinging away through the trees. | A line of cars swung out of the palace gates. | The state has swung from Republican to Democrat. | She swung back to face him. | She swung at me with the iron bar. | We're trying to swing it so that we can travel on the same flight. |  |
Idioms: get in/into the swing (of something) : to get used to an activity or a situation and become fully involved in it ; I’ve only been here a week so I haven’t got into the swing of things yet. | go with a swing : to be lively and fun ; She made the whole party go with a swing. | go with a swing : to have a strong rhythm | in full swing : having reached a very lively level ; When we arrived the party was already in full swing. | swings and roundabouts : used to say that there are advantages and disadvantages whatever decision you make ; If you earn more, you pay more in tax, so it's all swings and roundabouts. ; What you gain on the swings you may lose on the roundabouts. | no room to swing a cat : when somebody says there’s no room to swing a cat , they mean that a room is very small and that there is not enough space | swing the balance : to affect the result of something in one way rather than another ; In an interview, smart presentation can swing the balance in your favour. ; New evidence swung the balance against the prosecution. | swing both ways : to be bisexual (= sexually attracted to both men and women) | swing for the fences : to really try to achieve something great, even when it is not reasonable to expect to be so successful ; entrepreneurs who think big and swing for the fences ; Investors need to manage risk and not swing for the fences. | swing into action : to start doing something quickly and with a lot of energy ; The ambulance crew swung into action to resuscitate the patient. ; The rescue operation swung into action immediately. ; The emergency services swung into action as soon as the disaster was reported. | swing the lead : to pretend to be ill when in fact you are not, especially to avoid work ; I don't think there's anything wrong with her—she's just swinging the lead.

Tags: Oxford_5000 saying old-fashioned_or_literary literary History War_and_conflict
Definition: a weapon with a long metal blade (= sharp cutting part) and a handle
Example: to draw/sheathe a sword (= to take it out of/put it into its cover)
Idioms: be a double-edged/two-edged sword/weapon : to be something that has both advantages and disadvantages ; Fame is a double-edged sword. | cross swords (with somebody) : to fight or argue with somebody | the pen is mightier than the sword : people who write books, poems, etc. have a greater effect on history and human affairs than soldiers and wars | put somebody to the sword : to kill somebody with a sword ; All the men were put to the sword. | a/the sword of Damocles : a bad or unpleasant thing that might happen to you at any time and that makes you feel worried or frightened | turn swords into ploughshares : to stop fighting and return to peaceful activities
Synonym: blade | blade | blade | blade | blade | blade | blade

Definition: containing symbols, or being used as a symbol
Example: He shook his fist in a symbolic gesture of defiance.

Definition: kind to somebody who is hurt or sad; showing that you understand and care about their problems | showing that you approve of somebody/something or that you share their views and are willing to support them | easy to like
Example: a sympathetic listener | He was speaking to a highly sympathetic audience. | a sympathetic character in a novel

Definition: the feeling of being sorry for somebody; showing that you understand and care about somebody’s problems | the act of showing support for or approval of an idea, a cause, an organization, etc. | friendship and understanding between people who have similar opinions or interests
Example: to express/feel sympathy for somebody | The seamen went on strike in sympathy with (= to show their support for) the dockers. | There was no personal sympathy between them.
Idioms: in sympathy with something : happening because something else has happened ; Share prices slipped in sympathy with the German market. | out of sympathy with somebody/something : not agreeing with or not wanting to support somebody/something

Definition: a set of physical conditions that show you have a particular disease or medical problem | a set of opinions or a way of behaving that is typical of a particular type of person, attitude or social problem
Example: PMS or premenstrual syndrome | With teenagers, be prepared for the ‘Me, me, me!’ syndrome (= they think of themselves first) .

Definition: the act of combining separate ideas, beliefs, styles, etc.; a mixture or combination of ideas, beliefs, styles, etc. | the natural chemical production of a substance in animals and plants | the artificial production of a substance that is present naturally in animals and plants | the production of sounds, music or speech by electronic means
Example: the synthesis of art with everyday life | protein synthesis | the synthesis of penicillin |

Definition: done according to a system or plan, in a complete, efficient or determined way
Example: a systematic approach to solving the problem

Definition: an act of trying to take the ball from an opponent in football (soccer), hockey, etc.; an act of knocking somebody to the ground, for example in rugby or American football | a player whose job is to stop opponents by knocking them to the ground | the equipment used to do a particular sport or activity, especially fishing | a man’s sexual organs | to make a determined effort to deal with a difficult problem or situation | to speak to somebody about a problem or difficult situation | to try and take the ball from an opponent | to make an opponent fall to the ground in order to stop them running | to deal with somebody who is violent or threatening you
Example: He was booked for a late tackle on Torres. |  | He owns a gun and tackle shop in the village. |  | The government is determined to tackle inflation. | I tackled him about the money he owed me. | He was tackled just outside the penalty area. | He can run fast and tackle hard. | He tackled a masked intruder at his home.
Synonym: equipment | equipment | | | seize and throw down

Definition: the particular method you use to achieve something | the art of moving soldiers and military equipment around during a battle or war in order to use them in the most effective way
Example: They tried all kinds of tactics to get us to go. |
Synonym: course of action | course of action

Definition: connected with the particular method you use to achieve something | carefully planned in order to achieve a particular aim | used or having an effect over short distances or for a short time | connected with military tactics
Example: tactical planning | a tactical decision | tactical weapons/missiles | He was given tactical command of the operation.

Definition: a small piece of paper, cloth, plastic, etc. attached to something to identify it or give information about it | an electronic device that can be attached to a person, animal or object so that police, researchers, etc. know where the person, etc. is | a name or phrase that is used to describe a person or thing in some way | a word or phrase that is added to a sentence for emphasis, for example I do in Yes, I do | a set of letters or symbols that are put before and after a piece of text or data in order to identify it or show that it is to be treated in a particular way | a short quotation or saying in a foreign language | a children’s game in which one child runs after the others and tries to touch one of them | a symbol or name used by a graffiti writer and painted in a public place | to fasten a tag onto something/somebody | to attach an electronic device to a person, animal or object so that the police, researchers, etc. know where the person, etc. is | to give somebody/something a name that describes what they are or do | to add a set of letters or symbols to a piece of text or data in order to identify it or to show that it is to be treated in a particular way | to add a link to various users' profiles from a photo on a social media website | to leave a name or mark on a piece of graffiti to show who made it
Example: He put name tags on all his shirts. | The police use electronic tags to monitor the whereabouts of young offenders on probation. | They are finally ready to drop the tag ‘the new Beatles’. |  |  | the Latin tag ‘Si vis pacem, para bellum.’ (= if you want peace, prepare for war) |  |  | Each animal was tagged with a number for identification. | Persistent young offenders will be electronically tagged in a scheme to cut youth crime. | The country no longer wanted to be tagged as a Third World nation. |  | If you upload a photo, people can tag the people in it. | Graffiti artists started tagging and painting entire subway trains.
Synonym: attach a tag to | add | attach a tag to | add | attach a tag to | label | attach a tag to | label

Definition: a story created using the imagination, especially one that is full of action and adventure | an exciting spoken description of an event, which may not be completely true
Example: a coming-of-age/morality tale | I love listening to his tales of life at sea.

Definition: a mark, an object or a sign that shows that somebody/something existed or was present | a very small amount of something | a line or pattern on paper or a screen that shows information that is found by a machine | a search to find out information about the identity of somebody/something, especially what number a phone call was made from | one of the two long pieces of leather that fasten a carriage or cart to the horse that pulls it | to find or discover somebody/something by looking carefully for them/it | to find the origin or cause of something | to describe a process or the development of something | to draw a line or lines on a surface | to follow the shape or outline of something | to copy a map, drawing, etc. by drawing on tracing paper (= paper that you can see through) placed over it
Example: It's exciting to discover traces of earlier civilizations. | The post-mortem revealed traces of poison in his stomach. | The trace showed a normal heart rhythm. | The police ran a trace on the call. |  | We finally traced him to an address in Chicago. | She could trace her family tree back to the 16th century. | Her book traces the town's history from Saxon times to the present day. | She traced a line in the sand. | He traced the route on the map. |

Synonym: find | follow the trail of | find | find | | | track down | = used special electronic equipment to find out who made the phone call

Definition: a name, symbol or design that a company uses for its products and that cannot be used by anyone else | a special way of behaving or dressing that is typical of somebody and that makes them easily recognized | to register something as a trademark

Definition: the activity of buying and selling things

Definition: a long line or series of marks that is left by somebody/something | a track, sign or smell that is left behind and that can be followed, especially in hunting | a path through the countryside | a route that is followed for a particular purpose | to pull something behind somebody/something, usually along the ground; to be pulled along in this way | to walk slowly because you are tired or bored, especially behind somebody else; to go from one place to another without enthusiasm | to be losing a game or other contest | to follow somebody/something by looking for signs that show you where they have been | to grow or hang downwards over something or along the ground; to move downwards over something | to advertise a film, TV programme, etc. in advance

Definition: the act of moving somebody/something from one place, group or job to another; an occasion when this happens | an act of changing to a different place, vehicle or route when you are travelling | a ticket that allows a passenger to continue their journey on another bus or train | the act of moving a sports player from one club or team to another | a picture or design that can be removed from a piece of paper and stuck onto a surface, for example by being pressed or heated | the process of using behaviour that has already been learnt in one situation in a new situation | to move from one place to another; to move something/somebody from one place to another | to move from one job, school, situation, etc. to another; to arrange for somebody to move | to change to a different vehicle during a journey; to arrange for somebody to change to a different vehicle during a journey | to copy information, music, an idea, etc. from one method of recording or presenting it to another; to be recorded or presented in a different way | if you transfer a feeling, a disease, or power, etc. from one person to another, the second person has it, often instead of the first | to officially arrange for something to belong to somebody else or for somebody else to control something | to move, or to move somebody, to a different sports team, especially a professional football (soccer) team | to send a phone call that you have received to another phone number

Definition: the quality of something, such as a situation or an argument, that makes it easy to understand | the quality of something, such as glass, that allows you to see through it | a picture printed on a piece of film, usually in a frame, that can be shown on a screen by shining light through the film | the quality in something, such as an excuse or a lie, that allows somebody to see the truth easily

Definition: a piece of equipment for catching animals | a clever plan designed to trick somebody, either by capturing them or by making them do or say something that they did not mean to do or say | an unpleasant situation from which it is hard to escape | a light carriage with two wheels, pulled by a horse | mouth | a cage from which a greyhound (= a type of dog) is let out at the start of a race | a small area filled with sand on a golf course | to put somebody in a dangerous place that they cannot get out of | to keep somebody in a bad situation that they want to get out of but cannot | to have part of your body, your clothing, etc. held in a place so tightly that you cannot remove it and it may be injured or damaged | to force somebody/something into a place or situation that they cannot escape from, especially in order to catch them | to catch an animal in a trap | to catch or keep something in a place and prevent it from escaping, especially so that you can use it | to trick somebody into something

(= a rude way of telling somebody to be quiet) |  |  | Help! I'm trapped! | He was trapped in an unhappy marriage. | I trapped my coat in the car door. | The escaped prisoners were eventually trapped in an underground garage and recaptured. | Raccoons used to be trapped for their fur. | Solar panels trap energy from the sun. | He felt he had been trapped into accepting the terms of the contract.

Definition: the belief that somebody/something is good, sincere, honest, etc. and will not try to harm or trick you | the belief that something is true or correct or that you can rely on it | an arrangement by which an organization or a group of people has legal control of money or property that has been given to somebody, usually until that person reaches a particular age; an amount of money or property that is controlled in this way | an organization or a group of people that invests money that is given or lent to it and uses the profits to help a charity | a group of companies that work together illegally to reduce competition, control prices, etc. | to have confidence in somebody; to believe that somebody is good, sincere, honest, etc. | to believe that something is true or correct or that you can rely on it | to hope and expect that something is true

Idioms: be in/out of tune : to be/not be singing or playing the correct musical notes to sound pleasant ; None of them could sing in tune. ; The piano is out of tune. | be in/out of tune (with somebody/something) : to be/not be in agreement with somebody/something; to have/not have the same opinions, feelings, interests, etc. as somebody/something ; These proposals are perfectly in tune with our own thoughts on the subject. ; The President is out of tune with public opinion. | call the shots/tune : to be the person who controls a situation | change your tune : to express a different opinion or behave in a different way when your situation changes ; Wait until it happens to him—he'll soon change his tune. | dance to somebody’s tune : to do whatever somebody tells you to | he who pays the piper calls the tune : the person who provides the money for something can also control how it is spent | march to (the beat of) a different drummer/drum | march to a different beat/tune : to behave in a different way from other people; to have different attitudes or ideas ; She was a gifted and original artist who marched to a different drummer. | sing a different tune : to change your opinion about somebody/something or your attitude towards somebody/something | to the tune of something : used to emphasize how much money something has cost ; The hotel has been refurbished to the tune of a million dollars.

Definition: a passage built underground, for example to allow a road or railway to go through a hill, under a river, etc. | an underground passage made by an animal or natural processes | to dig a tunnel under or through the ground
Example: a railway/railroad tunnel | The badger sett had twelve entrances to what must have been a labyrinth of tunnels. | The engineers had to tunnel through solid rock.
Idioms: light at the end of the tunnel : something that shows you are nearly at the end of a long and difficult time or situation ; For the first time since the start of his treatment, we can now see light at the end of the tunnel.

Definition: the number of people who attend a particular event | the number of people who vote in a particular election

Definition: the total amount of goods or services sold by a company during a particular period of time | the rate at which employees leave a company and are replaced by other people | the rate at which goods are sold in a shop and replaced by others | a small pie in the shape of a triangle or half a circle, filled with fruit or jam
Example: an annual turnover of $75 million | a high turnover of staff | a fast turnover of stock |

Definition: the action of turning something with your hand, or of turning a part of your body | an unexpected change or development in a story or situation | a sharp bend in a road or river | a thing that has been twisted into a particular shape | a fast dance that was popular in the 1960s, in which you twist from side to side | to bend or turn something into a particular shape | to bend or turn something into a shape or position that is not normal or natural; to be bent or turned in this way | to turn part of your body around while the rest stays still | to turn your body with quick, sharp movements and change direction often | to turn something around in a circle with your hand | to bend and change direction often | to injure part of your body, especially your ankle, wrist or knee, bending it in an uncomfortable way | to wind something around or through an object | to move or grow by winding around something | to deliberately change the meaning of what somebody has said, or to present facts in a particular way, in order to benefit yourself or harm somebody else | to turn or wind strings, etc. together to make something longer or thicker
Example: She gave the lid another twist and it came off. | the twists and turns of his political career | The car followed the twists and turns of the mountain road. | mineral water with a twist of lemon |  | Twist the wire to form a circle. | He grabbed me and twisted my arm behind my back. | He twisted his head around to look at her. | I twisted and turned to avoid being caught. | Twist the knob to the left to open the door. | The road twists and turns along the coast. | She fell and twisted her ankle. | She twisted a scarf around her head. | A snake was twisting around his arm. | You always twist everything I say. | They had twisted the sheets into a rope and escaped by climbing down it.
Idioms: get your knickers in a twist : to become angry, confused or upset | round the bend/twist : crazy | turn/twist the knife (in the wound) : to say or do something unkind deliberately; to make somebody who is unhappy feel even more unhappy ; Marcia laughed as she twisted the knife still deeper. ; Just to twist the knife, the filmmakers have provided a surprise ending. | twist somebody’s arm : to persuade or force somebody to do something | twist/wrap/wind somebody around your little finger : to persuade somebody to do anything that you want ; She has always been able to twist her parents around her little finger.

Definition: happening at the end of a long process | most extreme; best, worst, greatest, most important, etc. | from which something originally comes | the best, most advanced, greatest, etc. of its kind

Definition: in the end; finally | at the most basic and important level

Definition: so bad that you think it should not be allowed

Definition: the state of being uncertain | something that you cannot be sure about; a situation that makes you not be or feel certain
Example: There is considerable uncertainty about the company's future. | life’s uncertainties

Definition: in a state like sleep because of an injury or illness, and not able to use your senses | existing or happening without you realizing or being aware; not deliberate | not aware of somebody/something; not noticing something; not conscious | the part of a person’s mind with thoughts, feelings, etc. that they are not aware of and cannot control but that can sometimes be understood by studying their behaviour or dreams
Example: They found him lying unconscious on the floor. | unconscious desires | She is unconscious of the effect she has on people. |

Definition: to experience something, especially a change or something unpleasant

Definition: a university or college student who is studying for their first degree

Definition: to be the basis or cause of something

Definition: to make yourself responsible for something and start doing it | to agree or promise that you will do something

Definition: used to emphasize that something exists or is definitely true

Definition: if something is unexpected , it surprises you because you were not expecting it

Definition: to spread open or flat something that has previously been folded; to become open and flat | to be gradually made known; to gradually make something known to other people

Definition: having bad luck; caused by bad luck | if you say that a situation is unfortunate , you wish that it had not happened or that it had been different | embarrassing and/or offensive | a person who does not have much luck, money, etc.

Definition: to join people, things, parts of a country, etc. together so that they form a single unit

Definition: being the only one of its kind | very special or unusual | belonging to or connected with one particular person, place or thing

Example: Nationalist parties united to oppose the government's plans. | A special bond unites our two countries. |

Definition: the state of being in agreement and working together; the state of being joined together to form one unit | the quality of looking or being complete in a natural and attractive way | any of the principles of classical or neoclassical theatre that limit the action of a play to a single story, day and place | a single thing that may consist of a number of different parts | the number one

Definition: done by or involving all the people in the world or in a particular group | true or right at all times and in all places

Example: Could there be intelligent life elsewhere in the universe? | The idea of a parallel universe is hard to grasp. | the moral universe

Definition: not known or identified | not famous or well known | never happening or existing | places or things that are not known about | a person who is not well known | a fact or an influence that is not known | a quantity that does not have a known value
Example: A previously unknown group claimed responsibility for the bombing. | She was then still relatively unknown . | The disease is as yet unknown in Europe (= there have been no cases there) . | a journey into the unknown | A young unknown played the leading role. | There are so many unknowns in the proposal. | X and Y in the equation are both unknowns.
Idioms: an unknown quantity : a person or thing whose qualities or abilities are not yet known | unknown to somebody : without the person mentioned being aware of it ; Unknown to me, he had already signed the agreement.

Definition: that has never happened, been done or been known before

Definition: to remove a cover or curtain from a painting, statue, etc. so that it can be seen in public for the first time | to show or introduce a new plan, product, etc. to the public for the first time
