Definition: to hit a person or an animal hard with a whip , as a punishment or to make them go faster or work harder | to move, or make something move, quickly and suddenly or violently in a particular direction | to remove or pull something quickly and suddenly | to mix cream, etc. very quickly until it becomes stiff | to defeat somebody very easily in a game | to steal something | to tell the MPs belonging to a political party that they must vote a particular way on an issue | a long, thin piece of rope or leather, attached to a handle, used for hitting animals or people, to make them move, or move faster, or as a punishment | an official in a political party who is responsible for making sure that the party's MPs attend and vote in important debates in a parliament | a written instruction telling the MPs of a political party how to vote on a particular issue | the fact of being an MP belonging to a particular party, with the duties and rights associated with that | a sweet dish made from cream, eggs, sugar and fruit mixed together

Definition: the quality of being happy and ready to do something
Example: Success in studying depends on a willingness to learn.

Definition: an act of cleaning something using a cloth | a special piece of thin cloth or soft paper that has been treated with a liquid and that you use to clean away dirt and bacteria | to rub something against a surface, in order to remove dirt or liquid from it; to rub a surface with a cloth, etc. in order to clean it | to remove dirt, liquid, etc. from something by using a cloth, your hand, etc. | to remove information, sound, images, etc. from a computer, video, etc. | to deliberately forget an experience because it was unpleasant or embarrassing
Example: Can you give the table a quick wipe? | Remember to take nappies and baby wipes . | Please wipe your feet on the mat. | He wiped the sweat from his forehead. | You must have wiped off that programme I recorded. | I tried to wipe the whole episode from my mind.
Idioms: wipe/mop the floor with somebody : to defeat somebody completely in an argument or a competition | wipe the grin/smile off your face : to stop smiling, especially because somebody else is annoyed and does not think the situation is funny ; Wipe that grin off your face and get back to work. ; Wipe that stupid smile off your face. | wipe somebody/something off the face of the earth | wipe something off the map : to destroy or remove somebody/something completely | wipe the slate clean : to agree to forget about past mistakes or arguments and start again with a relationship

Definition: metal in the form of thin thread ; a piece of this | a piece of wire that is used to carry an electric current or signal | an electronic listening device that can be hidden on a person | a wire fence | a telegram (= a message sent by telegraph and then printed and given to somebody) | to connect a building, piece of equipment, etc. to an electricity supply using wires | to connect somebody/something to a piece of equipment | to put a special device somewhere in order to listen secretly to other people’s conversations | to send somebody a message by telegram | to send money from one bank to another using an electronic system | to join things together using wire
Example: a coil of copper wire | electrical wires | an undercover police informer who was wearing a wire | Three prisoners escaped by crawling under the wire. | We sent a wire asking him to join us. | Make sure the plug is wired up correctly. | In the test, volunteers were wired up to brain monitors. | The room had been wired for sound. | He wired the news to us. | The bank wired the money to her. | The components have to be wired together in a certain way.
Idioms: get your wires crossed : to become confused about what somebody has said to you so that you think they meant something else ; We seem to have got our wires crossed. I thought you were coming on Tuesday. | go, come, etc. (right) down to the wire : if you say that a situation goes down to the wire , you mean that the result will not be decided or known until the very end | a live wire : a person who is lively and full of energy | pull wires : to use your influence in order to get an advantage for somebody | under the wire : at the last possible opportunity; just in time ; The files arrived just under the wire and we made the deadline. ; We got in under the wire.

Definition: the ability to make sensible decisions and give good advice because of the experience and knowledge that you have | how sensible something is | the knowledge that a society or culture has gained over a long period of time
Example: She was known to be a woman of great wisdom. | I question the wisdom of giving a child so much money. | the collective wisdom of our people
Idioms: conventional/received/popular wisdom : the view or belief that most people hold ; Conventional wisdom has it that riots only ever happen in cities. | in his/her/its, etc. (infinite) wisdom : used when you are saying that you do not understand why somebody has done something ; The government in its wisdom has decided to support the ban. | a pearl of wisdom : a wise remark ; Thank you for those pearls of wisdom.
Synonym: good judgment | good judgment | philosophy | philosophy | good judgment | good judgment

Definition: able to make sensible decisions and give good advice because of the experience and knowledge that you have | sensible; based on good judgement
Example: a wise man | a wise decision/move/choice/investment
Idioms: be none the wiser | not be any the wiser : to not understand something, even after it has been explained to you ; I've read the instructions, but I'm still none the wiser. | be none the wiser | not be any the wiser : to not know or find out about something bad that somebody has done ; If you put the money back, no one will be any the wiser. | be wise after the event : to understand something, or realize what you should have done, only after something has happened ; Anyone can be wise after the event. | be/get wise to somebody/something : to become aware that somebody is being dishonest ; He thought he could fool me but I got wise to him. | crack wise : to make jokes ; This entertainer can crack wise with the best of them. | put somebody wise (to something) : to inform somebody about something

Tags: Oxford_5000 informal old-fashioned formal Personal_qualities Feelings
Definition: the ability to say or write things that are both clever and humorous | a person who has the ability to say or write things that are both clever and humorous | your ability to think quickly and clearly and to make good decisions | having the type of intelligence mentioned | the intelligence or good sense to know what is the right thing to do
Example: to have a quick/sharp/dry/ready wit | a well-known wit and raconteur | He needed all his wits to find his way out. | a quick-witted group of students | At least you had the wit to ask for help.
Idioms: be at your wits’ end : to be so worried by a problem that you do not know what to do next ; She was at her wits’ end wondering how she’d manage it all in the time. ; I'm at my wits' end trying to cope with his moods. | be frightened/scared/terrified out of your wits : to be very frightened; to frighten somebody very much ; I was scared out of my wits! ; The latest news has scared the wits out of investors. | have/keep your wits about you : to be aware of what is happening around you and ready to think and act quickly ; They do tough interviews so you'll need to have your wits about you. | live by your wits : to earn money by clever or sometimes dishonest means | to wit : you use to wit when you are about to be more exact about something that you have just referred to ; Pilot error, to wit failure to follow procedures, was the cause of the accident.
Synonym: intelligence | intelligence | intelligence | intelligence | intelligence | intelligence | intelligence | intelligence | intelligence | intelligence

Tags: Oxford_5000 informal British_English North_American_English US_English formal especially_North_American_English especially_British_English North_American_English_usually Money
Definition: to take money out of a bank account | to move back or away from a place or situation; to make somebody/something do this | to stop taking part in an activity or being a member of an organization; to stop somebody/something from doing these things | to stop giving or offering something to somebody | to say that you no longer believe that something you previously said is true | to become quieter and spend less time with other people
Example: With this account, you can withdraw up to £300 a day. | Government troops were forced to withdraw. | In 2016 Britain voted to withdraw from the EU. | Workers have threatened to withdraw their labour (= go on strike) . | The newspaper withdrew the allegations the next day. | She's beginning to withdraw into herself.
Synonym: take | take | retire | reverse | reverse | retract

Tags: Oxford_5000 informal British_English North_American_English US_English formal especially_North_American_English especially_British_English North_American_English_usually psychology Money Social_issues Health_problems
Definition: the act of taking an amount of money out of your bank account | the act of moving or taking something away or back | the act of no longer taking part in something or being a member of an organization | the period of time when somebody is getting used to not taking a drug that they have become addicted to, and the unpleasant effects of doing this | the act of saying that you no longer believe that something you have previously said is true | the behaviour of somebody who wants to be alone and does not want to communicate with other people
Example: You can make withdrawals of up to $250 a day. | the withdrawal of support | his withdrawal from the election | I got withdrawal symptoms after giving up smoking. | The newspaper published a withdrawal the next day. | She is showing signs of withdrawal and depression.

Definition: a person who sees something happen and is able to describe it to other people | a person who gives evidence in court | a person who is present when an official document is signed and who also signs it to prove that they saw this happen | evidence of a person’s strong religious beliefs, that they show by what they say and do in public | to see something happen (typically a crime or an accident) | to be the place, period, organization, etc. in which particular events take place | to be present when an official document is signed and sign it yourself to prove that you saw this happen | to be a sign or proof of something | used when giving an example that proves something you have just said | to speak to people about your strong religious beliefs
Example: He failed to interview a key witness. | a defence/prosecution witness | He was one of the witnesses at our wedding. |  | to witness an accident/a murder/an attack | Recent years have witnessed a growing social mobility. | to witness a signature | The huge attendance figures for the exhibition witness to a healthy interest in modern art. | Authentic Italian cooking is very healthy—witness the low incidence of heart disease in Italy. |
Idioms: bear/give witness (to something) : to provide evidence of the truth of something ; The crowd of mourners at his funeral bore witness to the great affection in which he was held. ; The ancient temples bear silent witness to the passing dynasties. | be (a) witness to something : to see something take place ; He has been witness to a terrible murder. ; a witness to murder | be (a) witness to something : to show that something is true; to provide evidence for something ; His good health is a witness to the success of the treatment.

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English especially_British_English Australian_English New_Zealand_English South_African_English both_British_English informal North_American_English Working_life Business
Definition: all the people who work for a particular company, organization, etc. | all the people in a country or an area who are available for work
Example: The factory has a 1 000-strong workforce. | A quarter of the local workforce is/are unemployed.

Definition: a period of physical exercise that you do to keep fit
Example: She does a 20-minute workout every morning.

Definition: the office, factory, etc. where people work
Example: the introduction of new technology into the workplace

Tags: Oxford_5000 British_English especially_British_English North_American_English both_British_English informal both_especially_North_American_English especially_North_American_English both_North_American_English Business
Definition: a period of discussion and practical work on a particular subject, in which a group of people share their knowledge and experience | a room or building in which things are made or repaired using tools or machines
Example: There will be the opportunity for practical theatre work in drama workshops. | The craftsmen worked in a freezing cold workshop.

Tags: Oxford_5000 computing informal disapproving saying Insects,_worms,_etc. Computers
Definition: a long, thin creature with a soft body and no bones or legs | long, thin creatures that live inside the bodies of humans or animals and can cause illness | the young form of an insect when it looks like a short worm | a computer program that is a type of virus and that spreads across a network by copying itself | a person you do not like or respect, especially because they have a weak character and do not behave well towards other people | to use a twisting and turning movement, especially to move through a narrow or crowded place | to give an animal medicine that makes worms pass out of its body in the faeces
Example: birds looking for worms | The dog has worms. | This apple is full of worms. |  |  | She wormed her way through the crowd to the reception desk. |
Idioms: a can of worms : if you open up a can of worms , you start doing something that will cause a lot of problems and be very difficult | the early bird catches the worm : the person who takes the opportunity to do something before other people will have an advantage over them | the worm will turn : a person who is normally quiet and does not complain will protest when the situation becomes too hard to bear
Synonym: small legless elongated animal | small legless elongated animal | small legless elongated animal | creep | creep | creep | creep | small legless elongated animal

Definition: the practice of showing respect for God or a god, by saying prayers, singing with others, etc.; a ceremony for this | a strong feeling of love and respect for somebody/something | a polite way of addressing or referring to a magistrate or mayor
Example: an act/a place of worship | What she feels for him is akin to worship. |

Definition: important, pleasant, interesting, etc.; worth spending time, money or effort on
Example: It was in aid of a worthwhile cause (= a charity, etc.) .

Definition: having the qualities that deserve somebody/something | having qualities that deserve your respect or attention | having good qualities but not very interesting or exciting | typical of what a particular person or thing might do, give, etc. | deserving, or suitable for, the thing mentioned | an important person
Example: Very few of his ideas are worthy of further attention. | The money we raise will be going to a very worthy cause . | her worthy but dull husband | He gave a speech that was worthy of Martin Luther King. | trustworthy | a meeting attended by local worthies

Definition: a damaged area of the body , such as a cut or hole in the skin or flesh made by a weapon : | a problem or great unhappiness : | to damage an area of the body , especially by making a cut or hole in the skin : | to make someone feel upset : | past simple and past participle of wind | past simple and past participle of wind | a hurt or injury to the body , such as a cut or tear in the skin or flesh : | to hurt the feelings of someone; upset : | to hurt or injure the body , as with a cut or tear in the skin or flesh :
Example: a gunshot wound | She refuses to talk about the incident , saying it would only reopen old wounds (= make her remember unhappy past experiences ) . | Flying glass wounded her in the face and neck . | He was deeply wounded by her fierce criticism . |  |  | a puncture wound | He totally ignored her, and she was deeply wounded. | Several people were wounded by falling rocks .

Definition: to cover something completely in paper or other material, for example when you are giving it as a present | to cover something/somebody in material, for example in order to protect it/them | to put something around something/somebody | to cause a word or text to be carried over to a new line as the end of the previous line is reached by the writer; to be carried over in this way
Example: He spent the evening wrapping up the Christmas presents. | Wrap the meat in foil before you cook it. | A scarf was wrapped around his neck. | How can I wrap the text around?
Idioms: be wrapped up in somebody/something : to be so involved with somebody/something that you do not pay enough attention to other people or things ; They are completely wrapped up in their children. ; She was so wrapped up in her work that she didn’t realize how late it was. | wrap/twist/wind somebody around your little finger : to persuade somebody to do anything that you want ; She has always been able to wrap her parents around her little finger.

Definition: the joint between the hand and the arm
Example: She's broken her wrist.
Idioms: a slap on the wrist : a warning or mild punishment ; We're hoping that she gets off with a slap on the wrist from the judge.

Definition: to shout loudly, for example because you are angry, excited, frightened or in pain | a loud shout of pain, excitement, etc. | an organized shout of support for a team at a sports event
Example: He yelled at the other driver. | to let out/give a yell |

Definition: the total amount of crops, profits, etc. that are produced | to produce or provide something, for example a profit, result or crop | to stop resisting something/somebody; to agree to do something that you do not want to do | to allow somebody to win, have or take control of something that has been yours until now | to move, bend or break because of pressure | to allow vehicles on a bigger road to go first
Example: a high crop yield | Higher-rate deposit accounts yield good returns. | After a long siege, the town was forced to yield. | He refused to yield up his gun. | Despite our attempts to break it, the lock would not yield. | Yield to oncoming traffic.

Definition: a young person or a child
Example: The camp is for youngsters aged 8 to 14.

Definition: an area or a region with a particular feature or use | one of the areas that a larger area is divided into for the purpose of organization | an area or a part of an object, especially one that is different from everything that is around it | one of the parts that the earth’s surface is divided into by imaginary lines that are parallel to the equator
Example: a war/combat/demilitarized/exclusion zone | postal charges to countries in zone 2 | When the needle enters the red zone the engine is too hot. | the northern/southern temperate zone
Idioms: in the zone : in a state in which you feel confident and are performing at your best ; When I'm in the zone, writing is the most satisfying thing in the world. | a/the twilight world (of something) | the twilight zone : a state in which things are strange, mysterious or secret; a state that exists on the dividing line between two things ; the twilight world of the occult ; The dissidents lived in a twilight world of hushed voices and secret meetings. ; the twilight zone between living and merely existing ; They lived in the twilight zone on the fringes of society.
